topics.title: Study on Freeze Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study on Freeze Drying # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Freeze Drying (Lyophilization)** is a low-temperature dehydration process in which water is removed from a frozen product by **sublimation** — the direct phase transition of water from the **solid state (ice)** to the **vapor state (water vapor)** — under high vacuum conditions, bypassing the intermediate liquid phase entirely. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Freeze drying is a unit operation classified under **Separation Techniques** in Pharmaceutical/Food Technology, governed by the **Phase Equilibrium Principles** of solid–vapor systems. The driving force is the **vapor pressure difference** between the ice front and the condenser surface. Mathematically, the process is described by the **Sublimation Equation**: $$\dot{m}_s = \frac{(P_{ice} - P_{chamber}) \cdot A}{R_s}$$ where: - $\dot{m}_s$ = rate of sublimation (kg/s) - $P_{ice}$ = vapor pressure at ice front (Pa) - $P_{chamber}$ = chamber pressure (Pa) - $A$ = surface area of sublimation (m²) - $R_s$ = resistance to mass transfer (Pa·s/kg) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Mountain Clothesline** > Imagine you hang a wet shirt outside on a cold winter day at very high altitude (low pressure). The shirt first **freezes stiff** (water becomes ice). Then, even though the temperature is well below 0 °C, the ice slowly **vanishes into thin air** without ever melting into water. The shirt ends up bone-dry but still retains its original shape, color, and texture. This is exactly what freeze drying does to food, vaccines, and antibiotics — it removes water as **vapor from ice**, preserving the molecular structure. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Freeze Drying The following standard engineering metrics are essential for KTU examination purposes: - **Triple Point of Water:** $P_{tp} = \mathbf{611.657 \text{ Pa}}$ at $\mathbf{0.01 \,°C}$ (273.16 K) - **Sublimation Enthalpy (Latent Heat):** $\Delta H_{sub} = \mathbf{2838 \text{ kJ/kg}}$ at 0 °C - **Heat of Vaporization (Liquid):** $\Delta H_{vap} = \mathbf{2501 \text{ kJ/kg}}$ - **Heat of Fusion:** $\Delta H_{fus} = \mathbf{334 \text{ kJ/kg}}$ - **Operating Chamber Pressure:** typically $\mathbf{10 \text{ to } 100 \, Pa}$ - **Condenser Temperature:** typically $\mathbf{-40 \,°C to -80 \,°C}$ (or lower) - **Critical Product Temperature:** must stay below **collapse temperature** $T_c$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Tip:** Always remember the **Triple Point Rule** — freeze drying is only possible when system pressure is **below 611.657 Pa**, otherwise ice will melt into liquid instead of sublimating. ## 1.4 Why Freeze Drying? The Engineering Rationale | Property | Conventional Drying (Hot Air) | Freeze Drying | |---|---|---| | Operating Temperature | 60–200 °C | -30 to -80 °C | | Phase transition | Liquid → Vapor | **Solid → Vapor** | | Heat damage | High (Maillard, denaturation) | **Negligible** | | Product shrinkage | Significant | **Minimal** | | Rehydration ability | Poor | **Excellent (>90%)** | | Shelf life extension | Moderate | **Excellent (1–5 years)** | | Nutrient retention | 40–60% | **>95%** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Sequential Stages of Freeze Drying A complete freeze drying cycle is executed in three rigorously controlled stages. Each stage has unique thermodynamic and mass transfer characteristics. ### Stage 1: Freezing Stage (Solidification) - The product is cooled below its **eutectic temperature** $T_{eu}$ (for crystalline substances) or **glass transition temperature** $T_g'$ (for amorphous substances). - Freezing converts free water into ice crystals; bound water remains in amorphous matrix. - **Cooling rate** determines ice crystal size: - **Slow freezing** → large ice crystals → faster primary drying, but possible structural damage - **Fast freezing** → small ice crystals → better structural retention, but slower mass transfer > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Concept:** The product must be frozen to at least $3\text{–}5\,°C$ below its $T_g'$ (or $T_{eu}$) to ensure all freezable water is converted to ice. Otherwise, residual liquid causes **melt-back** and product collapse. ### Stage 2: Primary Drying (Sublimation) - Chamber pressure is reduced **below the triple point pressure** ($P < 611.657$ Pa). - Heat is supplied via shelves (conduction + radiation). - Ice sublimates directly into vapor. - Vapor migrates through the **dried layer** (also called the *cake*). - Condenser collects the vapor by re-solidification. - This is the **longest stage** (often 60–80% of total cycle time). ### Stage 3: Secondary Drying (Desorption) - Remaining **bound water** (10–35% moisture) is removed by **desorption**. - Shelf temperature is raised (30–50 °C) under continued vacuum. - Driven by **isotherm desorption kinetics** at low moisture content. - This stage brings residual moisture to < 1–3% (w/w). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory for KTU Board Exams** — the following table consolidates all governing equations of freeze drying. | # | Equation | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{P_{ice} - P_c}{R_s} \cdot A$ | Sublimation rate (mass transfer) | kg/s | | 2 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_s \cdot \Delta H_{sub}$ | Heat required for sublimation | W (J/s) | | 3 | $R_s = \dfrac{R_p + R_s'}{A}$ | Total resistance to vapor flow | Pa·s/kg | | 4 | $R_p = \dfrac{L^2}{K_p}$ | Resistance of dried cake (Poisson's model) | m/s | | 5 | $K_p = \dfrac{k_d \cdot M_w}{\rho_{ice} \cdot R \cdot T}$ | Permeability of dried layer | kg·m/(m²·s·Pa) | | 6 | $J_w = \dfrac{D_w' \cdot (C_{w,i} - C_{w,p})}{\delta}$ | Water vapor flux in dried layer | kg/(m²·s) | | 7 | $\ln P_{ice} = -\dfrac{A}{T} + B$ | Vapor pressure of ice (Antoine-type) | Pa | | 8 | $t_d = \dfrac{L \cdot \rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub}}{T_s - T_b} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Primary drying time | seconds | | 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{dry}}{m_{initial}} \times 100$ | Yield efficiency | % | | 10 | $MR = \dfrac{X - X_e}{X_0 - X_e} = e^{-kt}$ | Moisture Ratio (Fickian model) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Notation:** The vertical pipe symbol is replaced by `\vert` in formulas to prevent markdown table corruption. ## 2.3 Heat Transfer Modes in Freeze Drying Three heat transfer mechanisms operate at the sublimation interface: 1. **Conduction through frozen layer** (from shelf): $$Q_c = \dfrac{k_f \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_i)}{L_f}$$ 2. **Conduction through dried cake** (from product surface): $$Q_d = \dfrac{k_d \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_i)}{L_d}$$ 3. **Radiation** (between shelves and vial walls): $$Q_r = \sigma \cdot F \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_i^4)$$ where $k_f$ and $k_d$ are thermal conductivities of frozen and dried layers respectively, $L_f$ and $L_d$ are their thicknesses, and $\sigma$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant. ## 2.4 Mass Transfer Through the Dried Cake Vapor escaping from the sublimation front must traverse the **dried porous cake**. This is governed by: - **Poisson's Equation** (laminar flow through porous medium): $$J_v = -\dfrac{K_p}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dz}$$ - **Knudsen flow contribution** (when pore size ≈ mean free path): $$J_K = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{r_p}{RT} \cdot \left( \dfrac{8RT}{\pi M_w} \right)^{1/2} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dz}$$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Industrial Applications of Freeze Drying:** > - **Pharmaceuticals:** Antibiotics (penicillin), vaccines (mRNA, viral vectors), injectables, blood plasma > - **Food Industry:** Instant coffee, freeze-dried fruits (strawberries, raspberries), space food (NASA), emergency rations > - **Biotechnology:** Enzyme preservation, probiotic cultures, diagnostic reagents > - **Cosmetics & Nutraceuticals:** Lyophilized hyaluronic acid, collagen serums > - **Botanical & Herbal:** Preservation of medicinal plant extracts --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Sublimation Rate from First Principles Starting from the **kinetic theory of sublimation**, the net mass flux leaving an ice surface equals the difference between the **molecular evaporation flux** and the **molecular condensation flux**. **Step 1: Hertz–Knudsen Equation** The rate of mass leaving the ice surface is given by: $$J_{evap} = \dfrac{P_{ice}(T_i)}{\sqrt{2 \pi M_w R T_i}}$$ **Step 2: Rate of mass returning to the surface** $$J_{cond} = \dfrac{P_c}{\sqrt{2 \pi M_w R T_i}}$$ **Step 3: Net sublimation flux** $$J_{net} = J_{evap} - J_{cond} = \dfrac{P_{ice}(T_i) - P_c}{\sqrt{2 \pi M_w R T_i}}$$ **Step 4: Convert flux to mass rate per unit area** $$\dot{m} = J_{net} \cdot A = \dfrac{A \cdot (P_{ice} - P_c)}{\sqrt{2 \pi M_w R T_i}}$$ **Step 5: Account for mass transfer resistance of dried layer** Including the resistance $R_s$ offered by the porous dried cake: $$\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{A \cdot (P_{ice} - P_c)}{R_s}$$ This is the **operational sublimation rate equation** used universally in pharmaceutical freeze drying cycle design. ## 3.2 Derivation: Primary Drying Time (Steady-State Model) Assume a slab geometry of thickness $L$ with one side heated at temperature $T_s$ and the other side being the ice interface at $T_i$. **Step 1: Heat transfer to sublimation front** $$\dot{Q} = \dfrac{k_d \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_i)}{L_d}$$ **Step 2: Mass balance at the sublimation interface** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_s \cdot \Delta H_{sub}$$ **Step 3: Equate (1) and (2)** $$\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{k_d \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_i)}{L_d \cdot \Delta H_{sub}}$$ **Step 4: Time required to sublime a layer of thickness $dL$** $$dt = \dfrac{\rho_{ice} \cdot A \cdot dL}{\dot{m}_s}$$ **Step 5: Integrate from $L=0$ to $L=L$ (total cake thickness)** $$t_d = \int_0^L \dfrac{\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot L_d}{k_d \cdot (T_s - T_i)} \, dL$$ **Step 6: Assuming constant temperature difference and constant resistance** $$t_d = \dfrac{\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot L^2}{2 k_d \cdot (T_s - T_i)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Primary drying time scales **quadratically** with product thickness $L$. Doubling the fill depth quadruples the drying time — a critical design constraint for **vial selection** in pharmaceutical manufacturing. ## 3.3 Derivation: Vapor Pressure of Ice (Clausius–Clapeyron) **Step 1: Clausius–Clapeyron Equation (solid–vapor equilibrium)** $$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sub}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{sub}}$$ **Step 2: Assume $\Delta V_{sub} \approx V_{vapor} = \dfrac{RT}{P}$** (vapor behaves as ideal gas; $V_{solid}$ negligible) $$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sub} \cdot P}{R \cdot T^2}$$ **Step 3: Separate variables and integrate** $$\int_{P_0}^{P} \dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sub}}{R} \int_{T_0}^{T} \dfrac{dT}{T^2}$$ **Step 4: Final integrated form** $$\ln \left( \dfrac{P}{P_0} \right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{sub}}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} \right)$$ **Step 5: Linearized working form (commonly tabulated)** $$\ln P = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$$ with empirical constants $A = 24.7219$ and $B = 6141.668$ K, valid from $173$ to $273$ K. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A freeze dryer processes **1 kg** of a pharmaceutical formulation initially containing **80% water (w/w)**. After drying, the residual moisture is **2% (w/w)**. The chamber pressure is held at **30 Pa** with shelf temperature at **-25 °C**. Calculate: (a) the mass of ice removed, (b) the heat required for primary drying, and (c) the sublimation rate per m² if the surface area is **0.05 m²** and the ice vapor pressure at -25 °C is **207 Pa**. **Solution:** **Part (a) — Mass of ice removed** Initial water content: $$m_{w,0} = 0.80 \times 1 = 0.80 \text{ kg}$$ Dry solid mass: $$m_s = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 \text{ kg}$$ Final water content (2% of total mass): $$m_{w,f} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.98} \times 0.20 = 0.00408 \text{ kg}$$ Ice removed (sublimated): $$m_{ice} = 0.80 - 0.00408 = 0.7959 \text{ kg} \approx \mathbf{0.796 \text{ kg}}$$ **Part (b) — Heat required for primary drying** Using $\Delta H_{sub} = 2838$ kJ/kg: $$Q = m_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} = 0.7959 \times 2838 = 2258.97 \text{ kJ} \approx \mathbf{2259 \text{ kJ}}$$ **Part (c) — Sublimation rate per unit area** $$\dot{m}_s/A = \dfrac{P_{ice} - P_c}{R_s} = \dfrac{207 - 30}{R_s} = \dfrac{177}{R_s}$$ Assuming a typical cake resistance $R_s = 2 \times 10^5$ Pa·s/kg: $$\dot{m}_s/A = \dfrac{177}{2 \times 10^5} = 8.85 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} = \mathbf{3.19 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)}}$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Freeze Drying vs. Alternatives | Parameter | Freeze Drying | Spray Drying | Fluidized Bed Drying | Vacuum Drying | |---|---|---|---|---| | Operating T (°C) | -40 to +50 | 150–220 | 40–120 | 30–80 | | Product State | Frozen | Liquid droplet | Granular | Wet solid | | Phase change | Solid → Vapor | Liquid → Vapor | Liquid → Vapor | Liquid → Vapor | | Energy Intensity | **High (2.5–3×)** | Moderate | Low | Moderate | | Quality retention | **Excellent** | Moderate | Good | Good | | Capital cost | **High** | High | Moderate | Moderate | | Cycle time | **Long (12–48 h)** | Seconds | Minutes | Hours | | Moisture final | <1% | 3–5% | 1–3% | 1–5% | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Complete Freeze Drying Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Aqueous Product Feed] --> B[Stage 1: Pre-treatment
Formulation, pH adjustment, Sterile filtration] B --> C[Stage 2: Loading onto Shelves
Vials or trays arranged] C --> D[Stage 3: Freezing Step
Cool to Tg' minus 3-5 deg C] D --> E{Is Temperature
below Teu / Tg'?} E -->|No| F[Extend Freezing Time] F --> E E -->|Yes| G[Stage 4: Vacuum Initiation
Reduce chamber pressure below 611 Pa] G --> H[Stage 5: Primary Drying
Sublimation under high vacuum
Heat supplied via shelves] H --> I[Stage 6: Vapor Transport
Vapor migrates through dried cake] I --> J[Stage 7: Condensation
Vapor condenses on cold plates below minus 40 deg C] J --> K[Stage 8: Secondary Drying
Raise shelf temp to 30-50 deg C] K --> L[Stage 9: Final Moisture Check
Target below 1 percent w/w] L --> M[Stage 10: Stoppering and Sealing
Under vacuum or inert gas] M --> N[End: Lyophilized Product Ready
Long shelf life preserved] ``` ## 4.2 Equipment Architecture: Freeze Dryer Components ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ChamberUnit[Drying Chamber Assembly] S1[Shelf 1

T greater than 0.01 deg C and P greater than 611 Pa] -->|Cooling at 1 atm| B[Liquid Water Below 0 deg C
Metastable Supercooled] B -->|Nucleation and freezing| C[Ice Region
Solid Phase] C -->|Pressure reduction below 611 Pa| D[Sublimation Path
Solid to Vapor Direct Transition] D --> E[Water Vapor Region
Low pressure, low temperature] E -->|Condensation on cold coils| F[Re-frozen Ice on Condenser
Minus 50 deg C] F -->|Defrost cycle| G[Liquid Water Removed
Drain] ``` ## 4.4 Mass and Heat Transfer Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HT[Heat Transfer Pathway] H1[Heat Source
Shelf with circulating fluid] --> H2[Heat Conduction
Through frozen layer] H2 --> H3[Sublimation Interface
At ice front] H3 --> H4[Latent Heat Absorption
Delta H sub equals 2838 kJ per kg] end subgraph MT[Mass Transfer Pathway] M1[Water Vapor Generation
At sublimation interface] --> M2[Diffusion Through
Dried porous cake] M2 --> M3[Pressure Driven Flow
Darcy regime] M3 --> M4[Chamber Headspace
Low pressure] M4 --> M5[Condenser Surface
Minus 50 deg C cold trap] M5 --> M6[Ice Deposition
on condenser coils] end H3 -.-> M1 classDef htNode fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1.5px classDef mtNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1.5px class H1,H2,H3,H4 htNode class M1,M2,M3,M4,M5,M6 mtNode ``` ## 4.5 Process Control Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Recipe Input
Setpoints from HMI] --> B[PLC Controller] B --> C[Shelf Temperature Control Loop] B --> D[Chamber Pressure Control Loop] B --> E[Condenser Temperature Loop] C --> F[Solid State Relay
Heater Activation] D --> G[Throttling Valve
Vacuum Adjustment] E --> H[Refrigeration Compressor
On Off Control] F --> I[Process Outputs
Dried Product] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Feedback Signals] J --> B ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Define freeze drying. What is the role of the triple point of water in this process?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Freeze drying (lyophilization) is a low-temperature dehydration process in which water is removed from a frozen product by **sublimation** — the direct conversion of ice (solid) into water vapor (gas) under high vacuum — bypassing the liquid phase. **Triple Point Role (1.5 marks):** The triple point of water ($P_{tp} = 611.657$ Pa, $T_{tp} = 0.01 \,°C$) is the unique condition at which solid, liquid, and vapor phases coexist in equilibrium. For freeze drying to occur by sublimation (solid → vapor), the chamber pressure **must be maintained below 611.657 Pa**. If pressure exceeds this value, the ice would melt into liquid water rather than sublime, ruining the lyophilization process. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **List any three advantages and two disadvantages of freeze drying as compared to conventional drying methods.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages (2 marks — 0.67 each):** 1. **Superior quality retention:** Low operating temperatures prevent thermal degradation of heat-sensitive nutrients, vitamins, and pharmaceutical actives. 2. **Excellent rehydration:** The porous structure formed during sublimation allows rapid and near-complete rehydration (>90%). 3. **Long shelf life:** Residual moisture below 1–3% inhibits microbial growth and chemical degradation, extending shelf life to 1–5 years. **Disadvantages (1 mark — 0.5 each):** 1. **High capital and operating cost:** Vacuum pumps, condensers, and long cycle times (12–48 hours) make freeze drying expensive. 2. **Energy-intensive:** Energy consumption is 2.5–3 times higher than spray drying due to sublimation enthalpy requirements. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Adapted from KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a) Describe in detail the three stages of the freeze drying cycle with their governing principles.** (7 marks) **(b) A pharmaceutical product is freeze dried in vials of 2 cm fill depth. The initial moisture is 75% (w/w). After drying, residual moisture is 1.5% (w/w). If 500 vials are processed, each containing 10 mL of formulation (density 1.05 g/mL), calculate the total mass of water removed, the heat required, and the average primary drying time. Given: $\Delta H_{sub} = 2838$ kJ/kg, $k_d = 0.025$ W/m·K, $\rho_{ice} = 917$ kg/m³, $(T_s - T_i) = 25$ K.** (7 marks) ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### Part (a) Solution (7 marks): **Stage 1: Freezing (2 marks)** The product is cooled in the freeze dryer shelves to a temperature **below the eutectic temperature ($T_{eu}$) for crystalline products** or **below the glass transition temperature of the freeze concentrate ($T_g'$) for amorphous products**, typically $3\text{–}5\,°C$ below $T_g'$. Freezing converts free water into ice crystals, while bound water remains in the amorphous matrix. Cooling rate controls ice crystal morphology — slow freezing produces large crystals (faster drying, possible structural damage), whereas fast freezing produces small crystals (better retention, slower drying). Proper freezing prevents "melt-back" during subsequent vacuum application. **Stage 2: Primary Drying (3 marks)** After freezing, the chamber is evacuated to a pressure **below 611.657 Pa** (triple point of water). Controlled heat is applied via the shelves. The ice **sublimates directly into vapor** at the sublimation interface. The water vapor must diffuse through the **already-dried porous cake** to reach the chamber headspace, then travel to the **condenser** held at $-40\,°C$ to $-80\,°C$, where it re-solidifies. The driving force is the **vapor pressure difference** $(P_{ice} - P_{chamber})$. The sublimation rate is described by: $$\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{A(P_{ice} - P_c)}{R_s}$$ This stage is the **slowest** (60–80% of total cycle time) and is governed by coupled **heat transfer** (from shelf through frozen layer to interface) and **mass transfer** (vapor through dried cake). **Stage 3: Secondary Drying (2 marks)** Once all free ice is sublimated, the product temperature is raised to $30\text{–}50\,°C$ while maintaining vacuum. The remaining **bound water** (typically 5–20% of original) is removed by **desorption** from the amorphous matrix. This follows an **isothermal desorption kinetics** model and reduces residual moisture to < 1–3% (w/w). This stage typically takes 3–6 hours and is critical for long-term stability. #### Part (b) Solution (7 marks): **Given:** - Fill depth: $L = 2$ cm $= 0.02$ m - Initial moisture: 75% (w/w) → $X_0 = 0.75$ - Final moisture: 1.5% (w/w) → $X_f = 0.015$ - Vials: $N = 500$ - Volume per vial: $V = 10$ mL - Density: $\rho = 1.05$ g/mL - $\Delta H_{sub} = 2838$ kJ/kg - $k_d = 0.025$ W/m·K - $\rho_{ice} = 917$ kg/m³ - $(T_s - T_i) = 25$ K **Step 1: Mass per vial** $$m_{vial} = V \cdot \rho = 10 \times 1.05 = 10.5 \text{ g}$$ **Step 2: Water removed per vial** $$m_{w,vial} = 10.5 \times (0.75 - 0.015) = 10.5 \times 0.735 = 7.7175 \text{ g}$$ **Step 3: Total water removed** $$m_{w,total} = 500 \times 7.7175 = 3858.75 \text{ g} = \mathbf{3.859 \text{ kg}}$$ **Step 4: Total heat required for primary drying** $$Q_{total} = m_{w,total} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} = 3.859 \times 2838 = 10951.8 \text{ kJ} \approx \mathbf{1.095 \times 10^4 \text{ kJ}}$$ **Step 5: Primary drying time (per vial, using derived formula)** $$t_d = \dfrac{\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot k_d \cdot (T_s - T_i)}$$ **Step 6: Substitute values (unit conversions: $\Delta H_{sub} = 2838 \times 10^3$ J/kg)** $$t_d = \dfrac{917 \times (2838 \times 10^3) \times (0.02)^2}{2 \times 0.025 \times 25}$$ $$t_d = \dfrac{917 \times 2.838 \times 10^6 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{1.25}$$ $$t_d = \dfrac{917 \times 1135.2}{1.25} = \dfrac{1,041,000}{1.25} \approx 832,800 \text{ s}$$ **Step 7: Convert to hours** $$t_d = \dfrac{832,800}{3600} \approx \mathbf{231.3 \text{ hours} \approx 9.64 \text{ days}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Mark Loss Alert):** Students often forget to convert $\Delta H_{sub}$ from kJ/kg to J/kg when using SI base units. The unit conversion **kJ → J (multiply by $10^3$)** is mandatory. A typical mark deduction of **1 mark** is applied for unit inconsistency. Also, the drying time formula uses the **square of depth** ($L^2$), not linear depth — confusing this costs **1 mark**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Adapted Pattern]** **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a laboratory-scale freeze dryer. List the main components and state the function of each.** (7 marks) **(b) Discuss in detail the industrial applications of freeze drying in the pharmaceutical and food industries. Compare freeze drying with spray drying based on five key parameters.** (7 marks) ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### Part (a) Solution (7 marks): **Construction (4 marks):** A laboratory-scale freeze dryer consists of four main subsystems: 1. **Drying Chamber:** A stainless steel vacuum-tight vessel containing horizontal **shelves** (typically 3–5) that are temperature-controlled by a circulating heat transfer fluid (silicone oil). Vials or trays are placed on these shelves. 2. **Vacuum System:** Comprises a **rotary vane vacuum pump** (or combination of rotary + diffusion pump for ultra-high vacuum) capable of achieving chamber pressures of 1–100 Pa, connected via an **isolation butterfly valve** to allow chamber separation. 3. **Condenser Unit:** Located between the chamber and the vacuum pump, containing **refrigerated coils** at $-40\,°C$ to $-80\,°C$. Vapor from the chamber condenses and deposits as ice on these coils, protecting the pump from contamination. 4. **Control System:** A **PLC** with **HMI** interface for recipe programming, executing temperature ramps, pressure setpoints, and end-of-stage criteria. Includes **Pirani + capacitance pressure transducers** and **product thermocouples**. **Working (3 marks):** The product is loaded, frozen on the shelves, then vacuum is applied. Sublimation occurs as heat is supplied through the shelves. Vapor travels to the condenser where it is trapped. After primary drying, shelf temperature is raised for secondary drying. Finally, vials are stoppered under vacuum using hydraulically actuated stoppering bars. #### Part (b) Solution (7 marks): **Pharmaceutical Applications (2 marks):** - Preservation of **injectable antibiotics** (e.g., penicillin G sodium, ceftriaxone) - **mRNA vaccines and viral vector** formulations (e.g., COVID-19 vaccines) - **Lyophilized protein therapeutics** (monoclonal antibodies, enzymes) - **Diagnostic kits** and **blood plasma derivatives** **Food Industry Applications (2 marks):** - **Instant coffee and tea** (retains aroma compounds lost in spray drying) - **Freeze-dried fruits** for breakfast cereals and bakery - **Space food and military rations** (lightweight, long shelf life) - **Probiotic cultures** and **enzyme preparations** for food processing **Comparison with Spray Drying (3 marks — table format):** | Parameter | Freeze Drying | Spray Drying | |---|---|---| | Operating temperature | $-30$ to $+50\,°C$ | $150$–$220\,°C$ | | Product form | Solid cake / powder from frozen | Powder from liquid droplet | | Heat damage | Negligible | Significant for heat-sensitive compounds | | Energy consumption | High (2.5–3×) | Moderate | | Capital cost | Very high | High | | Final moisture | <1% | 3–5% | | Product rehydration | Excellent | Moderate | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Freeze Drying Questions:** > > 1. **Triple Point Confusion:** Students frequently write "freeze drying is done at 0 °C and atmospheric pressure" — this is **incorrect** and costs **1 mark** immediately. Always state: *chamber pressure < 611.657 Pa* and *product temperature < 0 °C*. > > 2. **Stages Confusion:** Many students merge **Primary Drying** (sublimation) and **Secondary Drying** (desorption) into a single process. Examiners allot **2 marks** specifically for distinguishing them — losing this distinction is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. > > 3. **Unit Mismatch:** The sublimation equation is dimensionally sensitive. Mixing **kJ** with **J** or using **atm** instead of **Pa** results in **1 mark** penalty. > > 4. **Forgetting Bound Water:** In secondary drying questions, students often assume "all water is removed" — but bound water (~5–20%) requires desorption, not sublimation. Examiners test this specifically. > > 5. **Confusing $T_g'$ with $T_{eu}$:** Crystalline products use $T_{eu}$ (eutectic), amorphous products use $T_g'$ (glass transition). Using the wrong one is a **1-mark** loss. > > 6. **Heat Transfer Direction:** Heat flows **from the shelf through the frozen layer to the sublimation interface**, not from the chamber. Reversed description loses **0.5–1 mark**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme** - **Freeze drying = Lyophilization = Solid-to-Vapor phase transition (sublimation).** - **Triple point of water:** 611.657 Pa at 0.01 °C — **chamber pressure must be below this**. - **Sublimation enthalpy:** $\Delta H_{sub} = 2838$ kJ/kg at 0 °C. - **Three stages:** Freezing → Primary Drying (sublimation) → Secondary Drying (desorption). - **Freezing criteria:** $T < T_{eu} - 3$ to $5\,°C$ (crystalline) **OR** $T < T_g' - 3$ to $5\,C$ (amorphous). - **Primary drying driver:** Vapor pressure difference $(P_{ice} - P_{chamber})$. - **Secondary drying driver:** Desorption isotherms at elevated temperature. - **Condenser temperature:** Typically $-40$ to $-80\,°C$; must be colder than product. - **Drying time scales with** $L^2$ — quadratic dependence on fill depth. - **Resistance to mass transfer** $R_s$ depends on dried cake thickness and permeability. - **Sublimation rate equation:** $\dot{m}_s = A(P_{ice} - P_c)/R_s$. - **Vapor pressure of ice:** $\ln P = A - B/T$ (Antoine-type linearized form). - **Final moisture target:** < 1–3% (w/w) for pharmaceutical; < 5% acceptable for food. - **Stoppering is done under vacuum** or under inert gas (N₂) to maintain shelf life. - **Rehydration capacity:** > 90% (a hallmark of properly lyophilized products). - **Energy intensity:** 2.5–3× higher than spray drying — major CAPEX/OPEX concern. - **Industrial users:** Pfizer, Novartis, GSK, Nestlé, Starbucks (instant coffee), NASA. - **Key advantages:** Thermal preservation, structural retention, long shelf life, rehydration. - **Key disadvantages:** Cost, time, energy demand, batch process. - **Critical design parameters:** Shelf temperature, chamber pressure, fill depth, vial geometry, condenser capacity. - **Quality control tests:** Residual moisture (Karl Fischer), reconstitution time, cake appearance, BET surface area. - **Freeze dry microscopy (FDM)** is used to determine collapse temperature experimentally. ---

topics.title: Study on Spray Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study on Spray Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spray Drying** is a continuous, single-step particle-processing operation in which a liquid feed (solution, emulsion, suspension, or paste) is **atomized into a spray of fine droplets** and brought into intimate contact with a **hot drying gas** (typically air or nitrogen), resulting in the **rapid evaporation of moisture** and the formation of **dry, free-flowing powder particles** within a single closed vessel (the drying chamber).

In the strict vocabulary of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCFTL507 – Pharmaceutical / Chemical Formulation Technology Laboratory)** syllabus, spray drying is classified under the broader umbrella of **drying technology** alongside tray drying, fluidized bed drying, and freeze drying. It is distinct because it combines **three unit operations in one piece of equipment**:

1. **Atomization** (mechanical dispersion of liquid into droplets)
2. **Droplet–gas contact** (heat & mass transfer)
3. **Particle formation & separation** (powder recovery via cyclone / bag filter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics in the Industry**
> - **Inlet temperature range:** 150 °C – 220 °C (co-current open cycle)
> - **Outlet temperature range:** 80 °C – 110 °C
> - **Residence time:** 5 – 30 seconds (short!)
> - **Feed droplet size:** 10 – 500 µm
> - **Final particle size:** 1 – 200 µm
> - **Standard test reference:** **USP ⟨786⟩** for *particulate matter in injectables* often relies on spray-dried APIs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine boiling water in a kettle. Steam escapes as large puffs, and the water inside the kettle slowly reduces. Now imagine if you could **blow that same amount of water into millions of microscopic droplets inside a hot room** — every droplet would dry almost instantly because its surface area is enormous compared to its volume. **That is exactly what a spray dryer does.**

A real-world analogy everyone has experienced:

> ☕ **Instant Coffee Production** — Green coffee extract is sprayed as a fine mist into a tall, hot tower. Within seconds, the water evaporates and the dry coffee powder settles. The same tower that takes **hours** in a tray dryer finishes the job in **seconds**. The secret is **atomization**: breaking the liquid into millions of tiny droplets, which dramatically accelerates evaporation.

A second analogy:

> 🍶 **Drying milk on a hot pan** vs. **drying milk in a spray dryer** — A pan can only dry a thin film slowly. A spray dryer creates the "thin film" in mid-air, across millions of droplets, all at once. The total surface area exposed to hot air increases by **three to four orders of magnitude**.

## 1.3 Why Spray Drying Matters (Engineering Significance)

Spray drying is indispensable in industries where:

| Industry | Typical Product |
|----------|----------------|
| Pharmaceuticals | Dry-powder inhalers (DPIs), excipients, heat-sensitive APIs (with co-current design) |
| Food & Dairy | Milk powder, coffee, egg powder, flavors |
| Ceramics & Catalysts | Alumina, zirconia, catalyst supports |
| Detergents | Enzyme encapsulation, detergent powders |
| Polymers & Dyes | Pigment powders, latex drying |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** Spray drying is the only continuous drying method that **simultaneously produces a dry powder of defined particle size, morphology, and residual moisture** in a single step. It is therefore the gold standard for **thermal-sensitive products** when co-current flow is used.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle (The "How")

The spray drying process follows a deterministic sequence of phenomena:

1. **Feed preparation** — The liquid feed is homogenized, filtered, and pre-heated if necessary. Feed solids concentration typically lies between **10 % w/w and 50 % w/w**.
2. **Atomization** — The feed is pumped to an atomizer, which converts the bulk liquid into a fine spray of droplets. The choice of atomizer dictates the **spray pattern** (full-cone, hollow-cone, or flat-fan) and the **droplet size distribution**.
3. **Droplet–hot gas contact** — The spray meets a stream of pre-heated drying gas (usually air). Two flow configurations exist:
   - **Co-current flow** — spray and gas move in the *same direction* (gentle, used for heat-sensitive products)
   - **Counter-current flow** — spray and gas move in *opposite directions* (more efficient heat transfer, used for heat-resistant products)
4. **Evaporation & particle formation** — Moisture is driven off the droplet surface; the droplet shrinks, develops a crust (when the solute reaches saturation), and finally becomes a dry particle.
5. **Particle–gas separation** — Dry powder is separated from the exhaust gas using a **cyclone separator** (primary collector) and a **bag filter** (secondary collector).
6. **Exhaust gas treatment** — The humid exhaust is filtered and vented.

## 2.2 The Three "Governing Pillars" of Spray Drying

### 🔹 Pillar 1 — Atomization
Atomization controls **droplet size**, which directly determines **final particle size**. A widely used empirical correlation for centrifugal (rotary) atomizers is:

$$ d_{50} \;=\; \frac{K \cdot Q^{0.5}}{(N^{0.6} \cdot D^{0.4} \cdot \mu_f^{0.2})} $$

where:
- $d_{50}$ = **mass-median droplet diameter** (m)
- $Q$ = feed flow rate (m³/s)
- $N$ = rotational speed of the disc (rev/s)
- $D$ = disc diameter (m)
- $\mu_f$ = feed viscosity (Pa·s)
- $K$ = empirical constant (depends on disc design)

### 🔹 Pillar 2 — Heat & Mass Transfer
The classical **droplet evaporation model** (Ranz–Marshall correlation) gives the Nusselt number for a single droplet:

$$ Nu \;=\; 2.0 \;+\; 0.6 \, Re^{0.5} \, Pr^{0.33} $$

For the entire dryer, the **overall energy balance** is:

$$ Q_{in,gas} \;=\; m_s \, \lambda \, (W_2 - W_1) \;+\; m_{ds} \, c_{p,ds} \, (T_{out} - T_{feed}) $$

where $\lambda$ is the latent heat of vaporization, $W_1, W_2$ are inlet and outlet humidity ratios, and $m_s, m_{ds}$ are mass flow rates of solvent and dry solid, respectively.

### 🔹 Pillar 3 — Particle Recovery
The cyclone cut-size (diameter at 50 % collection efficiency, $d_{50,c}$) is given by:

$$ d_{50,c} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{9 \, \mu_g \, B_c}{2 \pi \, N_e \, \rho_p \, v_i}} $$

This is the **minimum particle diameter** that the cyclone will retain with ≥ 50 % efficiency.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below summarizes every equation you must memorize for the lab viva + ESE.**

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | Application in Lab |
|---|--------------------|---------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Mass balance (overall) | $F_{in} = F_{out} + E_{loss}$ | F = mass flow rate (kg/h) | Verifying product recovery |
| 2 | Moisture evaporation rate | $\dot{m}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{gas}(W_1 - W_2)$ | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, W = humidity ratio (kg water/kg dry air) | Calculating water removed |
| 3 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{amb}} \times 100$ | Temperatures in K or °C | Performance assessment |
| 4 | Specific energy consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{\dot{m}_{evap}}$ | kJ per kg of water | Comparing dryers |
| 5 | Ranz–Marshall (Nusselt) | $Nu = 2.0 + 0.6\, Re^{0.5} Pr^{0.33}$ | dimensionless | Droplet evaporation |
| 6 | Reynolds number (droplet) | $Re_d = \dfrac{\rho_g \, v \, d_d}{\mu_g}$ | $\rho_g$ = gas density, $v$ = relative velocity, $d_d$ = droplet diameter | Atomization study |
| 7 | Mass-median droplet size (rotary) | $d_{50} = \dfrac{K \cdot Q^{0.5}}{N^{0.6} \, D^{0.4} \, \mu_f^{0.2}}$ | m | Particle-size prediction |
| 8 | Cyclone cut size | $d_{50,c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu_g B_c}{2 \pi N_e \rho_p v_i}}$ | m | Recovery efficiency |
| 9 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$ | % | Powder characterization |
| 10 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d}$ | kg water / kg dry solid | Drying kinetics |
| 11 | Spray dryer capacity (liquid feed) | $\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{evap} + \dot{m}_{powder}$ | kg/h | Material balance |
| 12 | Heat duty of heater | $Q_h = \dot{m}_{air} \, c_{p,air} \, (T_{in} - T_{amb})$ | kW | Energy audit |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPOGRAPHY RULE (KTU valuation):** Always use $\vert$ (written as `\vert` in LaTeX) instead of the keyboard pipe `|` when writing absolute values in answers. E.g., $\vert \Delta T \vert$ **not** $\vert\Delta T\vert$ with the literal pipe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharma industry:** Spray drying is used to produce **amorphous solid dispersions** of poorly water-soluble drugs, dramatically improving bioavailability.
- **Food industry:** It is the cornerstone of **instant coffee, infant formula, and whey powder** production.
- **Materials engineering:** Spray-dried ceramic powders yield **spherical, free-flowing granules** ideal for press-and-sinter processing.
- **Energy efficiency:** Modern spray dryers integrate **waste-heat recovery** and **closed-cycle nitrogen loops** for oxygen-sensitive products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why" matters (the KTU examiner's "Show Understanding" rule):** Examiners often award the *understanding* mark when you state *why* a particular atomizer is chosen. E.g., "A **centrifugal (rotary) atomizer** is selected for highly viscous or particulate-laden feeds because it is **non-clogging** and produces a wider droplet-size distribution than pressure nozzles."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Mass & Energy Balance of a Spray Dryer

This is the single most important derivation for KTU ESE. We will derive it in **twelve explicit steps** so that no student can lose the "step mark" allocated in the marking scheme.

### Given (typical ESE problem)
A spray dryer processes **100 kg/h** of a milk solution containing **20 % w/w total solids**. The feed enters at **25 °C**, the inlet air is at **200 °C** with absolute humidity **0.012 kg water/kg dry air**, and the outlet air is at **95 °C** with absolute humidity **0.038 kg water/kg dry air**. Calculate:
- (a) the rate of water evaporation,
- (b) the rate of dry powder production,
- (c) the thermal efficiency of the dryer.

**Assumptions**
- Steady-state operation
- No heat loss to surroundings (idealized)
- Dry air flow rate is constant (humidification only adds water to the air)
- Specific heat of dry air: $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Specific heat of water vapor: $c_{p,v} = 1.88$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Latent heat of vaporization at ~100 °C: $\lambda = 2257$ kJ/kg
- Ambient temperature: $T_{amb} = 25$ °C

### Step 1 — Express the mass flows clearly
- Feed mass flow rate: $F_{feed} = 100$ kg/h
- Solids mass flow rate (constant throughout, since solids are non-volatile):
$$ F_s = 0.20 \times 100 = 20 \text{ kg/h} $$
- Water in feed:
$$ F_{w,feed} = 0.80 \times 100 = 80 \text{ kg/h} $$

### Step 2 — Write the overall mass balance
The water leaving the dryer appears in two streams:
1. As **water vapor absorbed by the exhaust air**,
2. As **residual moisture remaining in the powder**.

If the **outlet powder moisture** is $M_{db}$ (dry basis) kg water / kg dry solid, then:
$$ \text{Water in product} = M_{db} \times F_s $$
$$ \text{Water evaporated} = F_{w,feed} - M_{db} \times F_s $$

### Step 3 — Write the air-side mass balance
For the drying air:
$$ \dot{m}_{da} \, (W_2 - W_1) = \dot{m}_{evap} $$
where $\dot{m}_{da}$ is the mass flow rate of **dry air** (constant). Solving for the dry air flow:
$$ \dot{m}_{da} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{evap}}{W_2 - W_1} $$

### Step 4 — Plug the humidity data
$$ W_2 - W_1 = 0.038 - 0.012 = 0.026 \; \text{kg water / kg dry air} $$

### Step 5 — Assume a typical product moisture $M_{db} = 0.04$ kg/kg
For a milk powder, a typical outlet moisture is ~3–4 % w.b., i.e., $M_{db} \approx 0.04$ kg/kg.
$$ \dot{m}_{evap} = 80 - (0.04 \times 20) = 80 - 0.8 = 79.2 \text{ kg/h} $$

### Step 6 — Calculate the dry air flow rate
$$ \dot{m}_{da} = \dfrac{79.2}{0.026} = 3046.15 \; \text{kg dry air / h} $$

### Step 7 — Calculate the rate of dry powder production
$$ \dot{m}_{powder} = F_s + (\text{residual water}) = 20 + 0.8 = 20.8 \; \text{kg/h} $$

### Step 8 — Energy balance — sensible heat of inlet air
$$ Q_{in,sensible} = \dot{m}_{da} \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{ref}) $$
Using $T_{ref} = 0$ °C as datum:
$$ Q_{in,sensible} = 3046.15 \times 1.005 \times (200 - 0) = 612{,}270 \; \text{kJ/h} $$

### Step 9 — Energy balance — heat required for evaporation
$$ Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_{evap} \cdot \lambda = 79.2 \times 2257 = 178{,}754.4 \; \text{kJ/h} $$

### Step 10 — Energy balance — heat lost in exhaust air (sensible)
The outlet air carries heat as:
$$ Q_{out,sensible} = \dot{m}_{da} \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{out} - 0) + \dot{m}_{v,out} \cdot c_{p,v} \cdot T_{out} $$
$$ \dot{m}_{v,out} = \dot{m}_{da} \cdot W_2 = 3046.15 \times 0.038 = 115.75 \; \text{kg/h} $$
$$ Q_{out,sensible} = (3046.15 \times 1.005 \times 95) + (115.75 \times 1.88 \times 95) $$
$$ = 290{,}856 + 20{,}679 = 311{,}535 \; \text{kJ/h} $$

### Step 11 — Calculate the heat actually utilized for evaporation
Energy used to evaporate water = energy supplied to air − energy leaving with exhaust:
$$ Q_{used} = Q_{in,sensible} - Q_{out,sensible} = 612{,}270 - 311{,}535 = 300{,}735 \; \text{kJ/h} $$

### Step 12 — Calculate the thermal efficiency
$$ \eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{used}}{Q_{in,sensible}} \times 100 = \dfrac{300{,}735}{612{,}270} \times 100 \approx 49.1 \;\% $$

This result is **realistic**: industrial spray dryers typically operate at **40 – 60 % thermal efficiency** in co-current open cycle.

### Step 13 — Final consolidated answer

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{evap}   &= 79.2 \;\; \text{kg/h} \\
\dot{m}_{powder} &= 20.8 \;\; \text{kg/h} \\
\eta_{th}        &\approx 49.1 \;\; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Particle-Size Prediction (Master Equation)

A useful rule-of-thumb to derive in the exam: how feed solids concentration and droplet size control product particle size.

**Starting principle:** A single droplet of diameter $d_d$ containing solids of mass fraction $X_s$ yields a final dry particle of diameter $d_p$. By conservation of solids mass:
$$ \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^{\,3} \, \rho_p = \frac{\pi}{6} d_d^{\,3} \, \rho_f \, X_s $$

Solving:
$$ d_p = d_d \left(\dfrac{\rho_f \, X_s}{\rho_p}\right)^{1/3} $$

**Substituting $\rho_f \approx 1050$ kg/m³ (milk solution), $X_s = 0.20$, and $\rho_p \approx 1100$ kg/m³ (dry milk):**
$$ d_p = d_d \, (0.20 \times 1050 / 1100)^{1/3} = d_d \, (0.1909)^{1/3} \approx 0.575 \, d_d $$

This means: **for a droplet of 100 µm, the dry particle is approximately 57.5 µm**. KTU examiners love this derivation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Operating Variables Affect Product Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is gold for viva questions. Memorize the trends, not the numbers.**

| Operating Variable | Direction of Change | Effect on Outlet Temperature | Effect on Particle Size | Effect on Residual Moisture | Effect on Powder Yield |
|--------------------|---------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| Feed flow rate $\uparrow$ | Increase | Decreases | Increases | Increases | Decreases (more wall deposition) |
| Inlet temperature $\uparrow$ | Increase | Increases | Decreases (faster crust formation) | Decreases | Slight increase |
| Atomizer speed / pressure $\uparrow$ | Increase | Decreases (more evaporation area) | Decreases | Decreases (faster drying) | Slight decrease (fines loss) |
| Feed solids concentration $\uparrow$ | Increase | Increases | Increases | Decreases | Increases |
| Aspirator flow rate $\uparrow$ | Increase | Decreases | Decreases | Increases | Decreases (fines to bag filter) |
| Feed viscosity $\uparrow$ | Increase | Slight decrease | Increases | Increases | Decreases |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Spray Dryer

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block is a **functional flow diagram** of the entire spray-drying system. Every node has a clean alphanumeric identifier and an unembellished label, in strict compliance with the diagram-rendering rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FeedTank] --> A2[FeedPump]
    A2 --> A3[Atomizer]
    H1[AirFilter] --> H2[Heater]
    H2 --> H3[HotAirInlet]
    A3 --> D1[DryingChamber]
    H3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[DriedParticleStream]
    D1 --> D3[ExhaustGasStream]
    D2 --> S1[PrimaryCyclone]
    S1 --> S2[ProductHopper]
    S1 --> S3[FinesToBagFilter]
    D3 --> S3
    S3 --> S4[CleanExhaust]
    S4 --> V1[ExhaustBlower]
    C1[ControlPanel] -.-> A2
    C1 -.-> A3
    C1 -.-> H2
    C1 -.-> V1
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **A1 → A2 → A3:** Liquid feed is metered and atomized.
- **H1 → H2 → H3:** Ambient air is filtered, electrically/steam-heated, and introduced as hot drying gas.
- **D1:** The drying chamber is where atomization meets hot air.
- **S1 → S2:** The cyclone separates the bulk powder into the product hopper.
- **S3 → S4:** Fines that escape the cyclone are captured in a bag filter.
- **C1 (dashed):** The control panel monitors and regulates pump speed, atomizer speed, heater power, and exhaust blower.

## 4.2 Atomizer Selection Logic (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start] --> Q2{Feed is a Solution or Suspension?}
    Q2 -->|Solution, low viscosity| Q3[PressureNozzle]
    Q2 -->|Suspension or High Viscosity| Q4[RotaryCentrifugalDisc]
    Q2 -->|Paste or Very Viscous| Q5[TwoFluidPneumaticNozzle]
    Q3 --> Q6[Produces fine spray, narrow distribution]
    Q4 --> Q7[Coarser spray, handles particulates]
    Q5 --> Q8[Most flexible, highest energy use]
```

## 4.3 Process Variable Interconnection Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[InletTemp] --> P2[OutletTemp]
    P3[FeedFlow] --> P2
    P4[AtomizerSpeed] --> P5[DropletSize]
    P5 --> P6[ParticleSize]
    P3 --> P7[ResidualMoisture]
    P2 --> P7
    P1 --> P7
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P8[PowderYield]
    P7 --> P8
    P2 --> P9[ThermalEfficiency]
    P1 --> P9
    P3 --> P9
```

### Diagram Interpretation

- **Outlet temperature** is governed by **inlet temperature**, **feed flow rate**, and **aspirator flow**.
- **Particle size** is governed by **atomizer speed/pressure** and **feed viscosity**.
- **Residual moisture** depends on the trio: **inlet temperature, outlet temperature, feed flow**.
- **Powder yield** is the ultimate measure of process success, integrating all variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Observation Tip:** During the experiment, always note the **droplet size estimate from the atomizer type**, the **chamber wall deposits** (a sign of over-wetting), and the **bag-filter fines color** (a sign of over-drying or excessive atomization).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define spray drying and list its main industrial applications.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Spray drying** is a continuous particle-formation process in which a liquid feed is atomized into a hot drying chamber to produce a dry powder in a single step. The three unit operations combined are: (i) **atomization**, (ii) **heat and mass transfer**, and (iii) **particle separation**.
>
> **Main applications:**
> - Pharmaceutical: production of dry-powder inhalers, excipients, amorphous solid dispersions.
> - Food: milk powder, instant coffee, infant formula, flavors.
> - Ceramics and catalysts: production of spherical, free-flowing granules.
> - Detergents: encapsulation of enzymes.
> - Dyes and polymers: pigment powders, latex drying.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare pressure-nozzle, rotary-disc, and two-fluid atomizers on the basis of droplet size, feed type, and energy consumption.*

**Model Answer:**

| Atomizer Type | Typical $d_{50}$ Droplet Size | Suitable Feed | Energy Use |
|---------------|--------------------------------|---------------|------------|
| **Pressure nozzle** | 30 – 150 µm | Low-viscosity, particle-free solutions | Low (uses feed pressure) |
| **Rotary disc** | 20 – 250 µm | Viscous feeds, suspensions, pastes | Moderate (electrical) |
| **Two-fluid (pneumatic)** | 5 – 60 µm | Highly viscous, pastes, slurries | High (compressed air) |

> **Key point to remember:** A rotary atomizer is preferred when the feed is a **suspension or high-viscosity paste** because it is **non-clogging** and produces a wide droplet size distribution.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Internal Choice (KTU Rule):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **co-current open-cycle spray dryer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spray dryer is fed with **150 kg/h** of a coffee extract containing **30 % w/w total solids** at **30 °C**. The inlet air temperature is **210 °C** and outlet air is at **95 °C** with humidity ratio $W_2 = 0.040$ kg/kg. The inlet air humidity ratio is $W_1 = 0.010$ kg/kg. Determine: **(i)** water evaporation rate, **(ii)** dry coffee powder production rate (assume outlet moisture $M_{db} = 0.05$ kg/kg), and **(iii)** thermal efficiency of the dryer. Use $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_{p,v} = 1.88$ kJ/(kg·K), and $\lambda = 2257$ kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

A **co-current open-cycle spray dryer** consists of the following components:

1. **Feed tank with stirrer** — to keep solids suspended.
2. **Feed pump** — peristaltic or positive-displacement, to deliver feed at controlled rate.
3. **Atomizer** — usually a **centrifugal disc** or **pressure nozzle**, located at the top of the drying chamber.
4. **Air filter** — to remove particulates from intake air.
5. **Heater** — electric or steam-fired air-heater, raising the air to **150 – 220 °C**.
6. **Drying chamber** — large cylindrical/cyclone-shaped vessel, often **3 – 8 m tall and 2 – 4 m in diameter**.
7. **Disperser** — at the top of the chamber, ensures uniform distribution of inlet air around the spray.
8. **Cyclone separator** — primary collection device, recovers ~95 % of the powder.
9. **Bag filter** — secondary collector for fines.
10. **Exhaust blower** — maintains slight negative pressure.
11. **Control panel** — monitors $T_{in}, T_{out}, P_{atomizer}, \dot{m}_{feed}, \dot{m}_{air}$.

**Working:** The feed is atomized at the top; simultaneously, hot air enters from the same top region and flows *downward* in the **same direction** as the spray. This co-current arrangement ensures that the **hottest air meets the wettest droplets** — the droplet surface remains cool due to evaporative cooling, protecting heat-sensitive products. The dry powder is collected at the chamber bottom and the cyclone, while the moist exhaust passes through the bag filter before venting.

> **Key "Show Understanding" line for the 7-mark answer:** *In co-current design, the partial pressure of water vapor at the droplet surface keeps the droplet temperature close to the wet-bulb temperature (~50 – 60 °C) even when inlet air is 200 °C, making the process ideal for heat-sensitive pharmaceuticals and biologicals.*

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute mass flows of solids and water in feed:**
$$ F_s = 0.30 \times 150 = 45 \; \text{kg/h} $$
$$ F_{w,feed} = 0.70 \times 150 = 105 \; \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 2 — Compute water evaporation rate:**
Residual water in product = $M_{db} \times F_s = 0.05 \times 45 = 2.25$ kg/h
$$ \dot{m}_{evap} = 105 - 2.25 = 102.75 \; \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 3 — Compute dry air flow rate:**
$$ \dot{m}_{da} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{evap}}{W_2 - W_1} = \dfrac{102.75}{0.040 - 0.010} = \dfrac{102.75}{0.030} = 3425 \; \text{kg dry air/h} $$

**Step 4 — Compute dry coffee powder rate:**
$$ \dot{m}_{powder} = 45 + 2.25 = 47.25 \; \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 5 — Energy balance — sensible heat input:**
$$ Q_{in} = \dot{m}_{da} \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot T_{in} = 3425 \times 1.005 \times 210 = 722{,}816 \; \text{kJ/h} $$

**Step 6 — Energy balance — heat lost in exhaust air:**
$$ \dot{m}_{v,out} = \dot{m}_{da} \cdot W_2 = 3425 \times 0.040 = 137 \; \text{kg/h} $$
$$ Q_{out} = (3425 \times 1.005 \times 95) + (137 \times 1.88 \times 95) $$
$$ = 326{,}940 + 24{,}468 = 351{,}408 \; \text{kJ/h} $$

**Step 7 — Thermal efficiency:**
$$ \eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{722{,}816 - 351{,}408}{722{,}816} \times 100 \approx 51.4 \;\% $$

**Final consolidated answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{evap}   &= 102.75 \;\; \text{kg/h} \\
\dot{m}_{powder} &= 47.25 \;\; \text{kg/h} \\
\eta_{th}        &\approx 51.4 \;\; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **stages of droplet drying** inside a spray-drying chamber, with a neat sketch of the droplet temperature vs. time profile. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical industry wants to spray-dry a drug solution to produce particles with $d_p = 50$ µm. The feed solids concentration is $X_s = 0.15$ w/w, density of feed is $1050$ kg/m³, and dry particle density is $1200$ kg/m³. Calculate: **(i)** the required droplet diameter, and **(ii)** the atomizer rotational speed if a rotary disc of diameter $D = 0.05$ m is used, the feed flow rate is $Q = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s, the feed viscosity is $\mu_f = 0.005$ Pa·s, and the constant $K = 0.4$. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

A droplet undergoes three classical stages as it travels through the drying chamber:

**Stage 1 — Initial adjustment (≈ 5 – 10 % of residence time):**
- The droplet is heated from feed temperature to the **wet-bulb temperature**.
- Evaporation rate increases as the droplet surface warms.
- Slope of $T$ vs. $t$ is positive and steep.

**Stage 2 — Constant-rate drying period (≈ 60 – 70 % of residence time):**
- Droplet surface is saturated with water; evaporation proceeds at a **constant rate** governed by external heat and mass transfer.
- Droplet temperature remains essentially constant at the **wet-bulb temperature** ($T_{wb}$).
- The droplet shrinks uniformly; diameter decreases linearly with time.

**Stage 3 — Falling-rate drying period (≈ 20 – 30 % of residence time):**
- A **dry crust** forms at the droplet surface once the solids concentration reaches saturation.
- Internal moisture must now diffuse through the crust — evaporation rate **falls**.
- Droplet temperature rises sharply towards the outlet gas temperature.
- Final crust thickness defines the morphology of the dry particle.

**Sketch description (verbal, for 7-mark answer):**
- $T$ on y-axis, $t$ on x-axis.
- Curve starts at $T_{feed}$, rises steeply to $T_{wb}$ (Stage 1).
- Plateau at $T_{wb}$ (Stage 2).
- Sharp rise in Stage 3, asymptotically approaching $T_{out}$.
- A second sketch should show droplet diameter $d$ vs. $t$: linear decrease in Stage 2, decelerating in Stage 3.

> **Show Understanding Tip:** Mention that for **amorphous solid dispersions**, the falling-rate stage is *prolonged* because the crust is rubbery, while for **crystalline products**, the crust is brittle and may fracture.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Use the particle-to-droplet size relation:**

$$
d_p = d_d \left(\dfrac{\rho_f \cdot X_s}{\rho_p}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for droplet diameter $d_d$:**

$$
d_d = d_p \left(\dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_f \cdot X_s}\right)^{1/3} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \left(\dfrac{1200}{1050 \times 0.15}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1200}{157.5} = 7.619 \quad\Rightarrow\quad (7.619)^{1/3} \approx 1.967
$$

$$
d_d = 50 \times 1.967 = 98.35 \;\mu\text{m} \approx 98.4 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the master equation for rotary disc atomizer:**

$$
d_{50} = \dfrac{K \cdot Q^{0.5}}{N^{0.6} \cdot D^{0.4} \cdot \mu_f^{0.2}}
$$

Substituting:
- $d_{50} = 98.35 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $K = 0.4$
- $Q = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s
- $D = 0.05$ m
- $\mu_f = 0.005$ Pa·s

$$
N^{0.6} = \dfrac{K \cdot Q^{0.5}}{d_{50} \cdot D^{0.4} \cdot \mu_f^{0.2}}
$$

Compute the numerator:
$$ K \cdot Q^{0.5} = 0.4 \times \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.4 \times 1.4142 \times 10^{-3} = 5.657 \times 10^{-4} $$

Compute the denominator:
$$ D^{0.4} = (0.05)^{0.4} = 10^{0.4 \times \log_{10}(0.05)} $$
$$ \log_{10}(0.05) = -1.3010 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 0.4 \times (-1.3010) = -0.5204 $$
$$ D^{0.4} = 10^{-0.5204} = 0.301 $$
$$ \mu_f^{0.2} = (0.005)^{0.2} = 10^{0.2 \times \log_{10}(0.005)} $$
$$ \log_{10}(0.005) = -2.3010 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 0.2 \times (-2.3010) = -0.4602 $$
$$ \mu_f^{0.2} = 10^{-0.4602} = 0.347 $$
$$ d_{50} \cdot D^{0.4} \cdot \mu_f^{0.2} = 98.35 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.301 \times 0.347 = 1.027 \times 10^{-5} $$

Now:
$$ N^{0.6} = \dfrac{5.657 \times 10^{-4}}{1.027 \times 10^{-5}} = 55.08 $$

Solve for $N$:
$$ N = (55.08)^{1/0.6} = (55.08)^{1.667} $$

Using logarithms:
$$ \log_{10} N = 1.667 \times \log_{10}(55.08) = 1.667 \times 1.7411 = 2.903 $$
$$ N = 10^{2.903} = 800 \; \text{rev/s} \approx 48{,}000 \; \text{rpm} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Always convert droplet and particle sizes to **metres** before substituting in SI formulas. Mixing µm and m is the #1 source of errors.
> 2. **Forgetting the dry-basis moisture:** Many students compute powder rate without subtracting residual moisture. The KTU scheme allocates **2 marks** for the correct moisture correction.
> 3. **Using the wrong humidity ratio:** Be precise — $W$ is in **kg water per kg dry air**, not relative humidity (%).
> 4. **Skipping the "Show Understanding" line:** A 14-mark question carries **3 – 4 marks** for explanation, not just calculation. Always add one paragraph on *why* a co-current design is used or *why* outlet temperature drops when feed flow increases.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert N from rev/s to rpm** (or vice-versa) — the answer should be reported in **rev/s** if the formula uses $N$ in rev/s.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist (one glance before the exam):**

- 🔹 **Definition:** Spray drying = atomization + hot-gas contact + particle recovery in one vessel.
- 🔹 **Three atomizer types:** Pressure nozzle, rotary disc, two-fluid pneumatic.
- 🔹 **Two flow configurations:** Co-current (heat-sensitive products) and counter-current (heat-resistant products).
- 🔹 **Three drying stages:** Initial heating → Constant-rate (at $T_{wb}$) → Falling-rate (crust formation).
- 🔹 **Inlet air temperature** dominates evaporation rate; **outlet temperature** is the master control knob.
- 🔹 **Higher feed rate** → lower outlet temperature, larger particles, higher residual moisture, lower yield.
- 🔹 **Higher atomizer speed** → finer droplets, finer particles, faster drying, possibly lower yield (more fines).
- 🔹 **Particle size relation:** $d_p = d_d \, (\rho_f X_s / \rho_p)^{1/3}$.
- 🔹 **Ranz–Marshall equation:** $Nu = 2.0 + 0.6 \, Re^{0.5} \, Pr^{0.33}$ — use for droplet evaporation.
- 🔹 **Typical industrial thermal efficiency:** 40 – 60 % for co-current open cycle.
- 🔹 **USP ⟨786⟩** and **spray-dried amorphous dispersions** are key pharma-relevant terms.
- 🔹 **Residence time in spray dryer:** only **5 – 30 seconds** — much shorter than tray drying (hours).
- 🔹 **Cyclone + bag filter** = standard two-stage powder recovery.
- 🔹 **Critical exam skill:** Always begin a numerical answer with the **mass balance** before the energy balance.
- 🔹 **Critical exam skill:** Always state **assumptions** (steady-state, no heat loss, datum temperature).
- 🔹 **Critical exam skill:** Always report the answer in **consistent units** and clearly label each sub-part.
- 🔹 **Critical exam skill:** Add a **"Show Understanding"** paragraph to claim the explanation marks.

> 🎯 **Final Examiner Wisdom:** A KTU 14-mark question rewards the *process* (assumptions → balance → substitution → arithmetic → interpretation) more than the *final number*. Method marks are 4–6; the final number is worth 1–2 marks only. **Show your work; tell the story of the dryer.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blanching of Vegetables with and without Acid Pre-Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - Processing of Cereals, Fruits and Vegetables Laboratory
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Blanching of Vegetables with and without Acid Pre-Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Blanching** is a thermal pre-treatment process in food processing wherein vegetables (or fruits) are briefly subjected to hot water, steam, or hot air at temperatures typically between **75 °C and 95 °C** for a short, well-defined residence time (usually **1–10 minutes**, depending on the commodity size and variety), with the primary objectives of:

1. **Inactivating deteriorative enzymes** — chiefly **peroxidase (POD)**, **catalase (CAT)**, **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)**, and **lipoxygenase (LOX)**.
2. **Fixing natural pigments** (chlorophyll, carotenoids, anthocyanins) to retain colour.
3. **Removing intercellular gases**, thereby reducing oxidative degradation.
4. **Wilting and softening tissue** to facilitate subsequent unit operations like drying, freezing, canning, and packaging.
5. **Reducing microbial load** as a surface sanitisation step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acid Pre-Treatment (Acid Blanching)** is a modification of conventional blanching in which the blanching medium (water) is acidified with food-grade organic acids — commonly **citric acid (0.1–1.0 % w/v)**, **ascorbic acid (0.05–0.5 % w/v)**, or **acetic acid** — to suppress oxidation reactions, stabilise colour (prevent enzymatic browning), and reduce leaching of water-soluble nutrients.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you cut open an apple and leave it on the counter — within minutes it turns brown (oxidation). The same thing happens invisibly inside vegetables at the cellular level, driven by enzymes. **Blanching is like giving the vegetable a controlled, short, hot shower** that "switches off" these enzymes (denatures them) without cooking the vegetable fully. The acid pre-treatment is analogous to **squeezing lemon juice on the apple** before exposure — the acid lowers the pH, slowing the enzymes down even more, so the colour and vitamins stay locked in.

In industrial food lines, blanching is the **"reset button"** between raw harvest and preservation operations — every downstream process (IQF freezing, spray drying, canning, dehydration) works far better on blanched material.

### 1.3 Standard Process Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Water blanching temperature | **85 – 100 °C** | IFT Minimum Processing Guidelines |
| Steam blanching temperature | **95 – 100 °C** | USDA-FSIS |
| Residence time | **1 – 10 min** | Commodity dependent |
| Acid concentration (citric) | **0.5 – 1.0 % (w/v)** | FPO / Codex |
| Cold-water cooling | **≤ 4 °C**, 2–3 min | Stops residual cooking |
| Enzyme inactivation endpoint | **Negative POD test** | Industry gold standard |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU lab curriculum, **Peroxidase (POD) inactivation** is conventionally used as the marker enzyme because it is the **most heat-stable** among vegetable enzymes. A negative POD test is taken as proof that all other less-stable enzymes have also been inactivated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Heat transfer to tissue:** The hot medium (water/steam) transfers sensible heat to the vegetable surface and then to its interior by conduction. Surface temperature rises rapidly to the medium temperature.
2. **Protein denaturation:** At temperatures above ~70 °C, the hydrogen and hydrophobic bonds stabilising enzyme tertiary structure break, causing **irreversible denaturation** and loss of catalytic activity.
3. **Cell membrane permeabilisation:** Pectinolytic enzymes (pectin methylesterase) are partially inactivated, and intercellular air expands and escapes — tissue becomes more permeable.
4. **Acid action (in pre-treated samples):** H⁺ ions from the organic acid lower the medium pH to **~2.5–3.5**, further destabilising enzyme active sites (which typically have an optimum at pH 6–7). The acid also **chelates pro-oxidant metal ions** (Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺) that catalyse lipid and pigment oxidation.
5. **Moisture absorption / leaching:** Soluble sugars, vitamins (especially vitamin C / ascorbic acid), and minerals diffuse out into the water — this is a major *undesirable* effect controlled by short contact times.
6. **Cooling:** Immediate immersion in cold water (≤ 4 °C) halts residual heat penetration and prevents over-blanching (which causes mushy texture, nutrient loss, and cooked flavour).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the **high-yield equations** that KTU examiners expect you to write and apply for this experiment. Memorise the symbols and units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | $W_1$ = initial mass, $W_2$ = oven-dried mass |
| 2 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_2} \times 100$ | % | Used in drying kinetics |
| 3 | Weight loss due to blanching | $WL = \dfrac{M_0 - M_b}{M_0} \times 100$ | % | $M_0$ = initial, $M_b$ = after blanching |
| 4 | Volume shrinkage | $VS = \dfrac{V_0 - V_b}{V_0} \times 100$ | % | By water displacement |
| 5 | Rehydration ratio | $RR = \dfrac{W_r}{W_d}$ | — | $W_r$ = rehydrated, $W_d$ = dehydrated mass |
| 6 | Rehydration coefficient | $RC = \dfrac{W_r \times 100}{W_d \times t_{max}}$ | %/min | KTU favourite |
| 7 | Texture (TPA hardness) | $H = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A}$ | N or N/mm² | From texture profile analyser |
| 8 | Colour change (ΔE) | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L_0^* - L^*)^2 + (a_0^* - a^*)^2 + (b_0^* - b^*)^2}$ | — | CIE-Lab system |
| 9 | Ascorbic acid retention | $R_{AA} = \dfrac{C_f}{C_i} \times 100$ | % | $C_i$, $C_f$ = initial & final |
| 10 | Peroxidase residual activity | $RA_{POD} = \dfrac{A_t}{A_0} \times 100$ | % | Guaiacol method |
| 11 | Heat transfer (Newton's law) | $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | $h$ = convective HTC |
| 12 | Thermal death kinetics | $\log \left( \dfrac{N}{N_0} \right) = -\dfrac{t}{D}$ | — | Decimal reduction time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical — KTU Valuation:** For absolute-value notations inside formulas (e.g. $\vert T_s - T_\infty \vert$), always use `\vert` rather than the bare vertical pipe `|` to keep the markdown table rendering intact.

### 2.3 Why This Matters in Food Engineering

- **Industrial relevance:** Blanching is the gateway step in **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing)**, **spray and drum drying** of vegetables, **retort canning**, and **osmotic dehydration**.
- **Quality control:** Wrong blanching conditions cause catastrophic quality defects — under-blanched vegetables blacken during frozen storage due to residual PPO, while over-blanching produces "cooked" flavour and 30–50 % vitamin C loss.
- **Energy economics:** Blanching is energy-intensive; acid pre-treatment allows *shorter* blanching times with *equal or better* enzyme inactivation — a key cost-saving lever.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Weight Loss Percentage During Blanching

The **weight loss** after blanching is a direct measure of the moisture and soluble-solid migration from the cut tissue into the blanching medium. The KTU derivation is shown below.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } M_0 &= \text{initial mass of the vegetable sample (g)} \\
M_b &= \text{mass of the vegetable immediately after blanching and draining (g)} \\
\text{Mass lost to medium} &= M_0 - M_b \\
\text{Weight loss fraction} &= \dfrac{M_0 - M_b}{M_0} \\
\text{Weight loss percentage} &= \dfrac{M_0 - M_b}{M_0} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A higher percentage means more leaching of water-soluble sugars, vitamins, and minerals — an undesirable outcome that acid pre-treatment aims to reduce.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Moisture & Weight Loss Calculation

**Given:** A 200 g sample of **carrot dice** is blanched in boiling water for 3 minutes. After draining and surface-blotting, the mass is 192 g. The sample is then oven-dried at 105 °C to a constant mass of **28 g**.

**(a) Initial moisture content (wet basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{wb} &= \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{200 - 28}{200} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{172}{200} \times 100 \\
&= 86.00 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Weight loss due to blanching:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WL &= \dfrac{M_0 - M_b}{M_0} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{200 - 192}{200} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{8}{200} \times 100 \\
&= 4.00 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Moisture content of blanched sample (wet basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{wb,\;blanched} &= \dfrac{192 - 28}{192} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{164}{192} \times 100 \\
&= 85.42 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion to remember:** Even though moisture % looks similar, the *absolute water content dropped* (from 172 g to 164 g) and ~8 g of solutes were leached out. The acid pre-treatment is expected to reduce this WL value by 30–60 %.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — CIE-Lab Colour Difference (ΔE)

**Given:** Colour readings (L*, a*, b*) for raw carrot dice are **(58.4, 32.1, 41.5)**. After water blanching the values are **(55.6, 28.7, 38.9)**. After 0.5 % citric-acid blanching they are **(57.9, 31.6, 41.0)**. Compute ΔE for both treatments relative to raw.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{\text{water}} &= \sqrt{(58.4-55.6)^2 + (32.1-28.7)^2 + (41.5-38.9)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(2.8)^2 + (3.4)^2 + (2.6)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{7.84 + 11.56 + 6.76} \\
&= \sqrt{26.16} \\
&= 5.11
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{\text{acid}} &= \sqrt{(58.4-57.9)^2 + (32.1-31.6)^2 + (41.5-41.0)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25} \\
&= \sqrt{0.75} \\
&= 0.87
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Interpretation threshold: } \Delta E < 1 &\Rightarrow \text{ colour difference imperceptible to human eye} \\
1 \le \Delta E \le 3 &\Rightarrow \text{ only an experienced observer notices} \\
\Delta E > 3 &\Rightarrow \text{ noticeable difference}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** Acid pre-treatment gave ΔE = 0.87 (< 1) — colour is essentially **preserved**; water blanching gave ΔE = 5.11 — colour shift is **clearly visible**. This is the single most compelling argument for using acid pre-treatment, and KTU examiners love this numerical comparison.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Expected Effects

| Quality Parameter | Without Acid (Water Blanching) | With Acid Pre-Treatment (0.5 % Citric) | Direction of Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peroxidase inactivation | Achieved at 3–5 min | Achieved at 2–3 min | ↓ time, ↓ energy |
| Weight loss | 4 – 8 % | 1.5 – 4 % | ↓ leaching |
| Colour retention (ΔE) | ΔE = 3 – 6 | ΔE < 1.5 | ↑ retention |
| Ascorbic acid retention | 60 – 75 % | 80 – 95 % | ↑ retention |
| Texture (firmness) | Softens significantly | Better retention | ↑ firmness |
| Microbial load | Reduced | Further reduced (acid) | ↑ safety |
| pH of product | ~ 6.0 (neutral) | ~ 4.5 – 5.0 | Acidified |

### 3.5 Enzyme Kinetics During Blanching (D-value Approach)

Peroxidase inactivation follows **first-order thermal death kinetics**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(t) &= N_0 \cdot 10^{-\,t/D_T} \\
\log_{10} N(t) &= \log_{10} N_0 - \dfrac{t}{D_T} \\
\text{where } D_T &= \text{decimal reduction time at temperature } T
\end{aligned}
$$

For carrot POD at 90 °C, typical $D_{90} \approx 1.2$ min. So **5 minutes** gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} \dfrac{N}{N_0} &= -\dfrac{5}{1.2} = -4.17 \\
\dfrac{N}{N_0} &= 10^{-4.17} \approx 6.76 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

i.e. **> 99.99 % inactivation** — well above the 5-log reduction recommended by Codex for blanching validation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — Laboratory Blanching Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Vegetable Procurement] --> B[Sorting and Washing]
    B --> C[Peeling and Cutting Uniform Size 1 cm]
    C --> D[Sample Grouping: Control vs Acid Treated]
    D --> E1[Control Group: Direct Water Blanching]
    D --> E2[Acid Group: 0.5 percent Citric Acid Dip for 10 min]
    E1 --> F1[Immersion in Boiling Water 95 deg C, 3 min]
    E2 --> F2[Immersion in Acidified Boiling Water 95 deg C, 3 min]
    F1 --> G1[Immediate Cold Shock in Ice Water 4 deg C]
    F2 --> G2[Immediate Cold Shock in Ice Water 4 deg C]
    G1 --> H1[Draining and Surface Blotting]
    G2 --> H2[Draining and Surface Blotting]
    H1 --> I1[Analyses: Weight Loss, Moisture, Colour, Texture, POD]
    H2 --> I2[Analyses: Weight Loss, Moisture, Colour, Texture, POD]
    I1 --> J[Tabulation and Statistical Comparison]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K[Inference: Acid Treatment Preserves Quality]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Functional Interactions in Blanching

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HeatSrc[Heat Source: Boiler or Hot Plate] --> Med[Blanching Medium]
    Med1[Plain Water] --> Med[Blanching Medium]
    Med2[Acidified Water 0.5 percent Citric] --> Med[Blanching Medium]
    Med --> Tissue[Vegetable Tissue]
    Tissue --> E1[Enzyme Inactivation: POD, PPO, CAT]
    Tissue --> E2[Pigment Stabilisation: Chlorophyll, Carotene]
    Tissue --> E3[Gas Expulsion: Intercellular O2 Removed]
    Tissue --> E4[Microbial Reduction]
    Med --> N1[Nutrient Leaching: Vitamin C, Sugars, Minerals]
    Med --> N2[Acid Chelation: Fe and Cu Ions Sequestered]
    E1 --> Q[Quality Output]
    E2 --> Q
    E3 --> Q
    E4 --> Q
    N1 --> Q
    N2 --> Q
    Q --> R[Downstream: Drying, Freezing, Canning]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Tree — Decision Logic for Acid Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Blanching Acid] --> Q1{Target Problem}
    Q1 -->|Enzymatic browning| A1[0.1 to 0.5 percent Citric Acid]
    Q1 -->|Vitamin C loss| A2[0.1 percent Ascorbic Acid]
    Q1 -->|Microbial control| A3[1 percent Acetic or Lactic Acid]
    Q1 -->|Texture firmness| A4[0.5 percent Calcium Chloride + Citric]
    A1 --> End[Validated Pre-Treatment Solution]
    A2 --> End
    A3 --> End
    A4 --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation aid:** Each block maps a single functional responsibility. In the KTU viva, you may be asked "Why is the acid group immersed *after* the dip and not directly into plain water?" — the answer is that **pre-soaking in cold acid** allows tissue saturation and acid penetration *before* thermal exposure, so the pH-buffer effect is maximised at the moment of enzyme denaturation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Define blanching. List the four primary objectives of blanching in vegetable processing.*

**Model Answer:**
Blanching is a short-duration thermal pre-treatment in which vegetables are heated in hot water (85–100 °C) or steam for 1–10 minutes.
The four primary objectives are:
1. Inactivation of deteriorative enzymes (peroxidase, polyphenol oxidase, catalase, lipoxygenase).
2. Fixation of natural pigments to retain colour.
3. Removal of intercellular gases and reduction of oxidative reactions.
4. Reduction of surface microbial load and conditioning of tissue for further processing.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for objectives.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
*Why is peroxidase (POD) used as the indicator enzyme for blanching validation rather than catalase or PPO?*

**Model Answer:**
POD is the most heat-stable enzyme among those commonly found in vegetables — its **D-value at 90 °C is ~ 1.2 minutes**, considerably higher than catalase or polyphenol oxidase. Therefore, **when POD activity is undetectable, all other less heat-stable enzymes are guaranteed to be inactivated**. This makes POD a conservative, fail-safe indicator, and a negative POD test (using guaiacol/H₂O₂) is the industry and Codex standard for confirming adequate blanching.

*(3 marks — 2 for reasoning, 1 for naming the test.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Module Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Important:** Under KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Module-2 question offers an internal choice between Q (a) and Q (b) of equal marks.

#### ▸ Question A — 14 Marks (Comprehensive Treatment)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, Choice A]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe, with a labelled flow diagram, the laboratory procedure for blanching of vegetables **with and without acid pre-treatment**. State the role of each pre-treatment reagent.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 250 g sample of **green pea** is blanched in plain water (95 °C, 3 min) and another 250 g sample in 0.5 % citric acid solution (95 °C, 3 min). After blanching, the masses are **238 g** and **246 g** respectively. Calculate the weight loss in each case and comment on the effect of acid pre-treatment on nutrient leaching.

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**
