1. **Procurement and preparation:** Procure fresh vegetables (carrot, beans, peas, cauliflower). Wash, peel and cut into uniform 1 cm pieces. *(1 mark)*
2. **Grouping:** Divide into two equal lots — Group I (control) and Group II (acid-treated). *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Acid pre-treatment (Group II only):** Immerse Group II in **0.5 % (w/v) citric acid solution at room temperature for 10 minutes** to allow acid penetration. Group I is kept in plain water for the same duration. *(1 mark)*
4. **Blanching:** Drain the pre-treatment solution. Immerse both groups in **boiling water at 95 °C for 3 minutes** (time as per commodity). Maintain a 1 : 10 vegetable-to-water ratio to prevent temperature drop. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Cold shock:** Immediately transfer both groups into **ice-cold water (≤ 4 °C) for 2–3 minutes** to stop residual cooking. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Draining and analyses:** Drain, surface-blot, and analyse for weight loss, moisture content, colour (L\*a\*b\*), texture, and POD activity. *(1 mark)*
7. **Role of reagents:** Citric acid lowers pH (~ 3) — denatures enzyme active sites; chelates Fe²⁺/Cu²⁺ — prevents oxidation; reinforces cell-wall pectin — maintains texture. *(0.5 mark)*

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

**Group I — Plain water blanching:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WL_I &= \dfrac{250 - 238}{250} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{12}{250} \times 100 \\
&= 4.80 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Group II — Acid pre-treated blanching:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WL_{II} &= \dfrac{250 - 246}{250} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{4}{250} \times 100 \\
&= 1.60 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage reduction in weight loss due to acid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction} &= \dfrac{4.80 - 1.60}{4.80} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{3.20}{4.80} \times 100 \\
&= 66.67 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Examiner-grade inference (1 mark):** Acid pre-treatment **reduced weight loss by ~ 66.7 %**, indicating significantly lower leaching of water-soluble solids, sugars, and vitamins. The lower pH (~ 3) at the cell surface denatures surface pectinolytic enzymes faster, retarding tissue breakdown, and the shorter effective time-at-temperature preserves nutrients — confirming the engineering advantage of acid pre-treatment for downstream drying/freezing operations.

---

#### ▸ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, Choice B]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the principle of enzyme inactivation by heat in blanching. Why is **peroxidase (POD)** chosen as the marker enzyme? Describe the **guaiacol test** for POD detection.

**(b) [7 marks]** The following CIE-Lab values were recorded for carrot dice:

| Sample | L* | a* | b* |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw (control) | 60.2 | 33.5 | 42.0 |
| Water blanched (95 °C, 4 min) | 56.1 | 29.4 | 38.5 |
| Acid blanched (0.5 % citric, 95 °C, 4 min) | 59.4 | 32.8 | 41.3 |

Compute ΔE for both treatments and explain which method is superior for colour retention.

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Principle & POD test (7 marks):**
- **Thermal denaturation of enzymes (3 marks):** Enzymes are globular proteins whose tertiary structure is stabilised by hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and salt bridges. When tissue is heated above ~ 70 °C, the kinetic energy exceeds these weak bonds, causing **irreversible unfolding (denaturation)**. The active site loses its specific 3D geometry, and catalytic activity is permanently lost.
- **Why POD is the marker (2 marks):** POD has the highest thermal stability among vegetable enzymes ($D_{90} \approx 1.2$ min). Inactivation of POD implies inactivation of all less-stable enzymes (PPO, CAT, LOX) — providing a **conservative, fail-safe endpoint**. It is also easily detected via colorimetric assay.
- **Guaiacol test (2 marks): Mix 1 mL of blanched vegetable extract + 1 mL of 1 % guaiacol + 1 mL of 0.3 % H₂O₂. Observe for brown colour within 3 minutes. Development of brown colour = positive POD (incomplete blanching). No colour change = negative POD = adequate blanching.**

**(b) ΔE calculations (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{\text{water}} &= \sqrt{(60.2-56.1)^2 + (33.5-29.4)^2 + (42.0-38.5)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(4.1)^2 + (4.1)^2 + (3.5)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{16.81 + 16.81 + 12.25} \\
&= \sqrt{45.87} \\
&= 6.77
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{\text{acid}} &= \sqrt{(60.2-59.4)^2 + (33.5-32.8)^2 + (42.0-41.3)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(0.8)^2 + (0.7)^2 + (0.7)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.64 + 0.49 + 0.49} \\
&= \sqrt{1.62} \\
&= 1.27
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Examiner-grade inference (1 mark):** $\Delta E_{\text{water}} = 6.77$ — clearly perceptible colour shift (carotenoid loss and leaching). $\Delta E_{\text{acid}} = 1.27$ — only an expert observer would notice the change. **Acid pre-treatment is decisively superior** for colour retention, primarily because the acidic pH suppresses oxidation of carotenoids and prevents browning reactions catalysed by residual PPO.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes that cost 1–2 marks each in this experiment:**
> 1. **Wrong formula for moisture** — confusing wet-basis and dry-basis. Use `Mwb` if mass is reported per 100 g of *original* sample; use `Mdb` for drying-rate calculations. Mixing them up = -1 mark.
> 2. **Omitting the unit "%"** in intermediate steps — the examiner *will* deduct a half-mark even if your arithmetic is right.
> 3. **Not stating the ΔE threshold** (1, 3, 5) — KTU expects the three-tier human-eye interpretation to be mentioned explicitly. Bare numbers lose marks.
> 4. **Skipping the cold-shock step** in procedure answers — many students forget to write "immediate immersion in ice water" and lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **No reagent concentration** in the procedure — write "0.5 % (w/v) citric acid", not just "citric acid". Generic answers = average scores.
> 6. **Confusing "blanching" with "pasteurisation"** — pasteurisation targets *microbes*; blanching targets *enzymes* (and incidentally reduces microbes). The objective is enzymes, full stop.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ⭐ **Blanching = short thermal pre-treatment (75–100 °C, 1–10 min)**, primarily to **inactivate enzymes** before further processing.
- ⭐ **POD is the marker enzyme** — most heat-stable; a negative guaiacol test confirms adequate blanching.
- ⭐ **Acid pre-treatment** uses **0.1–1 % citric / ascorbic acid** to lower pH, chelate pro-oxidant metals, and reduce nutrient leaching.
- ⭐ **Weight loss formula:** $WL = \dfrac{M_0 - M_b}{M_0} \times 100$ — acid treatment reduces this by 30–70 %.
- ⭐ **Moisture (wb):** $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$.
- ⭐ **Colour difference (ΔE):** $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ — thresholds 1, 3, 5.
- ⭐ **Always cold-shock** the blanched material in ice water (≤ 4 °C) to halt residual cooking.
- ⭐ **Vegetable-to-water ratio ≥ 1:10** to prevent temperature drop during blanching.
- ⭐ **ΔE < 1** = imperceptible; **1–3** = expert only; **> 3** = noticeable.
- ⭐ **Acid blanching advantages** = less weight loss, better colour, higher vitamin C retention, firmer texture, lower microbial count.
- ⭐ **D-value kinetics:** $\log(N/N_0) = -t/D_T$ — a 5-log reduction is the Codex standard.
- ⭐ **Common vegetables used in lab:** carrot, green peas, beans, cauliflower, potato, spinach.
- ⭐ **Industrial relevance:** every frozen, canned, or dehydrated vegetable product is *almost always* pre-blanched.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dehydration Study of Vegetables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Dehydration Study of Vegetables # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dehydration of Vegetables** is a unit operation in Food Engineering in which moisture is removed from vegetable tissue by the application of heat under controlled conditions of temperature, humidity, and air flow, with the primary objective of reducing the **water activity ($a_w$)** below the threshold level (**$a_w < 0.6$**) required to inhibit microbial growth and enzymatic activity, thereby extending shelf life and reducing mass & volume for storage/transportation. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory module, **PCFTL507 – Module Experiment 2**, dehydration study typically refers to a **thin-layer drying experiment** where a vegetable sample (e.g., carrot, potato, onion, beetroot, or cabbage) is dried in a **tray dryer / hot air oven / fluidized bed dryer** under isothermal conditions, and the temporal loss of mass is recorded to compute drying kinetics, moisture ratio, drying rate, effective moisture diffusivity, and activation energy. ## 1.2 Synonyms and Terminology | Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **Drying** | General term for moisture removal (often implies to a low final moisture). | | **Dehydration** | Artificial drying under controlled conditions to a specific low moisture level. | | **Desiccation** | Drying using chemical desiccants. | | **Concentration** | Removal of water from liquid foods. | | **Dehydro-freezing** | Combination of drying followed by freezing. | > [!NOTE] > In strict food engineering parlance, **"drying"** and **"dehydration"** are often used interchangeably, but dehydration usually implies a more controlled and complete process with lower final moisture content. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a wet sponge. If you leave it on a table, it loses water slowly. If you place it near a heater, the water evaporates faster. Now, the **drying rate** is essentially how fast the sponge loses water. The sponge has two phases of losing water: 1. **First Phase (Constant Rate Period)**: Water on the surface evaporates easily, just like a puddle drying in the sun. The rate stays constant because the surface remains saturated with water. 2. **Second Phase (Falling Rate Period)**: As the sponge dries, water has to travel from inside to the surface (like a long journey through a maze). The rate drops because diffusion inside becomes the bottleneck. **Vegetables behave exactly like this sponge** — they have a constant rate period (free water removal) followed by one or more falling rate periods (bound water removal via internal diffusion). ## 1.4 Objectives of the Dehydration Experiment The standard KTU laboratory objectives for this experiment are: 1. To determine the **moisture content (wet basis and dry basis)** of fresh and dehydrated vegetables. 2. To plot the **drying curve** (moisture content vs. time) and **drying rate curve** (drying rate vs. moisture content). 3. To identify the **constant rate period** and **falling rate period(s)** along with the **critical moisture content ($X_c$)**. 4. To fit the drying data to **thin-layer drying models** (e.g., Newton, Page, Henderson-Pabis, Midilli-Kucuk, etc.). 5. To determine the **effective moisture diffusivity ($D_{eff}$)** using **Fick's second law of diffusion**. 6. To determine the **activation energy ($E_a$)** using the **Arrhenius equation** (when drying is carried out at multiple temperatures). 7. To evaluate the **rehydration ratio** and **shrinkage** of the dehydrated product. ## 1.5 Standard Reference Parameters > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Reference Values (must be memorised):** > - Universal gas constant: **$R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$** > - Typical safe water activity for dried foods: **$a_w < 0.6$** > - Typical safe moisture content (wet basis) for dried vegetables: **$< 8\%$ (w.b.)** > - Standard drying temperatures for vegetables: **50 °C – 80 °C** (depending on heat sensitivity) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theory of Moisture in Vegetables ### 2.1.1 Types of Moisture in Food Vegetables contain water in two principal forms: 1. **Free Moisture (Free Water)**: Water that is loosely held in the cellular pores, capillaries, and on cell surfaces. It is removable relatively easily during the constant rate period. 2. **Bound Moisture**: Water that is tightly bound to the food matrix via: - Chemical adsorption to hydrophilic groups (e.g., OH, NH) - Osmotic retention in cellular structures - Capillary condensation > [!NOTE] > The **Bound Water** is the residual moisture that cannot be removed by ordinary drying — it requires energy to break the molecular bonds. ### 2.1.2 Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC) The **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$)** is the moisture content at which the food neither gains nor loses moisture when exposed to air of a given temperature and relative humidity. $$X^* = f(T, RH)$$ The difference between the current moisture content ($X$) and the EMC ($X^*$) is the **free moisture content** that can theoretically be removed. ## 2.2 Moisture Content Representation The moisture content of a food material can be expressed in two ways: ### (a) Moisture Content on Wet Basis (MC$_w$ or $X_{wb}$) $$X_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} = \frac{m_w}{m_{total}}$$ Where: - $m_w$ = mass of water (kg) - $m_s$ = mass of dry solid (kg) - $m_{total}$ = total mass (kg) ### (b) Moisture Content on Dry Basis (MC$_d$ or $X_{db}$) $$X_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$$ ### (c) Conversion Between the Two $$X_{db} = \frac{X_{wb}}{1 - X_{wb}}$$ $$X_{wb} = \frac{X_{db}}{1 + X_{db}}$$ ## 2.3 Drying Principles — The Two-Period Theory The drying of vegetables follows the classical **two-period theory of drying**: ### 2.3.1 Constant Rate Period (CRP) - The surface of the vegetable is saturated with water. - Drying rate is governed by **external heat and mass transfer**. - The rate depends on: - Air velocity ($v$) - Air temperature ($T$) - Air humidity - Moisture removed = **Free moisture** (non-bound water). - The drying rate is essentially equal to the rate of evaporation from a free water surface. ### 2.3.2 Falling Rate Period (FRP) - The surface starts to dry as free water is depleted. - Drying rate is governed by **internal moisture diffusion** (migration of moisture from inside the vegetable to the surface). - Moisture removed = **Bound moisture**. - The drying rate continuously decreases with time/moisture content. - The **Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$)** is the transition point between CRP and FRP. - FRP can be sub-divided into: - First falling rate period - Second falling rate period (where diffusion is purely molecular) > [!IMPORTANT] > **For most vegetables, drying is dominated by the falling rate period.** The constant rate period is often very short or even absent for some vegetables (like onion and garlic) due to their low initial surface moisture. ## 2.4 Mathematical Drying Rate Equation The general drying rate is defined as: $$N = -L_s \frac{dX_{db}}{dt}$$ Where: - $N$ = drying rate (kg water / m²·h) - $L_s$ = mass of dry solid per unit drying area (kg dry solid / m²) - $\frac{dX_{db}}{dt}$ = rate of change of moisture content (db) with time (1/h) The drying rate can also be expressed in terms of heat transfer: $$N = \frac{h_t (T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_s \rho_s}$$ Where: - $h_t$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - $T_g$ = bulk gas (air) temperature (°C or K) - $T_w$ = wet-bulb temperature (°C or K) - $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of vaporisation at $T_w$ (J/kg) - $\rho_s$ = density of dry solid (kg/m³) ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $X_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s}$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | kg/kg (decimal) | | 2 | $X_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | kg water/kg dry solid | | 3 | $X_{db} = \frac{X_{wb}}{1 - X_{wb}}$ | Conversion (wb to db) | — | | 4 | $MR = \frac{X_t - X^*}{X_0 - X^*}$ | Moisture Ratio (dimensionless) | — | | 5 | $MR = \exp(-k t)$ | **Newton (Lewis) Model** | k in 1/min | | 6 | $MR = \exp(-k t^n)$ | **Page Model** | k in 1/min, n dimensionless | | 7 | $MR = a \exp(-k t)$ | **Henderson-Pabis Model** | a, k constants | | 8 | $MR = a \exp(-k t) + c t$ | **Midilli-Kucuk Model** | a, c, k constants | | 9 | $\ln(MR) = \ln(\frac{8}{\pi^2}) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}$ | Fick's diffusion model (long time) | — | | 10 | $D_{eff} = -(\text{slope}) \cdot \frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2}$ | Diffusivity from slope | m²/s | | 11 | $D_{eff} = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | **Arrhenius equation** | $D_0$ in m²/s, $E_a$ in J/mol | | 12 | $\ln(D_{eff}) = \ln(D_0) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$ | Linearised Arrhenius | slope = $-E_a/R$ | | 13 | $RR = \frac{m_{rehydrated}}{m_{dehydrated}}$ | Rehydration ratio | dimensionless | | 14 | $S (\%) = \frac{V_0 - V_d}{V_0} \times 100$ | Shrinkage percentage | % | | 15 | $N = -L_s \frac{dX_{db}}{dt}$ | Drying rate | kg/m²·h | | 16 | $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ | Universal gas constant | — | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Tip:** For long drying times, only the first term of the Fickian infinite series is significant. This is the standard assumption used in deriving the **slope method** for calculating $D_{eff}$. ## 2.6 Thin-Layer Drying Models Thin-layer drying models describe how moisture content of a single layer of the material changes with time under constant drying conditions. The most commonly used models in the KTU syllabus are: ### 2.6.1 Newton (Lewis) Model $$MR = \exp(-k t)$$ - Simplest model; one parameter $k$ (drying constant) - Assumes drying is controlled only by external resistance ### 2.6.2 Page Model $$MR = \exp(-k t^n)$$ - Empirical modification of Newton model - Introduces a shape factor $n$ - Better fit for most agricultural products ### 2.6.3 Henderson-Pabis Model $$MR = a \exp(-k t)$$ - Two parameters $a$ and $k$ - $a$ accounts for non-ideal behaviour ### 2.6.4 Midilli-Kucuk Model $$MR = a \exp(-k t) + c t$$ - Three parameters; excellent fit for many vegetables ## 2.7 Real-World Utility The mathematical models derived in this experiment are essential for: - **Design of industrial dryers** — predicting drying time, size of equipment - **Process optimisation** — selecting the best temperature, air velocity, slice thickness - **Quality control** — preserving colour, nutrients, texture during drying - **Energy efficiency calculations** — predicting energy consumption - **Storage stability prediction** — for shelf-life of dried products --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Moisture Ratio from Mass Data During the drying experiment, we record: - $W_0$ = Initial mass of the sample (at time $t = 0$) - $W_t$ = Mass of the sample at time $t$ - $W_s$ = Mass of the bone-dry sample (after oven drying at 105 °C for 24 h, or the absolute dry mass) The instantaneous moisture content (dry basis) at time $t$ is: $$X_t = \frac{W_t - W_s}{W_s}$$ At the start ($t = 0$): $$X_0 = \frac{W_0 - W_s}{W_s}$$ The **Moisture Ratio (MR)** is defined as the ratio of the free moisture at any time $t$ to the initial free moisture. Assuming the equilibrium moisture content $X^*$ is small and often approximated as zero for hot air drying: $$MR = \frac{X_t - X^*}{X_0 - X^*}$$ For simplification, when $X^*$ is negligibly small: $$MR = \frac{X_t}{X_0} = \frac{W_t - W_s}{W_0 - W_s}$$ This is the formula directly used to plot **MR vs. time** and fit the various thin-layer drying models. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fick's Second Law of Diffusion for Thin-Layer Drying Fick's second law for one-dimensional moisture diffusion in an infinite slab (slice of vegetable) is: $$\frac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \frac{\partial^2 X}{\partial z^2}$$ With the following boundary and initial conditions: - IC: $X(z, 0) = X_0$ for $-L < z < L$ - BC1: $X(L, t) = X_e$ (or $X^*$) - BC2: $X(-L, t) = X_e$ - Symmetry: $\frac{\partial X}{\partial z} = 0$ at $z = 0$ Where $2L$ is the thickness of the slab. The analytical solution, using separation of variables, is: $$MR = \frac{X_t - X^*}{X_0 - X^*} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}\right)$$ For **long drying times**, the higher-order terms ($n \geq 1$) become negligibly small compared to the first term ($n = 0$). Keeping only the first term: $$MR = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}\right)$$ Taking the natural logarithm on both sides: $$\ln(MR) = \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}$$ This is in the form $y = c + m x$ where: - $y = \ln(MR)$ - $x = t$ - $m = -\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2}$ (slope) - $c = \ln(8/\pi^2)$ (intercept) Rearranging for the **effective moisture diffusivity**: $$D_{eff} = -\frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2} \cdot (\text{slope of } \ln(MR) \text{ vs. } t)$$ This is the famous **"slope method"** widely used in KTU practical exams. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Arrhenius Equation for Activation Energy The temperature dependence of $D_{eff}$ is given by the Arrhenius equation: $$D_{eff} = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right)$$ Where: - $D_0$ = pre-exponential factor (m²/s) - $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol or kJ/mol) - $R$ = universal gas constant = 8.314 J/(mol·K) - $T$ = absolute drying temperature (K) Taking the natural logarithm: $$\ln(D_{eff}) = \ln(D_0) - \frac{E_a}{R T}$$ This is a linear equation in the form $y = b + m x$ where: - $y = \ln(D_{eff})$ - $x = 1/T$ (in K⁻¹) - $m = -\frac{E_a}{R}$ (slope) - $b = \ln(D_0)$ (intercept) By plotting $\ln(D_{eff})$ vs. $1/T$ (a straight line), the **activation energy** is calculated from the slope: $$E_a = -(\text{slope}) \times R = -(\text{slope}) \times 8.314 \text{ J/mol}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Drying Rate from Mass Data The drying rate is defined as the change in moisture content per unit time per unit dry mass: $$N_t = \frac{m_{t} - m_{t+\Delta t}}{\Delta t \cdot A_{surface}}$$ For a single tray of known drying area and dry solid mass: $$N_t = -L_s \frac{dX}{dt} \approx -L_s \cdot \frac{X_{t+\Delta t} - X_t}{\Delta t}$$ Where $L_s = m_s / A$, the dry solid mass per unit drying area. ## 3.5 Worked-Out Sample Calculation (Numerical) > [!NOTE] > This is a complete KTU-style numerical problem often asked in the 14-mark question. **Problem:** A drying experiment on carrot cubes is performed in a tray dryer at 60 °C. The initial mass of the sample is 200 g. After complete drying (bone-dry), the mass is 35 g. The mass readings at different time intervals are given below: | Time (min) | Mass (g) | | :--- | :--- | | 0 | 200.0 | | 30 | 178.5 | | 60 | 158.3 | | 90 | 140.2 | | 120 | 124.0 | | 150 | 109.5 | | 180 | 96.8 | | 240 | 76.0 | | 300 | 60.5 | | 360 | 49.2 | | 420 | 41.0 | | 480 | 35.8 | | ∞ (dry) | 35.0 | **Step 1: Calculate the initial moisture content (db)** $$X_0 = \frac{W_0 - W_s}{W_s} = \frac{200.0 - 35.0}{35.0} = \frac{165.0}{35.0} = 4.714 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$ **Step 2: Calculate moisture content at each time (db)** $$X_t = \frac{W_t - 35.0}{35.0}$$ For example, at $t = 30$ min: $$X_{30} = \frac{178.5 - 35.0}{35.0} = \frac{143.5}{35.0} = 4.100$$ **Step 3: Calculate Moisture Ratio (assuming $X^* = 0$)** $$MR = \frac{X_t}{X_0} = \frac{W_t - W_s}{W_0 - W_s}$$ For example, at $t = 30$ min: $$MR = \frac{178.5 - 35.0}{200.0 - 35.0} = \frac{143.5}{165.0} = 0.870$$ **Step 4: Tabulate the values** | t (min) | $W_t$ (g) | $X_t$ (db) | MR | ln(MR) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 0 | 200.0 | 4.714 | 1.000 | 0.000 | | 30 | 178.5 | 4.100 | 0.870 | -0.1393 | | 60 | 158.3 | 3.523 | 0.748 | -0.2909 | | 90 | 140.2 | 3.006 | 0.638 | -0.4493 | | 120 | 124.0 | 2.543 | 0.539 | -0.6174 | | 150 | 109.5 | 2.129 | 0.452 | -0.7951 | | 180 | 96.8 | 1.766 | 0.375 | -0.9810 | | 240 | 76.0 | 1.171 | 0.248 | -1.3936 | | 300 | 60.5 | 0.729 | 0.155 | -1.8672 | | 360 | 49.2 | 0.406 | 0.086 | -2.4530 | | 420 | 41.0 | 0.171 | 0.036 | -3.3158 | | 480 | 35.8 | 0.023 | 0.005 | -5.2983 | **Step 5: Fit the Page Model** Plot $\ln(-\ln(MR))$ vs. $\ln(t)$. The Page model is: $$MR = \exp(-k t^n) \Rightarrow \ln(MR) = -k t^n \Rightarrow -\ln(MR) = k t^n$$ $$\ln(-\ln(MR)) = \ln(k) + n \ln(t)$$ From the linear plot, the slope gives $n$ and intercept gives $\ln(k)$. **Step 6: Calculate Effective Moisture Diffusivity** Plot $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ for the falling rate period (last few data points). A straight line of slope $m$ gives: $$D_{eff} = -\frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2} \cdot m$$ If the slope of the ln(MR) vs t plot (falling rate) = $-0.0051$ min⁻¹ and $2L = 5$ mm (so $L = 2.5$ mm = 0.0025 m): $$D_{eff} = -\frac{4 \times (0.0025)^2}{\pi^2} \times (-0.0051 \times 60) \text{ s}^{-1}$$ $$D_{eff} = -\frac{4 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696} \times (-0.306)$$ $$D_{eff} = 7.74 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ (This is in the typical range of $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-11}$ m²/s for vegetables.) ## 3.6 Activation Energy Calculation (Multi-Temperature Drying) If the same drying experiment is repeated at multiple temperatures, the following data is obtained: | Temperature (°C) | Temperature (K) | $D_{eff}$ (m²/s) | $1/T$ (K⁻¹) | $\ln(D_{eff})$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 50 | 323.15 | $4.20 \times 10^{-8}$ | $3.094 \times 10^{-3}$ | -17.586 | | 60 | 333.15 | $6.50 \times 10^{-8}$ | $3.002 \times 10^{-3}$ | -16.548 | | 70 | 343.15 | $9.85 \times 10^{-8}$ | $2.914 \times 10^{-3}$ | -16.131 | | 80 | 353.15 | $1.45 \times 10^{-7}$ | $2.832 \times 10^{-3}$ | -15.747 | From the slope of $\ln(D_{eff})$ vs. $1/T$: $$\text{Slope} = -5462 \text{ K}$$ $$E_a = -(\text{slope}) \times R = -(-5462) \times 8.314 = 45411 \text{ J/mol} = 45.41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ This value is typical for vegetables (range: **20–80 kJ/mol**). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Laboratory Tray Dryer Setup The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the typical laboratory tray dryer setup used in this KTU Module Experiment 2. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Heating Element
Electric Heater 2 to 4 kW] --> B[Air Stream
Hot Air Inlet] B --> C[Blower or Fan
Axial Fan 0.5 HP] C --> D[Plenum Chamber
Air Distribution] D --> E[Weighing Tray
with Vegetable Sample] E --> F[Digital Balance
External or Inline] F --> G[Data Logger or PC
Time and Mass Recording] E --> H[Exhaust Air
Outlet with Damper] H --> I[Humidity Sensor
RH Transmitter] I --> J[Temperature Sensor
Thermocouple T Type] J --> K[PID Controller
Temperature Control] K --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Working Principle:** The heating element raises the air temperature; the blower pushes this hot air through the plenum; the air passes over the thin layer of vegetables on the tray, picks up moisture, and is exhausted. The temperature and humidity sensors in the exhaust loop provide feedback to the PID controller to maintain a constant drying temperature. ## 4.2 Process Flow — Experimental Procedure for Dehydration Study ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Select Fresh Vegetable
Carrot or Potato or Onion] --> S2[Step 2: Wash Peel and Cut
Uniform Cubes or Slices 3 to 5 mm] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Record Initial Mass W0
Using Digital Balance] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Pre-heat the Tray Dryer
to Set Temperature 50 to 80 C] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Place Sample in Tray
Spread as Thin Layer] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Record Mass Every 15 or 30 min
Wt vs Time Tabulation] S6 --> S7{Qualitative Check
Constant Weight} S7 -- No --> S6 S7 -- Yes --> S8[Step 7: Determine Bone Dry Mass Ws
105 C for 24 h in Hot Air Oven] S8 --> S9[Step 8: Calculate MC db and MR
for All Time Intervals] S9 --> S10[Step 9: Plot Drying Curves
Xdb vs t, N vs X, MR vs t, lnMR vs t] S10 --> S11[Step 10: Fit Thin Layer Models
Newton Page Henderson-Pabis] S11 --> S12[Step 11: Calculate Deff and Ea
Using Ficks Law and Arrhenius] S12 --> S13[Step 12: Conduct Rehydration Test
Soak in Water at 25 C for 2 h] S13 --> S14[Step 13: Calculate Rehydration Ratio and Shrinkage] S14 --> R[Result and Discussion] ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Drying Rate Curve The following Mermaid flowchart simulates the conceptual depiction of the **drying rate curve** and the corresponding **moisture curve** as a state transition diagram. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A0["Initial Wetting Zone X greater than X0"] direction TB A0a[Fresh Vegetable with Initial Moisture X0] end subgraph A1["Constant Rate Period CRP"] direction TB A1a[Surface Saturated with Free Water] --> A1b[Rate N is Constant] --> A1c[Governed by External Heat and Mass Transfer] end subgraph A2["First Critical Point"] direction TB A2a[Moisture Reaches Critical Value Xc] --> A2b[Transition from CRP to FRP] end subgraph A3["Falling Rate Period FRP"] direction TB A3a[Internal Diffusion Controls] --> A3b[Rate Decreases Linearly] --> A3c[Second Falling Rate May Follow] end subgraph A4["Equilibrium Zone"] direction TB A4a[Moisture Approaches Equilibrium X asterisk] --> A4b[Drying Stops] end A0 --> A1 A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 ``` ## 4.4 Apparatus Used in the Experiment | # | Equipment | Specification | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Tray Dryer | 0.5 m² tray area, 2 kW heater | Hot air drying chamber | | 2 | Hot Air Oven | 105 °C, ±1 °C accuracy | Determination of bone-dry mass | | 3 | Digital Balance | 0.001 g precision | Mass measurement | | 4 | Vernier Caliper | 0.01 mm precision | Thickness measurement of slices | | 5 | Thermocouple / RTD | Type T / Pt-100 | Temperature monitoring | | 6 | Hygrometer | 0–100 % RH | Humidity measurement | | 7 | Stop Watch | 0.1 s | Time measurement | | 8 | Desiccator | With silica gel | Cooling samples without moisture re-adsorption | | 9 | Knife and Cutting Board | — | Sample preparation | | 10 | Beakers | 250 mL | Rehydration test | --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]** **Define moisture content of a food material. Distinguish between moisture content on wet basis and dry basis. Mention the standard reference value for safe moisture content of dried vegetables.** **Model Answer:** > **Moisture content** of a food material is defined as the amount of water present in the material, expressed either as a percentage or a fraction of the total mass or dry mass. **Wet Basis (wb):** Moisture expressed as a fraction of the total (wet) mass of the material. $$X_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} = \frac{m_w}{m_{total}}$$ **Dry Basis (db):** Moisture expressed as a fraction of the dry solid mass of the material. $$X_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$$ The dry basis is preferred in drying calculations because it gives a true representation of the actual water present per unit dry solid, while the wet basis is used in commercial food labelling. **Standard reference:** The safe moisture content (wb) for dried vegetables is typically less than **8 %**, and the water activity should be below **0.6** to inhibit microbial growth. --- ### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B]** **What is meant by a "thin-layer drying model"? List any three commonly used thin-layer drying models and write their mathematical forms.** **Model Answer:** > A **thin-layer drying model** is a mathematical equation that describes the moisture ratio of a thin layer of food material as a function of drying time under constant external drying conditions (constant temperature, humidity, and air velocity). The three commonly used models are: **(i) Newton (Lewis) Model:** $$MR = \exp(-k t)$$ **(ii) Page Model:** $$MR = \exp(-k t^n)$$ **(iii) Henderson-Pabis Model:** $$MR = a \exp(-k t)$$ Where MR is the moisture ratio, $t$ is the drying time, and $k$, $n$, $a$ are model constants. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with a typical internal choice. We provide both alternatives below. ### **Question B1 (Option A): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **two-period theory of drying** with neat sketches. Highlight the significance of the **critical moisture content**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A drying experiment is performed on potato slices (thickness 5 mm) at 60 °C in a tray dryer. The initial mass of the sample is 250 g. The bone-dry mass is 50 g. The mass recorded after 4 hours is 95 g. Calculate: **(i)** the initial moisture content (db), **(ii)** the moisture content (db) at $t = 4$ h, **(iii)** the moisture ratio, and **(iv)** the effective moisture diffusivity if the slope of the ln(MR) vs. t plot (falling rate region) is $-0.0048$ min⁻¹. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Two-Period Theory of Drying:** The drying of hygroscopic food materials like vegetables typically follows the **two-period drying theory** proposed by Lewis, Sherwood, and others. **Period I – Constant Rate Period (CRP):** - During this initial period, the surface of the vegetable remains saturated with free water. - The drying rate is constant because the moisture transport to the surface by capillary action is fast enough to replenish the surface water. - The mechanism is governed by **external heat and mass transfer** from the air to the surface. - The drying rate depends on air velocity, temperature, and humidity but NOT on the moisture content of the solid. **Period II – Falling Rate Period (FRP):** - Once the surface moisture is depleted, the wetted surface area starts to shrink. - The drying rate falls because the rate-limiting step shifts to **internal moisture diffusion** (Fickian diffusion). - In some products, a **second falling rate period** is observed, where only bound moisture remains. - The drying rate is a strong function of the instantaneous moisture content. **Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$):** - $X_c$ is the moisture content at the **transition point** between CRP and FRP. - It depends on the material's structure, drying rate, and slice thickness. - It marks the start of internal diffusion control. - For vegetables, $X_c$ is typically in the range of 1.0 to 2.0 (db). **Schematic:** - $X_{db}$ vs. $t$ shows a steep initial linear drop (CRP), followed by an exponential approach to $X^*$ (FRP). - $N$ vs. $X_{db}$ shows a horizontal line (CRP) followed by a sloped line falling to zero (FRP), meeting at $X_c$. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - $W_0 = 250$ g, $W_s = 50$ g, $W_{240} = 95$ g - $2L = 5$ mm $\Rightarrow L = 2.5$ mm = 0.0025 m - Slope of $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ = $-0.0048$ min⁻¹ - $t = 4$ h = 240 min **(i) Initial moisture content (db):** $$X_0 = \frac{W_0 - W_s}{W_s} = \frac{250 - 50}{50} = \frac{200}{50} = 4.00 \text{ kg/kg dry solid}$$ **(ii) Moisture content at 4 h (db):** $$X_{240} = \frac{W_{240} - W_s}{W_s} = \frac{95 - 50}{50} = \frac{45}{50} = 0.90 \text{ kg/kg dry solid}$$ **(iii) Moisture Ratio (assuming $X^* = 0$):** $$MR = \frac{X_t}{X_0} = \frac{0.90}{4.00} = 0.225$$ **(iv) Effective Moisture Diffusivity:** First, convert slope to seconds: $$\text{slope} = -0.0048 \text{ min}^{-1} = -0.0048 / 60 \text{ s}^{-1} = -8.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$$ Apply Fick's equation: $$D_{eff} = -\frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2} \cdot (\text{slope})$$ $$D_{eff} = -\frac{4 \times (0.0025)^2}{\pi^2} \times (-8.0 \times 10^{-5})$$ $$D_{eff} = -\frac{4 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696} \times (-8.0 \times 10^{-5})$$ $$D_{eff} = 2.533 \times 10^{-6} \times 8.0 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$D_{eff} = 2.026 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ **Result:** $D_{eff} \approx 2.03 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, which lies in the typical range of $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-11}$ m²/s for food materials. --- ### **Question B2 (Option B): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** What is **Fick's second law of diffusion**? Derive the equation for the calculation of **effective moisture diffusivity** of a vegetable slice in a thin-layer drying experiment, considering the infinite slab geometry and the long-time approximation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **Arrhenius equation** as applied to the temperature dependence of effective moisture diffusivity. A vegetable is dried at four temperatures (50, 60, 70, 80 °C) and the corresponding $D_{eff}$ values are $5 \times 10^{-9}$, $8 \times 10^{-9}$, $1.4 \times 10^{-8}$, and $2.2 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/s respectively. Calculate the **activation energy ($E_a$)** and the **pre-exponential factor ($D_0$)**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Fick's Second Law and Diffusivity Derivation:** **Fick's second law** in one-dimensional form: $$\frac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \frac{\partial^2 X}{\partial z^2}$$ For an **infinite slab** of thickness $2L$, with initial condition $X(z,0) = X_0$ and boundary conditions $X(\pm L, t) = X^*$, the analytical solution is: $$MR = \frac{X_t - X^*}{X_0 - X^*} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}\right)$$ **Long-time approximation:** For large $t$, the higher terms ($n \geq 1$) become negligible. Retaining only $n = 0$: $$MR = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}\right)$$ Taking the natural log: $$\ln(MR) = \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}$$ The plot of $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ gives a straight line with slope $m$: $$m = -\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2}$$ Therefore: $$D_{eff} = -\frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2} \cdot m$$ This is the **slope method** of calculating the effective moisture diffusivity. --- **(b) Arrhenius Equation Application:** The Arrhenius equation is: $$D_{eff} = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right)$$ Linearised form: $$\ln(D_{eff}) = \ln(D_0) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$ **Tabulating the data:** | $T$ (°C) | $T$ (K) | $D_{eff}$ (m²/s) | $1/T$ (K⁻¹) | $\ln(D_{eff})$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 50 | 323.15 | $5.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $3.0945 \times 10^{-3}$ | -19.113 | | 60 | 333.15 | $8.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $3.0017 \times 10^{-3}$ | -18.645 | | 70 | 343.15 | $1.4 \times 10^{-8}$ | $2.9142 \times 10^{-3}$ | -18.082 | | 80 | 353.15 | $2.2 \times 10^{-8}$ | $2.8317 \times 10^{-3}$ | -17.633 | **Linear regression** of $\ln(D_{eff})$ vs. $1/T$: $$\text{Slope } m = -5634.6 \text{ K}$$ $$\text{Intercept } b = -1.685$$ **Activation Energy:** $$E_a = -m \times R = 5634.6 \times 8.314 = 46,847 \text{ J/mol} \approx 46.85 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Pre-exponential factor:** $$\ln(D_0) = -1.685 \Rightarrow D_0 = \exp(-1.685) = 0.1855 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{D_0 \approx 0.186 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$ **Conclusion:** The activation energy of **46.85 kJ/mol** lies within the typical range (20–80 kJ/mol) for vegetables, indicating that moisture diffusion in this vegetable is moderately temperature-sensitive. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cause Loss of Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert moisture content units** — Many students calculate in wb and use the same value in db. Always check whether the formula requires wb or db. Drying calculations almost always use **db**. > > 2. **Unit error in $D_{eff}$ calculation** — The slope of $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ is in **min⁻¹** or **s⁻¹**, and the thickness $L$ must be in **metres**, not millimetres. Forgetting to convert gives an answer off by a factor of $10^6$. > > 3. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in the Arrhenius equation. The temperature $T$ in $1/T$ MUST be in Kelvin, not Celsius. > > 4. **Using $X^*$ as zero without checking** — In some problems, $X^*$ is given (non-zero) and must be used; the simplified MR formula applies only if $X^* = 0$. > > 5. **Mixing up the formula for the slope** — The slope is **negative** for $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ (since MR decreases). $D_{eff}$ is positive because of the additional negative sign. Don't drop the negative sign. > > 6. **Writing the constant incorrectly** — $\frac{8}{\pi^2} \approx 0.8106$ — using $8/\pi$ or $8/\pi^2$ in the wrong place is a common error. > > 7. **Confusing drying rate $N$ with drying constant $k$** — They are completely different quantities; the unit of $N$ is kg/m²·h, while the unit of $k$ is 1/min or 1/s. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** ### 1. Core Definitions - **Dehydration** = Artificial drying to a low final moisture (typically < 8 % wb). - **Drying** = General term for moisture removal. - **Moisture Ratio (MR)** = $\frac{X_t - X^*}{X_0 - X^*}$ (dimensionless, 0 to 1). - **EMC ($X^*$)** = Equilibrium moisture content; depends on $T$ and RH. - **Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$)** = Transition between CRP and FRP. ### 2. Key Conversions - $X_{db} = \frac{X_{wb}}{1 - X_{wb}}$ - Temperature K = °C + 273.15 - 1 mm = $10^{-3}$ m ### 3. Important Models (with formula) - **Newton:** $MR = \exp(-k t)$ — simplest, 1 parameter. - **Page:** $MR = \exp(-k t^n)$ — best empirical fit. - **Henderson-Pabis:** $MR = a \exp(-k t)$ — 2 parameters. - **Midilli-Kucuk:** $MR = a \exp(-k t) + c t$ — best 3-parameter. ### 4. Diffusion Calculation - Long-time Fick: $\ln(MR) = \ln(\frac{8}{\pi^2}) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}$ - Slope of $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ is **negative**; $D_{eff} = -\frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2} \times m$ - Typical $D_{eff}$ range: $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-11}$ m²/s ### 5. Arrhenius - $\ln(D_{eff}) = \ln(D_0) - \frac{E_a}{R T}$ - Slope of $\ln(D_{eff})$ vs. $1/T$ = $-E_a/R$ - Typical $E_a$ for vegetables: **20 to 80 kJ/mol** ### 6. Quality Parameters - **Rehydration Ratio (RR)** = $m_{rehydrated} / m_{dehydrated}$ (good quality: RR > 4) - **Shrinkage (%)** = $\frac{V_0 - V_d}{V_0} \times 100$ (lower is better) - **Water activity ($a_w$)** < 0.6 (safe storage threshold) ### 7. Constants - $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ - $8/\pi^2 = 0.8106$ - $\pi^2 = 9.8696$ ### 8. Critical Exam Skills - Always tabulate the data first. - Plot $X_{db}$ vs. $t$, $N$ vs. $X_{db}$, $MR$ vs. $t$, and $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$. - Identify $X_c$ from the kink in the drying rate curve. - Carry out at least 2 replicates for accuracy. - Report results with correct **significant figures** and **SI units**. ---

topics.title: Stages of Sugar Cooking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Stages of Sugar Cooking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sugar Cooking** is the controlled thermal processing of a sucrose-based syrup (often combined with other saccharides, acids, and water) to a specific end-point temperature or water activity, at which the syrup develops distinct physical, rheological, and sensory characteristics. The *stages* of sugar cooking are sequential equilibrium points along the boiling curve of the syrup, where the progressive evaporation of water concentrates the dissolved solids, raises the boiling point, and transforms the liquid from a thin fluid into a pliable, brittle, or glassy mass.

In **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology aligned with confectionery/food processing engineering, the stages are classified by:
- **Final cooking temperature** (°C / °F)
- **Residual moisture content** (%)
- **Cold-water test behavior** (the classic empirical identification method)
- **End-product application** (fondant, fudge, toffee, hard candy, caramel, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The stages are a direct function of the *solids-to-water ratio*. As water evaporates, the boiling point rises from 100 °C (pure water) up to ~160 °C (nearly anhydrous sugar). Each stage is therefore not a fixed temperature but a *function of the formulation, atmospheric pressure, and dissolved solids*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Elevator"

Imagine a **glass elevator** inside a pot of sugar syrup, starting at the **ground floor (water)** and rising floor by floor as the fire below evaporates water. As the elevator rises, the syrup's character changes:

- **Lower floors** → thin, watery, runny (thread stage — like a thin thread of honey).
- **Middle floors** → soft, moldable, pliable (soft ball — like a marshmallow).
- **Upper floors** → firm, chewy, resilient (hard ball — like a caramel).
- **Top floors** → brittle, glassy, snaps cleanly (hard crack — like a lollipop).
- **Penthouse** → burnt, dark, aromatic (caramel — Maillard chemistry unleashed).

Each "floor" corresponds to a specific **boiling temperature** and a specific **residual water %** inside the syrup. The candymaker's job is to push the elevator to the *exact floor* the recipe demands — no higher, no lower — by watching the thermometer and performing the **cold-water test**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the boiling point rise?** Sucrose (and other dissolved sugars) cause **boiling-point elevation** — a colligative property. The higher the dissolved-sugar concentration, the more energy (temperature) is needed to drive water out of the solution.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Boiling point of pure water at 1 atm** | **100 °C / 212 °F** |
| **Decomposition temperature of sucrose** | **~160 °C / 320 °F** |
| **Inversion threshold (acid + heat)** | **~105 °C** |
| **Caramelization onset (sucrose)** | **~160 °C / 320 °F** |
| **Standard cold-water test bath** | **15 °C / 59 °F (ice-water)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Science Behind the Stages

Sugar cooking is governed by **three simultaneous physico-chemical phenomena**:

1. **Evaporative Concentration** — As water leaves the syrup, the **°Brix** (mass fraction of soluble solids, expressed as %) rises. Higher Brix → higher boiling point → higher viscosity.
2. **Sucrose Hydrolysis (Inversion)** — In the presence of acid or invertase, sucrose (disaccharide) hydrolyzes into **glucose + fructose** (invert sugar). Invert sugar has a lower crystallization tendency and contributes to *hygroscopicity* and *grain control*.
3. **Thermal Decomposition** — At temperatures above ~150 °C, sucrose undergoes **caramelization** (pyrolysis of sugars) and, if amino acids are present, the **Maillard reaction** (non-enzymatic browning).

## 2.2 The Seven Classical Stages

| # | Stage Name | Temperature (°C) | Temperature (°F) | Water Content (%) | Sugar Solids (%) | Cold-Water Behavior | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thread (Syrup)** | **101 – 104** | **214 – 219** | ~30 | ~70 | Forms a thin, 2-cm thread that breaks easily | Sugar syrups, glazes |
| 2 | **Soft Ball** | **112 – 115** | **234 – 239** | ~13 | ~85 | Forms a soft, pliable ball that flattens when removed | Fondant, fudge, pralines |
| 3 | **Firm Ball** | **118 – 121** | **244 – 250** | ~10 | ~88 | Forms a firm ball that doesn't flatten, holds shape | Caramels, nougat |
| 4 | **Hard Ball** | **121 – 130** | **250 – 266** | ~6 | ~92 | Forms a hard but still pliable ball | Marshmallows, divinity, taffy |
| 5 | **Soft Crack** | **132 – 143** | **270 – 290** | ~3 | ~95 | Forms threads that bend before breaking | Butterscotch, saltwater taffy |
| 6 | **Hard Crack** | **149 – 154** | **300 – 310** | ~1 | ~99 | Forms brittle threads that snap cleanly | Lollipops, hard candy, brittles |
| 7 | **Caramel** | **160 – 177** | **320 – 350** | <1 | ~100 | Syrup turns amber → dark brown; smokes | Caramel color, spun sugar decoration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Memorize the *end-point temperature* AND the *cold-water test* description for each stage. The 14-mark university exam questions frequently ask students to *identify* a stage from a given cold-water test observation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $$\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m \cdot i$$ | **Boiling-point elevation** (colligative) | K, mol/kg | Predicting syrup boiling temp from solute conc. |
| $$\text{°Brix} = \frac{\text{mass of dissolved solids}}{\text{total mass of solution}} \times 100$$ | Sugar concentration by mass | % | Refractometer-based QC |
| $$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$ | **Water activity** (ratio of partial vapor pressures) | dimensionless | Predicting microbial/shelf-life stability |
| $$\%\text{water} = 100 - \text{°Brix}$$ | Water content of syrup | % | Residual moisture in finished candy |
| $$\eta = \eta_0 \cdot e^{\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)}$$ | **Arrhenius viscosity** equation (sucrose melt) | Pa·s | Modeling pourability vs. temperature |
| $$\text{Sucrose} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ / \text{heat}} \text{Glucose} + \text{Fructose}$$ | Inversion reaction | — | Acid-balance calculations |
| $$\text{Maillard rate} \propto e^{\left(\frac{-E_a}{RT}\right)} \cdot [\text{sugar}] \cdot [\text{amine}]$$ | Maillard browning kinetics | — | Predicting browning in caramels |

> [!NOTE]
> **$K_b$ for water = 0.512 K·kg/mol**. For sucrose ($i = 1$, $M = 342.3$ g/mol), a 1 molal solution raises the boiling point by ~0.5 °C. Confectionery syrups are typically **3–10 molal**, so boiling-point elevation can contribute **2–5 °C** of the observed end-point shift — the rest comes from the **concentration-induced increase in vapor-pressure deficit**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial candy lines** use **continuous vacuum cookers** that flash-evaporate water at lower temperatures (e.g., 110 °C under 28 in Hg vacuum) to prevent thermal degradation while still reaching the desired solids concentration.
- **PLC-controlled batch cookers** terminate the cook at a pre-set temperature OR a pre-set **refractometer Brix** reading.
- **In Quality Control (QC) labs**, the **cold-water test** is being replaced by **digital refractometry** and **infra-red moisture analyzers**, but the cold-water test remains the **field standard** taught in KTU labs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Boiling Point of a Sugar Solution as a Function of Concentration

Starting from **Raoult's Law** and **Clausius-Clapeyron**, we can derive an approximate relationship between syrup Brix and boiling temperature.

### Step 1: Express the vapor pressure of water above the solution

By Raoult's Law, the partial pressure of water above a sugar solution is:

$$
p_w = x_w \cdot p_w^*
$$

Where:
- $p_w$ = partial pressure of water vapor over the solution (Pa)
- $x_w$ = mole fraction of water in the solution
- $p_w^*$ = vapor pressure of pure water at temperature $T$ (Pa)

### Step 2: Apply boiling-point condition (atmospheric pressure)

Boiling occurs when $p_w = P_{atm}$:

$$
x_w \cdot p_w^*(T_b) = P_{atm}
$$

For pure water, $x_w = 1$, so $p_w^*(T_b^0) = P_{atm}$, and $T_b^0 = 100 \, °\text{C}$ at 1 atm.

### Step 3: Approximate $x_w$ from the Brix value

The mole fraction of water can be related to Brix ($B$, in %) by:

$$
x_w = \frac{\dfrac{(100 - B)}{18.015}}{\dfrac{(100 - B)}{18.015} + \dfrac{B}{342.30}}
$$

Because sucrose has a much higher molecular weight (342.30 g/mol) than water (18.015 g/mol), the mole fraction of water drops *non-linearly* with rising Brix.

### Step 4: Integrate the Clausius-Clapeyron equation

$$
\int_{T_b^0}^{T_b} \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T^2} \, dT = -\ln x_w
$$

Assuming $\Delta H_{vap}$ is approximately constant (≈ 40.7 kJ/mol for water near 100 °C):

$$
\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_b^0} - \frac{1}{T_b}\right) = -\ln x_w
$$

Solving for $T_b$:

$$
T_b = \left[\frac{1}{T_b^0} - \frac{R \cdot (-\ln x_w)}{\Delta H_{vap}}\right]^{-1}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical verification for the "Hard Crack" stage

Given: $B = 99\%$, atmospheric pressure, $T_b^0 = 373.15$ K.

Calculate $x_w$:

$$
x_w = \frac{1 / 18.015}{(1 / 18.015) + (99 / 342.30)} = \frac{0.05551}{0.05551 + 0.28924} = \frac{0.05551}{0.34475} = 0.1610
$$

Wait — this is the mole fraction of water at 99% Brix *by mass*. Let us correct: 1 g syrup contains 0.99 g sucrose + 0.01 g water. Moles of sucrose = $0.99 / 342.30 = 0.002892$ mol. Moles of water = $0.01 / 18.015 = 0.0005551$ mol. Total = 0.003447 mol.

$$
x_w = \frac{0.0005551}{0.003447} = 0.1610
$$

Substitute into the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron:

$$
-\ln(0.1610) = 1.826
$$

$$
T_b = \left[\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{8.314 \times 1.826}{40700}\right]^{-1}
$$

$$
T_b = \left[0.002680 - 0.0003730\right]^{-1} = \left[0.002307\right]^{-1} = 433.4 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
T_b = 433.4 - 273.15 = 160.3 \, °\text{C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This calculated value (160.3 °C) is very close to the experimentally observed hard-crack end-point (~150–154 °C).** The discrepancy arises because (a) commercial hard-crack syrups are *not* 99% sucrose by mass (they contain glucose syrup solids, invert sugar, and ~1% residual water), and (b) the constant $\Delta H_{vap}$ assumption breaks down at high temperatures. The derivation nonetheless confirms the **physical mechanism**: **boiling-point elevation is a direct consequence of dissolved solids lowering the mole fraction of water**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Stages → Tests → Products

| Stage | Temp (°C) | Cold-Water Visual | Tactile | Acoustic | Brix | Major Product | Defect If Missed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thread | 101–104 | 2-cm fine thread, fragile | None | None | 70–75 | Sugar glaze | Runny, won't set |
| Soft Ball | 112–115 | Soft ball, flattens in 5 s | Squeezable | Silent | 84–87 | Fudge, Fondant | Grainy, weeping |
| Firm Ball | 118–121 | Firm ball, holds shape | Chewy | Silent | 87–90 | Caramels | Sticky, slumps |
| Hard Ball | 121–130 | Hard ball, sticky | Resilient | Silent | 90–92 | Marshmallow, divinity | Tacky, won't cut |
| Soft Crack | 132–143 | Threads bend, then break | Plastic | Faint crack | 95–96 | Taffy, butterscotch | Sticky teeth |
| Hard Crack | 149–154 | Threads snap, brittle | Glassy | Sharp snap | 98–99 | Lollipops, brittles | Chewy, graining |
| Caramel | 160–177 | Brown, viscous smoke | Liquid | Sizzle | 99+ | Caramel color | Burnt, bitter |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sugar Cooking Process Flow
