# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Soft Ball Stage" in sugar cooking. Mention its typical temperature range and one product made at this stage.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Soft Ball Stage** is the point in sugar-cooking where a small sample of the hot syrup, when dropped into ice-cold water, forms a **soft, pliable ball** that flattens on its own weight after a few seconds. The temperature range is **112 – 115 °C (234 – 239 °F)**, with a residual water content of approximately **13%**. A typical product prepared at this stage is **fondant** (used as icing and candy centers). Other examples include **fudge** and **pralines**. The corresponding Brix is roughly **84 – 87%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Mentioning *both* the temperature range and the cold-water test description fetches full marks. Stating just one of them is a 1.5-mark loss.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why does the boiling temperature of a sugar syrup rise as water evaporates? Mention the underlying physical principle.**

**Model Answer:**

As water evaporates from a sugar syrup, the **concentration of dissolved solids (sugars) increases**, which lowers the **mole fraction of water** ($x_w$) in the solution. According to **Raoult's Law**, the partial vapor pressure of water above the solution is $p_w = x_w \cdot p_w^*$. Boiling requires $p_w = P_{atm}$; therefore, as $x_w$ decreases, the solution must be heated to a higher temperature to raise $p_w^*$ sufficiently to match atmospheric pressure. This is a direct demonstration of the **boiling-point elevation** phenomenon, a **colligative property** of solutions. Quantitatively, the shift is described by:

$$
\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m \cdot i
$$

where $K_b = 0.512$ K·kg/mol for water, $m$ is molality, and $i$ is the van't Hoff factor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **seven stages of sugar cooking** with their characteristic temperature ranges, cold-water test observations, and at least one product manufactured at each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **role of sucrose inversion** in confectionery manufacturing. Discuss how **acid addition** and **temperature** influence the inversion rate, and list the consequences of inversion on the finished product.

#### Model Solution (a):

The seven classical stages of sugar cooking are sequential equilibrium points along the boiling curve of a sucrose-based syrup. They are determined by the **residual water content** of the syrup and are identified by a **cold-water test** or a calibrated thermometer.

1. **Thread Stage (101 – 104 °C / 214 – 219 °F)**
   - Cold-water test: forms a fine, ~2 cm long thread that breaks easily.
   - Water content: ~30%, Brix ~70%.
   - Product: sugar syrups, glazes, candied fruits.

2. **Soft Ball Stage (112 – 115 °C / 234 – 239 °F)**
   - Cold-water test: forms a soft, pliable ball that flattens on standing.
   - Water content: ~13%, Brix ~85%.
   - Product: **fondant, fudge, pralines**.

3. **Firm Ball Stage (118 – 121 °C / 244 – 250 °F)**
   - Cold-water test: forms a firm ball that holds its shape when removed but still yields to pressure.
   - Water content: ~10%, Brix ~88%.
   - Product: **caramels, nougat, divinity base**.

4. **Hard Ball Stage (121 – 130 °C / 250 – 266 °F)**
   - Cold-water test: forms a hard but sticky ball; very tough to deform.
   - Water content: ~6%, Brix ~92%.
   - Product: **marshmallows, divinity, Turkish delight**.

5. **Soft Crack Stage (132 – 143 °C / 270 – 290 °F)**
   - Cold-water test: forms thin threads that bend before breaking; pliable.
   - Water content: ~3%, Brix ~95%.
   - Product: **butterscotch, saltwater taffy**.

6. **Hard Crack Stage (149 – 154 °C / 300 – 310 °F)**
   - Cold-water test: forms brittle threads that snap cleanly and audibly.
   - Water content: ~1%, Brix ~99%.
   - Product: **lollipops, hard-boiled candy, peanut brittles, toffee**.

7. **Caramel Stage (160 – 177 °C / 320 – 350 °F)**
   - Cold-water test: syrup darkens to amber, smokes; cannot be tested as a thread.
   - Water content: <1%, Brix ~100%.
   - Product: **caramel color, spun-sugar decoration, burnt-sugar flavorings**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage-to-product mapping is the most heavily weighted portion of this question** — KTU examiners award 1 mark per stage for correct product identification.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Sucrose Inversion — Role in Confectionery**

Sucrose ($\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}$) is a **non-reducing disaccharide** composed of glucose and fructose linked by an $\alpha$-1,2-glycosidic bond. **Inversion** is the hydrolytic cleavage of this bond, yielding one molecule each of **D-glucose** and **D-fructose**, collectively called **invert sugar**. The term "inversion" originates from the change in optical rotation: sucrose rotates polarized light at +66.5°, while the equimolar mixture rotates at –39.9°, hence the sign inverts.

**Reaction:**

$$
\text{Sucrose} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ \text{ or invertase}} \text{Glucose} + \text{Fructose}
$$

**Influencing Factors:**

1. **Acidity (pH):** Inversion rate is highly pH-dependent. The reaction is **acid-catalyzed**, with the rate approximately **doubling for every 1-unit drop in pH below 7**. Citric, tartaric, and cream-of-tartar (added in fudge-making) are common acidulants.
2. **Temperature:** Inversion rate roughly doubles for every **10 °C rise** in cooking temperature (Q10 ≈ 2). Long cooks at the hard-crack stage can invert a substantial fraction of the sucrose.
3. **Time:** Inversion is cumulative; prolonged holding at high temperature increases conversion.
4. **Water Activity:** Higher water content (lower Brix) favors hydrolysis.

**Consequences on the Finished Product:**

| Consequence | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Prevents graining (anti-crystallization)** | Invert sugar interferes with sucrose crystal growth, yielding a **smoother texture** in fondants and fudges. |
| **Increases hygroscopicity** | Fructose is highly hygroscopic; the finished candy absorbs atmospheric moisture, becoming **soft and sticky** if humidity is uncontrolled. |
| **Enhances browning** | Both glucose and fructose are **reducing sugars** that participate in the **Maillard reaction**, producing desirable golden-brown color and flavor in caramels. |
| **Lowers the glass-transition temperature ($T_g$)** | Invert sugar plasticizes the amorphous matrix, giving a **softer, more pliable** candy at lower moisture. |
| **Modifies sweetness profile** | Fructose is perceived as sweeter than sucrose, so inversion can subtly **intensify perceived sweetness**. |

**Quantitative note:** The first-order rate constant for acid-catalyzed inversion is approximately:

$$
k \approx 0.0001 \cdot [H^+] \cdot e^{(-12500 / RT)} \quad \text{(min}^{-1}\text{)}
$$

This means a pH-3 syrup at 120 °C reaches ~50% inversion in roughly 30 minutes.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **labeled temperature-water content diagram**, explain the relationship between residual moisture and the cooking end-point. Use the diagram to justify why the **Hard Crack Stage** is the most challenging to control.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **defects encountered in sugar confectionery** due to incorrect end-point determination. Provide at least **four defects** with their causes and remedies.

#### Model Solution (a):

A **temperature vs. residual water content diagram** is the canonical engineering tool for visualizing sugar-cooking stages. On the x-axis is the **residual water content (%)** of the syrup, and on the y-axis is the **cooking temperature (°C)**. The curve is **steeply negative and non-linear**: as water decreases from ~50% down to <1%, temperature climbs from ~100 °C to >160 °C.

**Key features of the curve:**

- **Lower region (100 – 110 °C, 30 – 50% water):** Large changes in water produce small temperature shifts. The curve is *flat*; the cold-water test is the only reliable method.
- **Middle region (110 – 130 °C, 6 – 30% water):** The curve steepens; the thermometer becomes the primary tool.
- **Upper region (130 – 160 °C, <3% water):** The curve becomes **near-vertical** because of the boiling-point elevation mechanism we derived in Section 3. A loss of just 1% residual water in this region corresponds to a temperature jump of **10 – 15 °C**.

**Why Hard Crack is the most challenging stage:**

1. **The curve is near-vertical:** A timing error of just 10 – 15 seconds can push the syrup from hard crack to caramel, with no observable intermediate stage.
2. **Thermal inertia of the pan and syrup:** Even after the heat source is switched off, the residual heat in the cookware continues to raise the syrup temperature ("carry-over cooking"). The cook must therefore "pull" the syrup 2 – 3 °C *before* the target temperature is reached.
3. **Sucrose decomposition kinetics accelerate above 150 °C:** The difference between a clear hard-crack candy and a dark, bitter, caramelized one is a matter of seconds.
4. **Cold-water test is unreliable above 150 °C** because the syrup cools too fast in the water bath to form a stable test sample — the operator relies entirely on the thermometer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the "pull temperature" (where the heat is cut) with the "end-point temperature" (where the syrup actually finishes cooking). Always state *both* values in your answer.

#### Model Solution (b):

| # | Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Graining (sugar crystals too large)** | Overcooking at the soft ball stage, or agitation during fondant crystallization that introduces seed crystals | Add invert sugar (acid-catalyzed); beat at correct temperature; add corn syrup to inhibit crystallization |
| 2 | **Stickiness / weeping (sugar sweating)** | Undercooking — residual moisture is too high; the syrup never reaches the proper glassy amorphous state | Cook to the exact target temperature; use a calibrated thermometer; reduce atmospheric humidity during storage |
| 3 | **Caramelization off-flavor (burnt taste)** | Overcooking past hard crack into the caramel stage; too high a heat flux at the bottom of the pan | Use a heavy-bottomed pan; reduce heat as the target temperature approaches; pull the syrup 2–3 °C before the end-point |
| 4 | **Brittleness / cracking (too hard)** | Overcooking beyond target; too little invert sugar; too little residual moisture | Lower the end-point temperature by 2–3 °C; add glucose syrup (DE 40–42) to soften the matrix |
| 5 | **Color too pale** | Undercooking; insufficient Maillard reaction; addition of too much acid (which suppresses browning by lowering pH) | Cook 2–3 °C higher; reduce acid; use milk solids (which provide amines for Maillard) in caramels |
| 6 | **Foaming / boil-over** | Rapid heating; high initial water content; viscous surface layer traps steam | Use a tall-sided pan; add a small amount of butter or oil; control heat input |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In **Part B (a)**, students often *forget to label the axes and units* of their diagrams. **−2 marks**.
> - In **Part B (b)**, students list defects without mentioning the *underlying cause*. Always use the format: **Defect → Cause → Remedy**.
> - Do not confuse the *Hard Crack* and *Caramel* stages in your answer. Hard crack is a *clear, brittle* state; caramel is *brown and burnt*. Examiners will deduct marks for this fundamental mis-identification.
> - Always express temperatures in **°C** in the KTU answer script — the KTU 2024 scheme is metric-primary.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sugar cooking is a concentration process.** Each stage is a specific point on the water-content vs. temperature curve.
- **The seven stages** are: Thread → Soft Ball → Firm Ball → Hard Ball → Soft Crack → Hard Crack → Caramel.
- **Temperature anchors (°C):** 101, 113, 120, 127, 138, 152, 170. (Useful for quick mental recall.)
- **Water content drops** from ~30% (thread) to <1% (caramel) over the seven stages.
- **Boiling-point elevation** is the physical principle driving the rising end-point temperature; derived from Raoult's Law and the Clausius-Clapeyron equation.
- **Sucrose inversion** (acid + heat + time) produces invert sugar, which controls graining, browning, and hygroscopicity.
- **Cold-water test** is the classic field identification method; refractometry is the modern laboratory standard.
- **Defect → Cause → Remedy** is the mandatory answer format for troubleshooting questions.
- **Hard Crack (150 °C)** is the most challenging stage due to the near-vertical slope of the cooking curve.
- **Always "pull" the syrup 2 – 3 °C before the target end-point** to compensate for residual pan heat (carry-over cooking).
- **Safety:** Sugar syrups above 150 °C can cause severe burns and ignite on contact with water; always have a Class K fire extinguisher nearby.
- **End-products at each stage** (high-yield recall): Glaze, Fondant, Caramel, Marshmallow, Butterscotch, Hard Candy, Caramel Color.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Fruit Candy/Toffee
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - Processing of Cereals, Fruits, Tubers and Legumes Laboratory
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Fruit Candy/Toffee

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fruit Candy** is a confectionery product prepared by impregnating whole or cut fruit pieces (or fruit pulp/juice) with sugar (sucrose) to such an extent that the final product retains its original shape, possesses a glossy translucent appearance, and is shelf-stable due to low water activity ($a_w < 0.6$) and high sugar concentration (typically **65–75 °Brix**).

**Fruit Toffee** (also called *Fruit Paste* or *Fruit Leather-type Toffee*) is a concentrated, dehydrated fruit confection made by boiling fruit pulp/juice together with sugar, pectin (or pectin-rich ingredients), citric acid, and sometimes glucose syrup, until the mixture reaches a thick, pliable consistency with a Total Soluble Solids (TSS) content of **72–78 °Brix**, followed by cooling, cutting, and wrapping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCFTL507, Module 2):**
> The experiment demonstrates the principle of **osmotic dehydration** (for candy) and **concentration by evaporation** (for toffee), both of which lower water activity to inhibit microbial spoilage and enzymatic activity.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are drying a wet sponge by soaking it in honey. The honey's high sugar content "pulls" water out of the sponge via osmosis, and at the same time, sugar molecules enter the sponge. This is exactly how **fruit candy** works:

- The **fruit piece** = the sponge (rich in water).
- The **sugar syrup** = the honey (concentration gradient).
- The **final candy** = the sponge now full of sugar and low on water, so bacteria/yeasts/molds cannot grow on it.

**Fruit toffee**, on the other hand, is like making a thick fruit jam and then drying it further until it becomes chewy like a caramel — except the flavor and nutrients of real fruit dominate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Metrics in this Experiment (Bolded for emphasis):**
> - **TSS of sugar syrup (initial):** 30–40 °Brix
> - **TSS of sugar syrup (final boil, toffee):** 72–78 °Brix
> - **Final moisture content (candy):** < 22%
> - **Final moisture content (toffee):** < 14–18%
> - **Water activity ($a_w$) for shelf stability:** **< 0.65**
> - **Sugar : Acid : Pectin ratio (classic toffee gel):** **100 : 1 : 1** (by weight)

---

### 1.3 Difference Between Candy and Toffee

| Parameter | Fruit Candy | Fruit Toffee |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Base form | Whole/cut fruit piece | Fruit pulp/juice |
| Process | Osmotic impregnation | Boiling + concentration |
| Shape retained | Yes | No (amorphous block) |
| Final texture | Plump, slightly chewy | Soft, chewy, pliable |
| TSS (final) | 65–75 °Brix | 72–78 °Brix |
| Typical fruit examples | Amla, raw mango, pineapple | Mango, guava, papaya, apple |
| Use of pectin | Not essential | Often essential |
| Typical shelf life | 6–12 months | 3–6 months |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles Behind the Two Products

#### 2.1.1 Principle of Fruit Candy Preparation

The conversion of fresh fruit into candy is governed by **three simultaneous mass-transfer phenomena** when fruit is steeped in sugar syrup:

1. **Osmotic Dehydration (OD):** Water moves *out* of the fruit into the surrounding syrup, driven by the chemical potential gradient ($\mu_{water}^{fruit} > \mu_{water}^{syrup}$).
2. **Solute Impregnation (SI):** Sugar molecules diffuse *into* the fruit matrix.
3. **Equilibrium:** After sufficient time, the fruit's internal water activity approaches that of the syrup.

The **rate** of these transfers depends on:
- **Syrup concentration (°Brix):** Higher → faster water loss, but a hard sugar crust may form.
- **Temperature:** Higher → faster diffusion (Fick's Law), but may cook the fruit.
- **Fruit geometry:** Smaller/thinner pieces → faster equilibration.
- **Agitation/movement:** Enhances mass transfer coefficient.

#### 2.1.2 Principle of Fruit Toffee Preparation

Toffee preparation is fundamentally **evaporative concentration** combined with **gel/structure formation**:

1. **Water removal by boiling:** Reduces volume and increases TSS.
2. **Pectin-sugar-acid gel network:** When pectin (a polysaccharide) is heated with sufficient sugar (≥ 60%) and at the correct pH (2.8–3.4), a three-dimensional gel network forms, giving the toffee its characteristic chewy texture.
3. **Glass transition:** At low moisture, the product enters a glassy state ($T_g$ above ambient), ensuring microbiological and chemical stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Water Activity ($a_w$):**
> Microorganisms cannot grow below $a_w = 0.61$ (most bacteria), $0.65$ (yeasts), or $0.70$ (molds). Both candy and toffee aim to push $a_w$ below **0.65**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application | Units |
|---|--------------------|----------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta W = \dfrac{W_0 - W_t}{W_0} \times 100$ | Percent weight loss (osmotic dehydration) | % |
| 2 | $SG = \dfrac{W_t - W_0}{W_0} \times 100$ | Solid gain (% solute impregnation) | % |
| 3 | $WL + SG = \dfrac{\Delta M}{M_0} \times 100$ | Mass balance; $\Delta M$ = total mass change | % |
| 4 | $\text{Yield \%} = \dfrac{W_{final}}{W_{raw} + W_{sugar}} \times 100$ | Process yield efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\text{°Brix} \approx \% \text{TSS (w/w)}$ | Refractometric sugar estimate | °Brix |
| 6 | $a_w \approx \dfrac{p}{p_0} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$ | Water activity definition | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{gel} \propto \dfrac{[Pectin] \times [Sugar]}{[Acid]}$ | Qualitative gel strength relationship | — |
| 8 | $MR = \dfrac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = e^{-kt}$ | Moisture Ratio (Fickian diffusion model) | — |
| 9 | $\Delta T = T_{boil} - 1.5\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Approx. syrup boiling point rise per °Brix | °C |
| 10 | $T_g \approx T_{room} + \Delta$ condition | Glass transition — stable amorphous state | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter for KTU lab viva:**
> Examiners routinely ask about **weight loss**, **solid gain**, **yield %**, and **TSS measurement** by refractometer. Memorize formulas 1, 4, 5, and 6 above.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Process Engineering:** Design of batch osmotic dehydrators, vacuum impregnation systems, and scraped-surface evaporators.
- **Shelf-Life Engineering:** Prediction of microbial stability from $a_w$ and moisture sorption isotherms (BET/GAB models).
- **Quality Control:** Use of refractometers (°Brix) and water activity meters in industry.
- **Product Development:** Formulation of sugar-free or reduced-sugar candies (e.g., using sorbitol, stevia, or maltodextrin).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Procedure for Fruit Candy (e.g., Raw Mango / Amla / Pineapple)

#### 3.1.1 Materials Required
- Fresh ripe/halved fruit — **500 g**
- Sugar — **700 g**
- Citric acid — **2–3 g**
- Potassium meta-bisulphite (KMS) — **0.5–1 g** (optional preservative)
- Water — **500 mL**

#### 3.1.2 Equipment
- Stainless steel vessel, gas stove/burner, refractometer (0–80 °Brix), muslin cloth, knife, trays, drying oven (60 °C).

#### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Process

1. **Selection & Washing:** Choose firm, ripe, unblemished fruit. Wash under running potable water.
2. **Peeling & Cutting:** Peel and cut into uniform cubes/strips (~1–2 cm).
3. **Blanching (optional):** Dip fruit in boiling water for **2–3 minutes** to inactivate enzymes and soften tissues.
4. **Pricking:** Prick each piece with a fork to enhance syrup penetration.
5. **Initial Soak (Low °Brix):** Prepare syrup at **30 °Brix** (300 g sugar + 500 mL water, boiled & cooled). Add 0.5 g KMS. Soak fruit for **24 hours**.
6. **First Syrup Raise:** Drain syrup, add **150 g sugar** to bring to **40 °Brix**, re-immerse fruit for **24 hours**.
7. **Second Syrup Raise:** Repeat to reach **50 °Brix** for 24 h.
8. **Third Syrup Raise:** Repeat to reach **60 °Brix** for 24 h.
9. **Fourth Syrup Raise:** Bring to **65–70 °Brix**, add citric acid, soak 24–48 h until equilibrium.
10. **Draining & Surface Drying:** Drain and place on wire mesh trays. Dry in oven at **55–60 °C** for **8–12 hours** until surface is non-sticky.
11. **Packaging:** Pack in airtight polythene/polypropylene pouches.

#### 3.1.4 Analytical Calculation for Candy (Worked Example)

Given: $W_0 = 500$ g fresh fruit, after final stage $W_t = 680$ g candied fruit.

$$\Delta W = \dfrac{W_0 - W_t}{W_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{500 - 680}{500} \times 100 = -36\%$$

The negative sign here would be misleading because weight actually *increased* due to sugar uptake. Therefore the correct reporting is:

$$\text{Solid Gain (SG)} = \dfrac{W_t - W_0}{W_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{680 - 500}{500} \times 100 = 36\%$$

So the fruit **gained 36% of its original weight in sugars**. (Note: water loss simultaneously occurred — net weight gain = sugar gain − water loss.)

$$\text{Yield \%} = \dfrac{680}{500 + 700} \times 100 = \dfrac{680}{1200} \times 100 = 56.67\%$$

---

### 3.2 Complete Procedure for Fruit Toffee (e.g., Mango / Guava)

#### 3.2.1 Materials Required (per 1 kg fruit pulp)
- Fruit pulp — **1 kg**
- Sugar — **800 g to 1 kg** (≈ 80–100% of pulp weight)
- Citric acid — **5 g**
- Pectin (commercial or natural from fruit) — **5–10 g**
- Glucose syrup (optional, prevents crystallization) — **50–100 g**
- Butter/ghee — **10 g** (anti-sticking)

#### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Process

1. **Washing, Peeling, Pulping:** Wash fruit, peel, deseed, extract pulp using a pulper or blender.
2. **Cooking the Pulp:** Heat pulp in a heavy-bottomed stainless steel pan to **80 °C** for **10 minutes** to destroy enzymes.
3. **Adding Sugar:** Add sugar gradually with constant stirring. Sugar dissolves, raising TSS.
4. **Concentration Stage:** Continue boiling. The temperature rises as water evaporates. The classic **sheet test** is performed:
   - Dip a wooden spoon in the boiling mass; the last drop should form a *sheet* rather than a drop — this is the candy stage.
5. **Adding Pectin & Acid:** Add pectin (premixed with a little sugar to prevent clumping) and citric acid. Boil 3–5 min more.
6. **Adding Glucose:** Add glucose syrup to prevent sucrose crystallization during storage.
7. **Final TSS Check:** Stop cooking when refractometer reads **72–78 °Brix** and the mass "leaves the bottom of the pan."
8. **Pouring & Setting:** Pour onto a greased tray (butter-coated) or into molds. Allow to cool and set for **6–12 hours**.
9. **Cutting & Wrapping:** Cut into desired shapes (cubes/strips) and wrap individually in butter paper / cellophane / metalized film.

#### 3.2.3 Analytical Calculations for Toffee

**Given:**
- Pulp: $M_p = 1000$ g
- Sugar: $M_s = 850$ g
- Acid: $M_a = 5$ g
- Pectin: $M_{pec} = 8$ g
- Final toffee mass: $M_f = 1340$ g

**Step 1 — Total Solids Input:**

$$TS_{in} = TS_{pulp} + TS_{sugar} + TS_{additives}$$

Assuming pulp = 14% TSS → $TS_{pulp} = 1000 \times 0.14 = 140$ g

Sugar is ~100% solid → $TS_{sugar} = 850$ g

Additives ≈ 13 g solid

$$TS_{in} = 140 + 850 + 13 = 1003 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Moisture Evaporated:**

$$M_{evap} = (M_p + M_s + M_a + M_{pec}) - M_f$$
$$M_{evap} = (1000 + 850 + 5 + 8) - 1340 = 523 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Final Moisture Content:**

$$M_{final} = M_f - TS_{in} = 1340 - 1003 = 337 \text{ g}$$
$$M\% = \dfrac{337}{1340} \times 100 = 25.15\%$$
$$\text{Solid \%} = 100 - 25.15 = 74.85\% \approx 75\ ^\circ\text{Brix}$$

**Step 4 — Yield %:**

$$\text{Yield \%} = \dfrac{M_f}{M_p + M_s} \times 100 = \dfrac{1340}{1850} \times 100 = 72.43\%$$

**Step 5 — Pectin-Sugar-Acid Ratio (Hugot's Classic Gel Ratio):**

$$\text{Ratio} = \text{Sugar : Acid : Pectin} = 850 : 5 : 8$$
$$\text{Normalized ratio} = 106 : 0.625 : 1 \text{ (dividing by 8)}$$

This is **not** the classic 100:1:1, indicating **excess sugar** — expected for toffee, which uses high sugar for the glassy chewy texture.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Critical Control Points

| Stage | Parameter | Target Value | Critical Limit | Why Critical |
|-------|-----------|--------------|----------------|--------------|
| Syrup prep (candy) | Initial °Brix | 30 | ±2 | Too high → surface shrinkage |
| Syrup progression | Final °Brix | 65–70 | < 60 → spoilage | Microbial stability |
| Toffee boil | Final °Brix | 72–78 | < 70 → soft, sticky | Texture failure |
| pH (toffee) | pH | 2.8–3.4 | > 3.6 → weak gel | Pectin gel fails |
| Drying (candy) | Temperature | 55–60 °C | > 70 °C → caramelization | Quality loss |
| Drying (candy) | Time | 8–12 h | < 6 h → surface sticky | Shelf-life failure |
| Moisture (toffee) | Final % | < 18% | > 22% → mold growth | Spoilage |
| Water activity | $a_w$ | < 0.65 | > 0.70 → yeast growth | Microbial stability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define "fruit candy" and explain why it is shelf-stable without refrigeration.

**Model Answer:**
Fruit candy is a sugar-preserved fruit product in which whole or cut fruit pieces are impregnated with sugar through repeated osmotic equilibration with progressively concentrated sugar syrup (final TSS ≈ 65–70 °Brix). It is shelf-stable because the high sugar concentration lowers the **water activity ($a_w$) below 0.65**, inhibiting the growth of bacteria, yeasts, and molds. Additionally, the high osmotic pressure causes osmotic dehydration of microbial cells, and the high sugar-to-water ratio suppresses enzymatic and chemical deteriorative reactions.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the role of citric acid and pectin in fruit toffee preparation?

**Model Answer:**
**Citric acid** lowers the pH to **2.8–3.4**, which is the optimum range for pectin gelation. It also inactivates enzymes, enhances flavor, and contributes to microbial stability.

**Pectin** (a high-methoxyl polysaccharide) forms a three-dimensional gel network when (a) sufficient sugar (≥ 60%) is present to dehydrate the pectin chains, (b) the pH is acidic (2.8–3.4) to neutralize carboxyl groups and reduce electrostatic repulsion, and (c) the temperature is moderate (≤ 100 °C). This gel network traps water and sugar, giving the toffee its characteristic chewy texture.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question offers internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe with a stepwise procedure the preparation of **mango candy** in the laboratory. List the ingredients with quantities for 500 g of fresh fruit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **percent solid gain** and **process yield** given: raw fruit = 500 g, sugar used = 700 g, final candied fruit mass = 680 g. Comment on the implications for shelf stability. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. **Selection & Washing:** Take 500 g of firm, ripe mangoes. Wash thoroughly.
2. **Peeling & Slicing:** Peel the mangoes and cut into uniform strips of about 2 cm length and 1 cm thickness.
3. **Pricking:** Prick each strip with a fork to facilitate syrup penetration.
4. **Blanching:** Dip strips in boiling water for 2–3 minutes, then cool.
5. **Initial Syrup (30 °Brix):** Dissolve 300 g sugar in 500 mL water, boil, cool, add 0.5 g KMS. Soak mango strips for 24 h.
6. **Stage 2 (40 °Brix):** Drain syrup, add 100 g sugar to raise to 40 °Brix, re-immerse for 24 h.
7. **Stage 3 (50 °Brix):** Repeat to 50 °Brix, hold 24 h.
8. **Stage 4 (60 °Brix):** Repeat to 60 °Brix, hold 24 h.
9. **Stage 5 (65 °Brix):** Repeat to 65 °Brix. Add 2 g citric acid. Hold 24–48 h.
10. **Draining & Drying:** Drain, arrange on wire mesh trays, dry at 55–60 °C for 8–12 h until non-sticky.
11. **Cooling & Packing:** Cool, then pack in airtight polypropylene pouches.

**Ingredients Summary (per 500 g fruit):**

| Ingredient | Quantity |
|------------|----------|
| Mango (ripe, firm) | 500 g |
| Sugar (total) | ~700 g |
| Citric acid | 2 g |
| KMS | 0.5 g |
| Water | 500 mL |

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $W_0 = 500$ g (raw fruit)
- $W_t = 680$ g (final candied fruit)
- $W_{sugar} = 700$ g (sugar used)

**Step 1 — Solid Gain (SG):**

$$SG = \dfrac{W_t - W_0}{W_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{680 - 500}{500} \times 100 = 36\%$$

**Step 2 — Process Yield:**

$$\text{Yield \%} = \dfrac{W_t}{W_0 + W_{sugar}} \times 100 = \dfrac{680}{500 + 700} \times 100 = 56.67\%$$

**Step 3 — Shelf Stability Comment:**
A 36% solid gain indicates substantial sugar impregnation, which corresponds to a final TSS of approximately **68–72 °Brix**. This drives water activity ($a_w$) below 0.65, ensuring **microbiological shelf stability** for 6–12 months at ambient temperature, provided the candy is packed in airtight, moisture-impermeable packaging.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students frequently **swap the denominator** in yield calculation (using only raw fruit mass instead of raw fruit + sugar). Always remember:
> $\text{Yield \%} = \dfrac{\text{Final Product Mass}}{\text{All Input Masses}} \times 100$
> Here, total input = fruit + sugar = **1200 g**, not 500 g.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **principles of fruit toffee preparation** with a flow diagram (description). List ingredients for 1 kg mango pulp. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A student prepared toffee from 1 kg pulp (14% TSS) + 850 g sugar + 8 g pectin + 5 g citric acid. Final toffee mass was 1340 g. Calculate the **final moisture content** and verify whether it meets the shelf-stable specification. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Principles of Fruit Toffee Preparation:**

1. **Evaporative concentration** of fruit pulp with sugar to raise TSS to 72–78 °Brix.
2. **Pectin–Sugar–Acid gel formation** at pH 2.8–3.4 with ≥ 60% sugar.
3. **Glass transition** at low moisture → amorphous, stable state.
4. **Water activity reduction** below 0.65 → microbiological stability.
5. **Optional glucose addition** → prevents sucrose crystallization during storage.

**Process Flow:**
`Pulp → Heat (80 °C, 10 min) → Add sugar → Boil to concentrate → Add pectin & acid → Add glucose → Final TSS check (72–78 °Brix) → Pour & cool → Cut & wrap`

**Ingredients per 1 kg pulp:**

| Ingredient | Quantity |
|------------|----------|
| Mango pulp | 1000 g |
| Sugar | 850 g |
| Pectin | 8 g |
| Citric acid | 5 g |
| Glucose syrup | 50 g |
| Butter (greasing) | 10 g |

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Pulp: $M_p = 1000$ g at 14% TSS → Total solids in pulp = **140 g**
- Sugar: $M_s = 850$ g (~100% solid)
- Pectin: $M_{pec} = 8$ g (≈ 100% solid)
- Citric acid: $M_a = 5$ g (≈ 100% solid)
- Final toffee mass: $M_f = 1340$ g

**Step 1 — Total Solids Input:**

$$TS_{in} = 140 + 850 + 8 + 5 = 1003 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Moisture Remaining:**

$$M_{final} = M_f - TS_{in} = 1340 - 1003 = 337 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Final Moisture %:**

$$M\% = \dfrac{337}{1340} \times 100 = 25.15\%$$

**Step 4 — Total Solids (TSS):**

$$TSS = 100 - 25.15 = 74.85\% \approx 75\ ^\circ\text{Brix}$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**
A final TSS of 75 °Brix corresponds to $a_w < 0.65$, satisfying the **shelf-stable specification**. The moisture content of 25.15% is at the upper edge; for higher safety margin, drying or further boiling to < 18% moisture is recommended. **Product meets shelf stability criterion.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> Students often forget that **citric acid and pectin contain negligible moisture** and treat them as 100% solids. Including them as solids is correct. Also, do **not** confuse "moisture %" with "water activity" — they are different. A 25% moisture food can have $a_w < 0.65$ if it is sugar-rich.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fruit Candy & Toffee (PCFTL507)**

- ✅ **Fruit Candy** retains fruit shape; uses **osmotic dehydration** in progressively concentrated syrup (30 → 40 → 50 → 60 → 65 °Brix).
- ✅ **Fruit Toffee** uses **fruit pulp** and is concentrated by boiling to **72–78 °Brix**.
- ✅ **Pectin-Sugar-Acid ratio** of approximately **100 : 1 : 1** is essential for proper gel formation in toffee.
- ✅ **Optimum pH for pectin gel:** **2.8–3.4** (use citric acid).
- ✅ **KMS (Potassium meta-bisulphite)** acts as a preservative in syrups — typical dose **0.05–0.1%**.
- ✅ **Glucose syrup** (5–10% of sugar weight) prevents **sucrose crystallization** in toffee.
- ✅ **Final moisture for candy** < 22%; **for toffee** < 18% (or TSS > 72%).
- ✅ **Sheet test** confirms toffee end-point (drop forms a sheet, not a ball).
- ✅ **Water activity ($a_w$)** target for shelf stability: **< 0.65**.
- ✅ **Yield %** is calculated on **total input mass (fruit + sugar + additives)**, not fruit alone.
- ✅ **Solid Gain %** = $\dfrac{W_t - W_0}{W_0} \times 100$ — measures sugar uptake in candy.
- ✅ **Refractometer** measures TSS in °Brix — assumed ≈ % soluble solids (w/w) for sugar solutions.
- ✅ **Pricking** of fruit pieces accelerates syrup penetration (reduces diffusion path length).
- ✅ **Drying** at 55–60 °C avoids caramelization and case-hardening of candy surface.
- ✅ **Packaging** must be moisture-impermeable (polypropylene, metalized film) to prevent $a_w$ rise.
- ✅ **Common defects:** Surface crystallization (no glucose), sticky candy (under-drying), weak gel (wrong pH), burnt flavor (overheating).
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** Osmo-concentration is an energy-efficient pre-treatment before air-drying of fruits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Chocolate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Chocolate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍫 Module Experiment 2: Preparation of Chocolate — Comprehensive KTU Lab Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> Chocolate is a homogeneous colloidal dispersion prepared from **cocoa mass** (or cocoa liquor), **cocoa butter**, **sugar**, and optionally **milk solids**, **lecithin** (emulsifier), and **vanillin/pg** (flavoring). The *Preparation of Chocolate* is a multi-stage food processing experiment designed to demonstrate the unit operations of **mixing → refining → conching → tempering → molding → cooling**, leading to a stable polymorphic crystalline structure (Form V) of cocoa butter.

### 🔑 Syllabus Highlight
As per the **PCFTL507 (Food Processing & Technology Laboratory)** syllabus under the **2024 NEP-aligned Scheme**, this experiment falls under **Module 2 — Confectionery and Chocolate Processing**. The Course Outcome (CO) targeted is:
> **CO2:** *Apply the principles of confectionery manufacture to prepare standardized chocolate products and evaluate their physico-chemical and sensory attributes.*

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of chocolate-making like **building a brick house**:

| Step in Chocolate | Analogy in House Construction |
|---|---|
| **Roasting cocoa beans** | Gathering and cleaning raw bricks |
| **Winnowing (cracking & husk removal)** | Sorting the bricks — keeping only the good parts (nibs) |
| **Grinding (melanging/refining)** | Crushing bricks into fine powder + mixing with mortar |
| **Conching** | Kneading the mortar thoroughly until smooth |
| **Tempering** | Carefully cooling the molten chocolate so crystals align |
| **Molding & Cooling** | Setting the structure into a stable, glossy shape |

Without proper **tempering** (the controlled cooling), the "house" (chocolate bar) would crumble into a dull, white-bloomed, gritty mess — just like a poorly cured concrete wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric to Remember (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - **Standard Dark Chocolate Composition (approx.):** Cocoa solids **45–75%**, Sugar **20–40%**, Cocoa Butter **20–30%**, Lecithin **0.3–0.5%**, Vanilla **0.1%**.
> - **Tempering Temperature Window for Dark Chocolate:** **45°C → 27°C → 32°C** (melt → cool → reheat).
> - **Cocoa Butter Polymorph (stable):** **Form V** (β₂) — melts sharply at **32–34°C**.

---

### 📚 Historical & Industrial Context (Concept-Setting)

The manufacturing of chocolate, as performed in modern industry and reproduced in the lab, follows the **four foundational pillars** first established by **Coenraad Johannes van Houten** (hydraulic press, 1828), **Rudolphe Lindt** (conche, 1879), and **Daniel Peter** (milk chocolate, 1875). Each of their inventions corresponds to a lab stage you will perform today.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values:**
> - Specific heat capacity of cocoa butter: **≈ 2.0 J·g⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - Melting point of stable Form V cocoa butter: **33.8 °C**
> - Optimum moisture content of finished chocolate: **< 1.0 %**
> - Particle size after refining (KTU bench standard): **< 30 µm**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Role of Each Ingredient

The preparation of chocolate is essentially a **formulation science**. Each ingredient plays a precise physico-chemical role. Skipping or substituting any one component will cause product failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Rule:** Chocolate is **NOT** an emulsion in the classic sense — it is a **suspension of non-fat solids (sugar + cocoa) in a continuous fat phase (cocoa butter)**, stabilized by surface-active agents (lecithin, milk proteins).

#### 🔬 Ingredient-Wise Functional Breakdown

| Ingredient | Primary Function | Secondary Function | Typical % (Dark) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cocoa Mass / Liquor** | Provides cocoa solids + cocoa butter; delivers chocolate flavor & color | Source of polyphenols, theobromine | 45–75% |
| **Cocoa Butter** | Continuous fat phase; provides "snap" and melt-in-mouth feel | Carries flavor; enables tempering | 20–30% |
| **Sugar (sucrose)** | Sweetness; bulking agent | Reduces fat requirement; affects particle size | 20–40% |
| **Lecithin (soy/sunflower)** | **Emulsifier / viscosity reducer** (Yellowness Index) | Stabilizes suspension; reduces fat need | 0.3–0.5% |
| **PGPR (Polyglycerol polyricinoleate)** | Secondary emulsifier — extremely potent flow improver | Reduces yield stress | 0.05–0.2% |
| **Vanillin / Ethyl Vanillin** | Flavor enhancer | Masks bitterness | 0.05–0.1% |
| **Milk Solids (whole milk powder)** | Used in milk chocolate — provides Maillard precursors | Smooth texture, light color | 0–25% |

---

### 2.2 The Six Physical Phenomena You Must Understand

These are the **core physico-chemical principles** that examiners love to test in the 14-mark question.

**Phenomenon 1 — Particle Size Reduction (Refining)**
Sugar and cocoa particles must be reduced below **30 µm** because human palate detects grittiness above ~25 µm. The smaller the particle, the smoother the perceived texture. This is quantified using the **Casson equation** for viscosity.

**Phenomenon 2 — Conching**
A long-duration (4–72 hrs in industry; 30–60 min in lab), heated, kneading operation that:
- Removes **acetic acid**, **butyric acid**, and other volatile off-flavors (moisture and acidity drops to < 1%).
- Coats every solid particle with a thin fat film (cocoa butter) → uniform suspension.
- Develops the rounded, mellow chocolate flavor.

**Phenomenon 3 — Polymorphism of Cocoa Butter**
Cocoa butter can crystallize into **six polymorphic forms** (I to VI), but only **Form V (β₂)** gives the desired gloss, snap, and contraction for demolding. The lab tempering protocol forces nucleation into Form V.

**Phenomenon 4 — Viscosity Control (Casson Model)**

$$\eta_{ca} = \frac{\sigma^{1/2} - \sigma_y^{1/2}}{\dot{\gamma}^{1/2}}$$

**Phenomenon 5 — Heat & Mass Transfer During Cooling**
Cooling rate determines which polymorph forms. Slow cooling → unstable Forms I-IV (bloom). Controlled tempering then rapid cooling → Form V.

**Phenomenon 6 — Emulsification by Lecithin**
Lecithin is **amphiphilic** — its hydrophilic head binds to sugar/cocoa particles; lipophilic tail dissolves in cocoa butter. It **reduces yield stress and Casson viscosity** dramatically (up to 50% fat reduction).

---

### 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these equations appear verbatim in ESE papers.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Casson Viscosity Equation** | $\eta_{ca} = \dfrac{\left(\sigma^{1/2} - \sigma_{y}^{1/2}\right)}{\dot{\gamma}^{1/2}}$ | $\sigma$ = shear stress (Pa), $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹) |
| 2 | **Yield Stress (Chocolate)** | $\sigma_y \leq 20$ Pa (well-tempered dark chocolate) | Higher = harder to mold |
| 3 | **Melting Point of Form V** | $T_m = 33.8\ ^\circ C$ | Mouth temperature ≈ 37°C → melt-in-mouth |
| 4 | **Tempering Range (Dark)** | $45^\circ C \rightarrow 27^\circ C \rightarrow 32^\circ C$ | Melt → cool → reheat |
| 5 | **Tempering Range (Milk)** | $40^\circ C \rightarrow 26^\circ C \rightarrow 30^\circ C$ | Slightly lower |
| 6 | **Tempering Range (White)** | $38^\circ C \rightarrow 26^\circ C \rightarrow 29^\circ C$ | Lowest range |
| 7 | **Refining Particle Size** | $d_{p} < 30\ \mu m$ | $d_{p} < 25\ \mu m$ for premium |
| 8 | **Fat Reduction by Lecithin** | Viscosity $\downarrow$ by **~30–50%** per **0.3%** lecithin | Beyond 0.5% → over-emulsification |
| 9 | **Moisture Content (Final)** | $M < 1.0\%$ | Higher → sugar bloom |
| 10 | **Total Solids Balance** | $\% \text{Cocoa Solids} + \% \text{Sugar} + \% \text{Milk Solids} = 100 - \% \text{Fat}$ | Mass balance check |
| 11 | **Glass Transition Temp (Sugar)** | $T_g \approx 35\ ^\circ C$ (amorphous) | Below $T_g$ → sucrose bloom risk |
| 12 | **Polymorph Stability Order** | $V > VI > IV > III > II > I$ | Only V is shelf-stable |

## 3. Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Equipment, Glassware & Raw Materials Required

> [!NOTE]
> This list mirrors the **standard KTU PCFTL507 lab manual requirement** — memorise it; it shows up in viva questions worth 3 marks.

**Equipment & Glassware**
- Laboratory-grade **ball mill / refiner** *or* heavy mortar-pestle set
- **Conche** (lab-scale rotary conche) *or* heated planetary mixer with scraper
- **Water bath** with thermostatic control (precision ±0.5 °C)
- **Digital thermometer / probe** (range 0–100 °C)
- **Stainless steel bowls / beakers** (500 mL)
- **Silicone / polycarbonate molds** (mold geometry affects crystallization)
- **Refractometer** (for Brix check, optional)
- **Vernier caliper** (for thickness measurement of finished bar)
- **Spatulas, weighing balance** (accuracy 0.01 g)
- **Tempering slab (marble)** with scrapers

**Raw Materials**
- Cocoa mass / cocoa liquor (≥ 100 g)
- Cocoa butter (≥ 50 g)
- Sugar (powdered / icing sugar preferred for faster refining)
- Soy lecithin (powder, food grade)
- Vanillin (or vanilla essence)
- Optional: milk powder (for milk chocolate variant)

---

### 3.2 Standard Recipe Formulation (Dark Chocolate — 200 g batch)

This is the **base formulation** used in most KTU-affiliated college labs.

| Ingredient | Mass (g) | % w/w | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cocoa Mass (liquor) | 100.0 | 50.00 | Cocoa solids + natural fat |
| Cocoa Butter | 30.0 | 15.00 | Extra fat for fluidity |
| Sugar (powdered) | 68.0 | 34.00 | Sweetness + texture |
| Lecithin (powder) | 1.5 | 0.75 | Emulsifier |
| Vanillin | 0.2 | 0.10 | Flavor |
| **TOTAL** | **199.7 ≈ 200 g** | **≈ 100%** | — |

**Validation of Mass Balance:**

$$\text{Total Fat} = \text{Fat from cocoa mass (≈55%)} + \text{Added cocoa butter}$$

$$\text{Total Fat} = (100 \times 0.55) + 30 = 55 + 30 = 85\ g \approx 42.5\%$$

$$\text{Non-fat solids} = 100 - 55 + 68 = 113\ g \approx 56.5\%$$

This **fat : non-fat ratio ≈ 42.5 : 56.5** falls within the textbook optimal range of **30–40% fat for dark chocolate** — formulation validated.

---

### 3.3 ⏱️ Exhaustive Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laboratory Discipline Reminder:**
> Always pre-warm all vessels, weigh with **analytical balance to ±0.01 g**, and record observations in the lab manual at every stage. KTU examiners verify continuity of record.

#### **STAGE 1 — Pre-Mixing (Duration: ≈ 10 min)**

1. Weigh all ingredients accurately on the analytical balance.
2. Pre-melt cocoa butter in the water bath at **40 °C** until fully liquid.
3. In a clean dry bowl, combine cocoa mass + sugar + vanillin. Hand-mix to a uniform paste.
4. Add **2/3 of the melted cocoa butter** and stir vigorously with a spatula.

#### **STAGE 2 — Refining (Duration: ≈ 20–30 min)**

5. Transfer the paste into the **laboratory ball mill / refiner** (or use heavy mortar-pestle).
6. Refine for **20–30 min** until the mass feels smooth between finger and thumb (the **"no grits" test**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why grinding is non-negotiable:** Sugar particles > 30 µm cause **grittiness** and prevent **uniform fat coating** during conching. Industrial refiners use 5-roll refiners; in lab we approximate with extended ball milling.

7. Verify particle size (if micrometer available): target **< 30 µm**.

#### **STAGE 3 — Conching (Duration: ≈ 30–45 min)**

8. Transfer refined paste to the **lab conche** (or planetary mixer with heating jacket).
9. Maintain temperature at **50–60 °C** throughout.
10. Add **remaining cocoa butter** gradually in 3 aliquots at 10-min intervals.
11. After 25 min, add **lecithin** and continue conching for 15–20 min more.
12. Stop when:
    - The mass has a **smooth, flowing, glossy** appearance.
    - No acidic or "raw bean" smell remains.
    - A small drop, when spread on glass, leaves **no streak**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Volatile Loss during Conching:** Approximately **0.5–1.5%** mass loss occurs due to evaporation of acetic acid, moisture, and short-chain carbonyls. Industrial conches remove this; lab conching simulates but cannot fully replicate the time.

#### **STAGE 4 — Tempering (CRITICAL STEP — Duration: ≈ 20 min)**

13. Pour the conched chocolate into a clean stainless bowl.
14. **Melt completely** to **45 °C** in a water bath (destroys all crystal nuclei).
15. **Cool slowly** to **27 °C** by spreading on a marble slab and stirring continuously with a scraper (≈ 10 min). The chocolate thickens and begins to set at the edges.
16. **Reheat gently** to **32 °C** in a 33 °C water bath (≈ 5 min).
17. Test: dip a knife blade — chocolate should set within **3–5 min** with a uniform gloss.

> [!WARNING]
> **Tempering Failure Indicators:**
> - **Dull surface, white streaks after 24 hrs** → FAT BLOOM (Forms I–IV crystallized)
> - **White powdery spots** → SUGAR BLOOM (moisture migration)
> - **Crumbly, falls apart** → No tempering done

#### **STAGE 5 — Molding & Cooling (Duration: ≈ 30 min)**

18. Pour tempered chocolate into clean, dry, **room-temperature (20–25 °C)** polycarbonate molds.
19. Tap molds gently on bench **3–5 times** to release trapped air bubbles.
20. Cool at **18–20 °C** for **20–30 min** (do NOT refrigerate — refrigeration causes sugar bloom due to condensation).
21. Demold when chocolate contracts away from mold walls with a sharp **"snap"** sound.

---

### 3.4 Quality Evaluation Parameters

After demolding, conduct the following standard KTU lab tests:

| Parameter | Method | Target Value | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Visual Gloss** | Visual rating (1–5 scale) | 4–5 (good) | 2 |
| **Snap Test** | Audible evaluation on breaking bar | Sharp, clear crack | 2 |
| **Surface Bloom** | Visual after 24 hr at 25 °C | No bloom | 3 |
| **Thickness** | Vernier caliper, 5 points | 5–8 mm uniform | 1 |
| **Weight of Bar** | Weighing balance | ±2% of theoretical | 1 |
| **Sensory (Taste/Texture)** | Panel of 5 (or self-evaluation) | Smooth, no grits | 3 |
| **Yield** | $\dfrac{\text{Demolded weight}}{\text{Total mass poured}} \times 100$ | $\geq 95\%$ | 2 |

---

### 3.5 Precautions & Troubleshooting Matrix

| Problem | Root Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Gritty mouthfeel** | Under-refining | Re-mill for 15 min, re-conche |
| **Fat bloom (white coating)** | Poor tempering / cooling too slow | Re-temper entire batch |
| **Sugar bloom (rough white spots)** | Moisture contact during cooling | Cool in dry atmosphere; never refrigerate |
| **Air bubbles on surface** | Insufficient tapping of mold | Tap mold 5–7 times after pouring |
| **Sticking to mold** | Mold too cold / unclean | Pre-warm mold to 25 °C; polish with cotton |
| **Viscosity too high** | Insufficient lecithin or cocoa butter | Add 0.1% lecithin + 2 g cocoa butter |
| **Viscosity too low** | Excess fat | Add 2 g sugar, re-conche 5 min |
| **Acidic taste** | Insufficient conching | Conch for additional 15–20 min at 55 °C |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📚 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> Define **conching** in chocolate manufacture. List any **two functions** of conching.

**Model Answer:**
Conching is a long-duration, heated, kneading operation performed on refined chocolate mass at **50–60 °C** in a conche machine. Its primary functions are:
1. **Removal of volatile acidic compounds** (acetic acid, butyric acid, moisture) from the chocolate mass, thereby reducing acidity and astringency.
2. **Uniform coating** of every solid particle (sugar, cocoa) with a thin film of cocoa butter, producing a homogeneous suspension and improving flow properties.
*(Secondary accepted answers: flavor development through Maillard reactions, viscosity reduction.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

> **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> What is **fat bloom** in chocolate? State **two causes** of fat bloom.

**Model Answer:**
Fat bloom is the appearance of a **white, dull, greasy coating** on the surface of chocolate after storage. It is caused by the **migration of liquid cocoa butter to the surface** and its recrystallization into **unstable polymorphic forms (I–IV)** instead of the stable **Form V (β₂)**.
Two causes:
1. Improper or absent tempering during manufacture.
2. Storage of chocolate at temperatures **above 28 °C**, causing melting and re-crystallization. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 📚 Part B — 14-Mark Essay Questions (Internal Choice — Attempt ANY ONE)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a **flow diagram**, describe the **step-by-step laboratory procedure** for the preparation of **dark chocolate** from cocoa mass. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle of tempering** in chocolate manufacture. Discuss the **temperature-time curve** for dark, milk, and white chocolate. Why is Form V preferred? **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✍️ Model Answer to Question A(a)

The laboratory preparation of dark chocolate is performed in five sequential stages, as illustrated in the block diagram below.

**Stage 1 — Pre-Mixing:** Cocoa mass (100 g) is melted with two-thirds of the cocoa butter at **40 °C**. Powdered sugar (68 g) and vanillin (0.2 g) are blended into the molten mass using a spatula to form a homogeneous paste.

**Stage 2 — Refining:** The paste is transferred to a laboratory ball mill and refined for **20–30 minutes** until a finger-rub test (a pinch of chocolate rubbed between thumb and forefinger) reveals **no detectable grits**. The target particle size is **< 30 µm**; this reduces grittiness perceived by the palate.

**Stage 3 — Conching:** The refined paste is transferred to a lab conche (or heated planetary mixer) and agitated at **50–60 °C** for **30–45 minutes**. The remaining cocoa butter is added in three aliquots to allow even fat distribution. Lecithin (1.5 g) is introduced during the last 15 minutes to reduce viscosity. Conching drives off volatile acids, coats every solid particle with fat, and mellows the flavor.

**Stage 4 — Tempering:** The molten chocolate is brought to **45 °C** (melt), cooled to **27 °C** on a marble slab with continuous scraping, then reheated to **32 °C** in a controlled water bath. This selective cooling-reheating nucleates only the stable **Form V (β₂) crystals**.

**Stage 5 — Molding & Cooling:** The tempered chocolate is poured into polycarbonate molds (pre-conditioned to 22 °C), tapped 3–5 times to remove air bubbles, and cooled at **18–20 °C** for 25 minutes. The bar is demolded when it releases with an audible **"snap"**.

**Yield calculation:** Yield = (Demolded mass / Total poured mass) × 100, expected **≥ 95%**. **(7 Marks — full marks for correct sequence, temperature values, particle size mention, and yield statement)**

---

### ✍️ Model Answer to Question A(b)

**Principle of Tempering:**
Tempering is the **controlled thermal manipulation** of molten chocolate to direct the **polymorphic crystallization** of cocoa butter into its most stable form — **Form V (β₂)**. Cocoa butter is **polymorphic** and can crystallize into six different forms (I to VI) depending on temperature history. Only **Form V** is **shelf-stable**, giving the chocolate its characteristic **gloss, snap, sharp melting point (33.8 °C), and easy demolding**.

**Temperature-Time Profiles:**

| Chocolate Type | Melt | Cool To | Reheat To |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dark | 45 °C | 27 °C | 32 °C |
| Milk | 40 °C | 26 °C | 30 °C |
| White | 38 °C | 26 °C | 29 °C |

The **45 °C melt** destroys all pre-existing crystal nuclei (Form V has melting point 33.8 °C, so 45 °C fully liquidizes it).
The **27 °C cool** brings the chocolate into the nucleation range of unstable Forms III and IV.
The **32 °C reheat** selectively **melts the unstable forms** (which have lower melting points) while **preserving the higher-melting Form V nuclei** as seeds. These seed crystals then propagate throughout the mass during cooling in the mold.

**Why Form V is Preferred:**
1. **Thermodynamically stable** → shelf-life up to 2 years without bloom.
2. **Sharp melting point (33.8 °C)** → melt-in-mouth feel below body temperature.
3. **Glossy surface** due to uniform crystal alignment.
4. **Good contraction** on cooling → easy demolding.
5. **Audible snap** when broken → quality signature.

Failure to temper results in **Forms I–IV**, which slowly transform into **Form VI** over weeks — the bar develops **fat bloom, dullness, and a sandy texture**. **(7 Marks — full marks for explaining principle, citing all three tempering profiles, and giving at least four reasons for Form V preference)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of each ingredient** used in chocolate formulation. What are the standard percentage ranges for dark chocolate? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Casson viscosity model** for chocolate. How does **lecithin** act as a viscosity-reducing emulsifier? **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✍️ Model Answer to Question B(a)

Chocolate formulation involves six functional ingredient categories:

**1. Cocoa Mass / Liquor (45–75%)** — Provides **cocoa solids** (color, flavor, polyphenols) and **cocoa butter** (fat). It is the flavor backbone.

**2. Cocoa Butter (20–30% added)** — The continuous **fat phase** that carries flavor, enables smooth flow, and forms the crystalline matrix on tempering.

**3. Sugar (20–40%)** — Provides **sweetness** and reduces overall fat requirement. Powdered sugar is preferred for faster refining.

**4. Lecithin (0.3–0.5%)** — A natural **phospholipid emulsifier** extracted from soy. Reduces **yield stress and Casson viscosity** by 30–50%, enabling significant fat reduction.

**5. PGPR (0.05–0.2%)** — A secondary synthetic emulsifier; even more potent than lecithin at flow modification.

**6. Vanillin / Flavor (0.05–0.1%)** — Synthetic or natural vanilla flavor. Enhances and rounds off the chocolate aroma.

**7. Milk Solids (0–25%)** — Used in milk chocolate; provide **Maillard reaction precursors** during conching, yielding the characteristic caramel notes.

**Standard Percentage Ranges for Dark Chocolate:**

| Component | Range (%) |
|---|---|
| Cocoa Solids (non-fat) | 45–75 |
| Cocoa Butter (total fat) | 25–35 |
| Sugar | 20–40 |
| Lecithin | 0.3–0.5 |
| Vanilla | 0.05–0.1 |

The mass balance validates as: $\text{Fat} + \text{Non-fat solids} + \text{Sugar} + \text{Emulsifier} \approx 100\%$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✍️ Model Answer to Question B(b)

**Casson Viscosity Model:**
Chocolate is a **non-Newtonian, shear-thinning** fluid with a definite **yield stress**. Its flow behavior is described empirically by the **Casson equation**:

$$\eta_{ca} = \frac{\left(\sigma^{1/2} - \sigma_{y}^{1/2}\right)}{\dot{\gamma}^{1/2}}$$

where:
- $\eta_{ca}$ = Casson viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\sigma$ = applied shear stress (Pa)
- $\sigma_y$ = yield stress (Pa) — minimum stress to initiate flow
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

A **well-tempered dark chocolate** has $\sigma_y \leq 20$ Pa and Casson viscosity in the range **2–5 Pa·s**. Above the yield stress, the structure breaks down and viscosity drops sharply (shear-thinning).

**Role of Lecithin as Emulsifier:**
Lecithin is an **amphiphilic molecule** with a hydrophilic phosphate head and two lipophilic fatty acid tails.

1. During conching, lecithin molecules orient such that their **heads adsorb onto sugar and cocoa particle surfaces** while their **tails dissolve in cocoa butter**.
2. This creates a **molecular bridge** between solid particles and the fat phase, **preventing particle aggregation**.
3. The steric/electrostatic repulsion lowers the **inter-particle friction** and **yield stress**.
4. Practically, **0.3% lecithin reduces Casson viscosity by 30–50%**, allowing manufacturers to **reduce added cocoa butter** — a major cost saving.
5. Beyond **0.5%**, viscosity reduction plateaus and may reverse (over-emulsification causes clumping). **(7 Marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on chocolate-preparation questions:**
> 1. **Confusing fat bloom with sugar bloom.** Fat bloom = migration of cocoa butter to surface (re-crystallization issue). Sugar bloom = moisture-induced dissolution and re-crystallization of sucrose on surface. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this swap.
> 2. **Writing tempering temperatures incorrectly.** A common mistake is stating "melt to 50 °C" — the correct melt is **45 °C for dark**, never higher (excessive heat scorches milk solids and degrades flavor).
> 3. **Forgetting to mention particle size.** Many students describe conching without mentioning the **< 30 µm refining target** — this is worth a full mark.
> 4. **Skipping the "Casson yield stress" concept.** Examiners frequently test viscosity modeling; a bare mention of "viscosity" without Casson model loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Incorrect mold temperature.** Writing "cool in refrigerator" guarantees bloom formation. Always state **18–20 °C controlled cooling**.
> 6. **No mention of mass balance / yield calculation.** A 14-mark question without yield calculation loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition**: Chocolate = suspension of non-fat solids in cocoa butter, stabilized by emulsifier.
- [x] **Six stages**: Pre-mix → Refine → Conche → Temper → Mold → Cool.
- [x] **Refining target particle size**: **< 30 µm** (palate detection threshold).
- [x] **Conching conditions**: **50–60 °C, 30–45 min** in lab (4–72 hrs industry).
- [x] **Lecithin function**: Reduces yield stress + Casson viscosity by 30–50%.
- [x] **Tempering profile (Dark)**: **45 °C → 27 °C → 32 °C**.
- [x] **Tempering profile (Milk)**: **40 °C → 26 °C → 30 °C**.
- [x] **Tempering profile (White)**: **38 °C → 26 °C → 29 °C**.
- [x] **Form V (β₂)** is the **stable polymorph**, melts at **33.8 °C**.
- [x] **Fat bloom** = unstable polymorphs I–IV crystallizing on surface.
- [x] **Sugar bloom** = moisture-induced sucrose recrystallization.
- [x] **Casson equation**: $\eta_{ca} = \left(\sigma^{1/2} - \sigma_y^{1/2}\right) / \dot{\gamma}^{1/2}$.
- [x] **Cooling condition**: **18–20 °C, 25 min** (NEVER refrigerate).
- [x] **Final moisture**: **< 1.0%**.
- [x] **Standard dark chocolate composition**: Cocoa solids 45–75%, sugar 20–40%, fat 25–35%.
- [x] **Yield calculation**: (Demolded mass / Total poured mass) × 100 ≥ **95%**.
- [x] **Snap test** = quality signature of well-tempered chocolate.
- [x] **Glossy surface** = uniform Form V crystals aligned at surface.
- [x] **Van Houten (1828)** — hydraulic press; **Lindt (1879)** — conche; **Peter (1875)** — milk chocolate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Osmotic Dehydration of Amla/Gooseberry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Osmotic Dehydration of Amla/Gooseberry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Osmotic Dehydration (OD)** is a water removal process in which cellular materials (whole, pieces, or sliced fruits/vegetables) are placed in a concentrated hypertonic solution of sugar, salt, or a combination of both, leading to two simultaneous counter-current mass transfer flows: **water migrating out of the tissue into the osmotic medium**, and **solutes from the medium infiltrating into the tissue**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with Food Process Engineering (PCFTL507), osmotic dehydration is classified as a **partial dewatering / pre-treatment process** that reduces water activity ($a_w$) to an intermediate level (typically **0.80 – 0.95**) — not low enough for microbial stability on its own, but sufficient as a preliminary step before drying, freezing, or minimal processing.

For **Amla (Phyllanthus emblica / Indian Gooseberry)**, osmotic dehydration is performed using a **sucrose syrup solution (40°–70° Brix)** since amla is highly acidic, low in sugars, and rich in ascorbic acid (Vitamin C). The mild thermal and osmotic stress preserves the nutraceutical profile while partially removing moisture and infusing sugars.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍎

> [!NOTE]
> **"Imagine a wet sponge floating in a bowl of thick sugar syrup."**

Think of amla tissue as a **sponge filled with water** (the cellular juice). When you drop this sponge into a **concentrated sugar syrup**, two things happen simultaneously:

1. **Water rushes OUT of the sponge** into the syrup, because nature always tries to balance concentrations (osmosis).
2. **Sugar slowly seeps IN** through the sponge's porous membrane, because sugar molecules are small enough to penetrate.

This is exactly what happens at the cellular level in amla during osmotic dehydration. The cell membrane acts as a *semi-permeable barrier*, allowing water to escape faster than solutes enter — this selective nature is the **beauty of osmotic dehydration**.

| Component | Direction | Driving Force |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Water | Out (tissue → syrup) | Water activity gradient |
| Solutes (sugar) | In (syrup → tissue) | Concentration gradient |
| Native solutes (Vit C, acids) | Out (tissue → syrup) | Leaching (minor) |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard engineering metrics govern osmotic dehydration of amla:
> - **Osmotic solution concentration:** 40°–70° Brix (sucrose)
> - **Process temperature:** 30°C–60°C (food-grade range)
> - **Immersion time:** 30 min – 6 hours
> - **Fruit-to-solution ratio:** 1:4 to 1:10 (w/w)
> - **Equilibrium water activity ($a_w$):** 0.80 – 0.95
> - **Sucrose molecular weight:** 342.30 g/mol
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** 101.325 kPa (for any boiling point corrections)
> - **Density of water at 25°C:** 0.997 g/cm³

---

## 1.4 Why Osmotic Dehydration for Amla?

> [!NOTE]
> **Amla's Unique Processing Challenges:**
> - Extremely **high Vitamin C content** (300–900 mg/100g) — heat-labile.
> - **High acidity** (pH ~2.0–2.5) — limits microbial growth but affects texture.
> - **Tannin-rich** — leads to astringency if over-processed.
> - **Thin skin, fibrous** — needs careful osmotic penetration control.

Osmotic dehydration solves these challenges by being a **low-temperature, low-energy** process that retains color, vitamins, and antioxidant activity far better than conventional air-drying.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

Osmotic dehydration is governed by the **thermodynamic principle of chemical potential equilibrium** across a semi-permeable membrane. When the amla tissue (lower solute concentration) contacts the hypertonic sucrose syrup (higher solute concentration):

$$\mu_{w}^{tissue} > \mu_{w}^{syrup}$$

This chemical potential difference drives water from the tissue to the syrup until equilibrium is approached.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass Transfer Mechanisms Occurring During Osmotic Dehydration of Amla:**

1. **Water Outflow (Primary mechanism)**
   - Water moves from amla cells → syrup due to osmotic pressure gradient.
   - Rate depends on cell membrane permeability and syrup concentration.

2. **Solute Inflow (Secondary mechanism)**
   - Sucrose molecules diffuse from syrup → amla tissue.
   - Limited by molecular size and membrane selectivity.

3. **Leaching of Native Solutes (Minor mechanism)**
   - Ascorbic acid, organic acids, and minerals leach out into the syrup.
   - This is undesirable — must be minimized to retain nutritional value.

4. **Tissue Shrinkage and Structural Compaction**
   - As water leaves, the tissue collapses, reducing porosity.
   - This creates a "case-hardening" effect that later helps in air-drying.

---

## 2.3 Key Engineering Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range for Amla |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Brix of osmotic solution | $B$ | 40°–70° |
| Temperature | $T$ | 30°C–60°C |
| Immersion time | $t$ | 0.5–6 h |
| Fruit-to-solution ratio | $R_{FS}$ | 1:4 to 1:10 |
| Solution pH | $pH$ | 3.0–4.0 (adjusted with citric acid) |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on osmotic dehydration.**

### **Table 2.1: Core Osmotic Dehydration Formulas**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Initial Moisture Content | $M_0 = \frac{m_0 - m_{d,0}}{m_0} \times 100$ | % w.b. |
| 2 | Final Moisture Content | $M_f = \frac{m_f - m_{d,f}}{m_f} \times 100$ | % w.b. |
| 3 | **Water Loss (WL)** | $WL = m_0 \, z_0^{w} - m_f \, z_f^{w}$ | g |
| 4 | **Solid Gain (SG)** | $SG = m_f \, z_f^{s} - m_0 \, z_0^{s}$ | g |
| 5 | **Weight Reduction (WR)** | $WR = WL - SG$ | g |
| 6 | Normalized Water Loss | $WL_n = \frac{WL}{m_0} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Normalized Solid Gain | $SG_n = \frac{SG}{m_0} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Moisture Ratio | $MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Effective Diffusivity (Fick's 2nd Law) | $\ln(MR) = \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2} \, t$ | – |
| 10 | Water Activity (Norrish equation) | $a_w = X_w \cdot \exp(-K \cdot X_s^2)$ | – |
| 11 | Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff) | $\pi = i \, C \, R \, T$ | Pa |
| 12 | Density of sucrose solution | $\rho_{syrup} = f(^{\circ}\text{Brix}, T)$ | kg/m³ |

Where:
- $m_0$ = initial mass of amla sample (g)
- $m_f$ = final mass after OD (g)
- $z_0^w, z_f^w$ = initial and final mass fractions of water
- $z_0^s, z_f^s$ = initial and final mass fractions of solids
- $L$ = characteristic half-thickness of amla piece (m)
- $D_{eff}$ = effective moisture diffusivity (m²/s)
- $i$ = van't Hoff factor, $C$ = molar concentration, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Osmotic Dehydration

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications in the Food Industry:**
> - **Intermediate moisture foods (IMF):** Candied amla, amla preserve (murabba).
> - **Pre-treatment before air-drying:** Reduces drying load by 30–50%, saves energy.
> - **Pre-treatment before freeze-drying:** Improves texture retention.
> - **Sugar-infused functional snacks:** Amla candy, amla bars.
> - **Retention of heat-sensitive nutrients:** Vitamin C, polyphenols preserved >80%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Moisture Diffusivity from Fick's Second Law

Osmotic dehydration kinetics follow Fick's second law of diffusion for a slab geometry. For an amla slice with characteristic half-thickness $L$, the moisture ratio $MR$ is given by:

$$MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2} \, t\right)$$

For long immersion times, only the first term ($n=0$) of the series is significant:

$$MR = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2} \, t\right)$$

**Step 1:** Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln(MR) = \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2} \, t$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $D_{eff}$:

$$\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2} \, t = \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \ln(MR)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the effective diffusivity:

$$\boxed{D_{eff} = \frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2 \, t} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \ln(MR) \right]}$$

**Step 4 (Practical form):** Since $\ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) = -0.2100$, this becomes:

$$D_{eff} = \frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2 \, t} \left[ -0.2100 - \ln(MR) \right]$$

**Step 5 (Graphical form):** A plot of $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ yields a straight line with slope:

$$\text{slope} = -\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_{eff} = -\frac{4 L^2 \, \times \, \text{slope}}{\pi^2}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Typical 7-Mark KTU Numerical):**
> *500 g of fresh amla slices (initial moisture content 85% w.b.) are immersed in 2.5 kg of 60°Brix sucrose solution at 45°C for 4 hours. After treatment, the amla mass is 380 g and moisture content is 70% w.b. Calculate Water Loss (WL), Solid Gain (SG), and Weight Reduction (WR).*

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Determine initial dry mass and water mass**

$$m_0 = 500 \text{ g}, \quad M_0 = 0.85$$

$$m_{d,0} = m_0 \times (1 - M_0) = 500 \times 0.15 = 75 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{w,0} = m_0 \times M_0 = 500 \times 0.85 = 425 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Determine final dry mass and water mass**

$$m_f = 380 \text{ g}, \quad M_f = 0.70$$

$$m_{d,f} = m_f \times (1 - M_f) = 380 \times 0.30 = 114 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{w,f} = m_f \times M_f = 380 \times 0.70 = 266 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Water Loss (WL)**

$$WL = m_{w,0} - m_{w,f} = 425 - 266 = 159 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Solid Gain (SG)**

$$SG = m_{d,f} - m_{d,0} = 114 - 75 = 39 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Weight Reduction (WR)**

$$WR = WL - SG = 159 - 39 = 120 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Normalized Values**

$$WL_n = \frac{159}{500} \times 100 = 31.8\%$$

$$SG_n = \frac{39}{500} \times 100 = 7.8\%$$

$$WR_n = \frac{120}{500} \times 100 = 24.0\%$$

### **Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{WL = 159 \text{ g}, \quad SG = 39 \text{ g}, \quad WR = 120 \text{ g}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Variables on WL & SG

| Variable | Effect on Water Loss (WL) | Effect on Solid Gain (SG) | Optimal for Amla |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| ↑ Brix (40°→70°) | ↑↑ Strong increase | ↑ Moderate increase | 60° Brix |
| ↑ Temperature (30°→60°C) | ↑ Increase | ↑↑ Strong increase | 45–50°C |
| ↑ Time (0.5→6 h) | ↑↑ Increases (asymptotic) | ↑ Linear increase | 3–4 h |
| ↑ Fruit:Solution Ratio | ↓ Slight decrease | ↑ Increase | 1:6 |
| ↑ Agitation | ↑ Mild increase | ↓ Mild decrease | 100 rpm |
| Smaller piece size | ↑↑ Rapid initial WL | ↑↑ Rapid SG | 5 mm slices |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"weight reduction"** with **"water loss."** Weight reduction is **NOT** equal to water loss — it equals (Water Loss − Solid Gain). Always derive WR after computing both WL and SG separately.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Verification: Mass Balance Check

For the above numerical problem, verify mass balance closure:

$$\Delta m_{system} = m_0 - m_f = 500 - 380 = 120 \text{ g}$$

This must equal $WR = WL - SG = 159 - 39 = 120 \text{ g}$. ✅ **Mass balance verified.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Osmotic Dehydration of Amla

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Amla Fruits] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Washing in Clean Water]
    C --> D[Peeling and Slicing 5 mm thickness]
    D --> E[Blanching 80°C for 3 min]
    E --> F[Cooling to Room Temperature]
    F --> G[Preparation of 60 Brix Sucrose Syrup]
    G --> H[Addition of 0.1 percent Citric Acid and 0.05 percent KMS]
    H --> I[Immersion of Amla in Syrup Ratio 1 colon 6]
    I --> J[Osmotic Dehydration at 45°C for 4 hours]
    J --> K[Removal and Draining of Syrup]
    K --> L[Rinsing with Cold Water]
    L --> M[Surface Water Removal by Tissue]
    M --> N[Osmotically Dehydrated Amla Product]
    N --> O[Quality Analysis WL SG WR aw]
    O --> P[Packaging and Storage]

    style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style J fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style N fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style P fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.2 Mass Transfer Schematic — Counter-Current Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Amla Tissue Cell] -- Water Outflow --> B1[Sucrose Syrup]
    B1 -- Sucrose Inflow --> A1
    A1 -. Vitamin C Leaching .-> B1
    B1 -. Minimal Water Inflow .-> A1

    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style B1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Solid arrows = major mass transfer fluxes. Dotted arrows = minor/leaching phenomena. The asymmetric fluxes (water out ≫ solute in) are the kinetic signature of osmotic dehydration.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Osmotic Dehydration System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input_Stage[Input Stage]
        IN1[Fresh Amla Fruits]
        IN2[Sucrose Solution 60 Brix]
        IN3[Citric Acid pH Adjuster]
    end

    subgraph Process_Unit[Process Unit]
        PU1[Tank with Agitator]
        PU2[Temperature Controller 45°C]
        PU3[Timer Controller 4 h]
    end

    subgraph Output_Stage[Output Stage]
        OUT1[Osmotically Dehydrated Amla]
        OUT2[Spent Syrup Lower Brix]
    end

    subgraph QA_Stage[Quality Assurance]
        QA1[Moisture Content]
        QA2[Water Activity aw]
        QA3[Vitamin C Retention]
        QA4[Texture Profile]
    end

    IN1 --> PU1
    IN2 --> PU1
    IN3 --> PU1
    PU1 --> PU2
    PU1 --> PU3
    PU1 --> OUT1
    PU1 --> OUT2
    OUT1 --> QA1
    OUT1 --> QA2
    OUT1 --> QA3
    OUT1 --> QA4

    style PU1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style OUT1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style QA_Stage fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selection of Process Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{Product Type?}
    S -->|Amla Candy| P1[60 Brix, 50°C, 5 h]
    S -->|Amla Preserve| P2[50 Brix, 40°C, 8 h]
    S -->|Pre-drying| P3[45 Brix, 45°C, 3 h]
    S -->|IMF Snack| P4[65 Brix, 55°C, 6 h]

    P1 --> Q1[Target aw below 0.85]
    P2 --> Q2[Target aw below 0.90]
    P3 --> Q3[Subsequent air-drying at 60°C]
    P4 --> Q4[Direct packaging]

    style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style P1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style P2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style P3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style P4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define osmotic dehydration. List two advantages over conventional hot-air drying.**

**Model Answer:**
> Osmotic dehydration is a process of partial water removal from plant tissues by direct contact with a hypertonic solution (typically sugar or salt), exploiting the semi-permeable nature of cell membranes to drive water outwards via osmotic pressure gradient.
>
> **Advantages over hot-air drying:**
> 1. Operates at low temperatures (30–60°C), preserving heat-sensitive nutrients like **Vitamin C** in amla.
> 2. Lower energy consumption since no phase change (evaporation) is required during the osmotic step itself.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the roles of sucrose and citric acid in the osmotic dehydration of amla.**

**Model Answer:**
> - **Sucrose** acts as the osmotic agent creating the hypertonic gradient that drives water out of amla tissue.
> - **Citric acid** lowers the pH of the syrup (3.0–4.0), inhibits microbial growth, and acts as an antioxidant synergist to retain ascorbic acid.

---

## 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

> **Question (14 Marks):**
> *(a)* Explain the principle, mechanism, and mass transfer phenomena involved in osmotic dehydration of amla. Discuss the effect of syrup concentration and temperature on water loss and solid gain. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* 1 kg of fresh amla slices (initial moisture 82% w.b.) undergoes osmotic dehydration in 60°Brix sucrose syrup at 45°C for 4 hours. The final mass is 720 g with moisture content 68% w.b. Calculate: (i) Water Loss, (ii) Solid Gain, (iii) Weight Reduction, (iv) Normalized Water Loss percentage. **(7 Marks)**

---
