### **Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Osmotic dehydration exploits the chemical potential gradient between the dilute cell sap of amla tissue and the concentrated sucrose syrup. Water migrates from low solute concentration (tissue) to high solute concentration (syrup).
>
> **Mechanism:** Cell membrane acts as a semi-permeable barrier allowing water to exit while restricting large solute molecules.
>
> **Mass Transfer Phenomena:** Two simultaneous counter-current flows — water outflow and solute inflow — with minor leaching of native solutes.
>
> **Effect of Syrup Concentration:** Increasing Brix (40°→70°) raises osmotic pressure, increasing both WL and SG. However, at very high Brix, the syrup becomes too viscous, reducing mass transfer rate.
>
> **Effect of Temperature:** Higher temperature (30°→60°C) increases membrane permeability and reduces syrup viscosity, accelerating both WL and SG. For amla, optimum is **45–50°C** to avoid thermal degradation of Vitamin C.

---

### **Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $m_0 = 1000$ g, $M_0 = 0.82$
- $m_f = 720$ g, $M_f = 0.68$

**Step 1: Initial dry and water mass**

$$m_{d,0} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.82) = 180 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{w,0} = 1000 \times 0.82 = 820 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Final dry and water mass**

$$m_{d,f} = 720 \times (1 - 0.68) = 230.4 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{w,f} = 720 \times 0.68 = 489.6 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Water Loss**

$$WL = m_{w,0} - m_{w,f} = 820 - 489.6 = 330.4 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4: Solid Gain**

$$SG = m_{d,f} - m_{d,0} = 230.4 - 180 = 50.4 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5: Weight Reduction**

$$WR = WL - SG = 330.4 - 50.4 = 280.0 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6: Normalized Water Loss**

$$WL_n = \frac{330.4}{1000} \times 100 = 33.04\%$$

### **Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{WL = 330.4 \text{ g}, \quad SG = 50.4 \text{ g}, \quad WR = 280.0 \text{ g}, \quad WL_n = 33.04\%}$$

---

## 5.3 PART B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

> **Question (14 Marks):**
> *(a)* With a neat process flow diagram, describe the laboratory procedure for osmotic dehydration of amla. Mention the role of KMS (Potassium Metabisulphite) in the syrup. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* Derive the expression for effective moisture diffusivity ($D_{eff}$) from Fick's second law for an amla slab. If $D_{eff} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s, half-thickness $L = 4$ mm, and moisture ratio $MR = 0.35$, calculate the time required for osmotic dehydration. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Procedure:**
> 1. Select uniform, ripe amla fruits; sort, wash, peel, and slice into 5 mm thickness.
> 2. Blanch slices in hot water (80°C, 3 min) to inactivate enzymes; cool immediately.
> 3. Prepare sucrose syrup at 60°Brix, add 0.1% citric acid and 0.05% KMS.
> 4. Immerse amla slices in syrup at 1:6 ratio in a stainless steel tank.
> 5. Maintain temperature at 45°C using a water bath with controlled agitation.
> 6. Hold for 4 hours; remove, drain, rinse with cold water, and blot surface moisture.
> 7. Record weights for mass balance; analyze for moisture, water activity, and Vitamin C.
>
> **Role of KMS:** Potassium Metabisulphite releases SO₂ which inhibits microbial growth and prevents non-enzymatic browning by inhibiting Maillard reactions — critical for retaining the pale yellow-green color of amla.

---

### **Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 Marks**

**Derivation (already shown in Section 3.1):**

$$D_{eff} = \frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2 \, t} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \ln(MR) \right]$$

Rearranging for time $t$:

$$t = \frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2 \, D_{eff}} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \ln(MR) \right]$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** $D_{eff} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s, $L = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $MR = 0.35$

**Step 1:** Compute $L^2$:

$$L^2 = (4 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 16 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute the logarithmic bracket:

$$\ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) = \ln(0.8106) = -0.2100$$

$$\ln(0.35) = -1.0498$$

$$\text{Bracket} = -0.2100 - (-1.0498) = 0.8398$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the time equation:

$$t = \frac{4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{9.8696 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.8398$$

$$t = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{2.4674 \times 10^{-8}} \times 0.8398$$

$$t = 2593.6 \times 0.8398 = 2178 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to hours:

$$t = \frac{2178}{3600} = 0.605 \text{ hours} \approx 36.3 \text{ minutes}$$

### **Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{t \approx 0.605 \text{ hours} \approx 36.3 \text{ minutes}}$$

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Osmotic Dehydration Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing moisture content basis:** KTU often specifies **% w.b. (wet basis)** — students wrongly convert to **% d.b. (dry basis)**. Always check.
> 2. **Mistaking WL for WR:** Weight Reduction = WL − SG, NOT just WL. Many students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract leaching losses:** Native solute loss must be considered when calculating SG from dry mass changes.
> 4. **Wrong unit for $D_{eff}$:** Effective diffusivity is always in **m²/s**, not mm²/s. Conversion factor: $1 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.
> 5. **Ignoring $a_w$ reduction:** Many students compute mass balance but forget to comment on **water activity reduction** — a key 2-mark concept.
> 6. **Plotting MR vs. t incorrectly:** Should be $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ for linear regression.

---

## 5.5 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Osmotic Dehydration of Amla:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Partial dewatering via osmotic gradient across semi-permeable cell membrane.
- ✅ **Two simultaneous fluxes:** Water outflow (major) + Solute inflow (minor).
- ✅ **Key metrics:** WL, SG, WR, $a_w$, $D_{eff}$.
- ✅ **Master formulas:** $WL = m_0 z_0^w - m_f z_f^w$; $SG = m_f z_f^s - m_0 z_0^s$; $WR = WL - SG$.
- ✅ **Fick's law form:** $\ln(MR) = \ln(8/\pi^2) - (\pi^2 D_{eff}/4L^2)t$.
- ✅ **Optimal conditions for amla:** 60°Brix, 45°C, 4 h, 1:6 ratio.
- ✅ **Additives in syrup:** Sucrose (osmotic agent), citric acid (pH + antioxidant), KMS (microbial + browning control).
- ✅ **Typical values:** WL ≈ 30–35%, SG ≈ 5–10%, WR ≈ 20–25%, $D_{eff} \approx 10^{-9}$ m²/s.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Low temperature, low energy, retains Vitamin C (>80%), pre-treatment for drying.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Cannot reduce $a_w$ below ~0.80; requires post-drying for shelf stability.
- ✅ **Mass balance closure:** $\Delta m_{system} = m_0 - m_f = WR = WL - SG$.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Amla candy, murabba, IMF snacks, pre-dried amla powder feedstock.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Jam, Jelly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Preparation of Jam, Jelly # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Jam** is a concentrated fruit product prepared by boiling sound, ripe fruit pulp with sugar (sucrose) to a thick, spreadable consistency, where the fruit pieces remain largely intact and distributed throughout a gel-like matrix formed by pectin–sugar–acid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Jelly** is a clear, transparent, sparkling fruit preserve prepared by boiling clarified fruit juice (free of suspended pulp) with sugar and acid, such that the final product sets into a firm, quivering gel held together by a continuous pectin–sugar–acid network. Both products are **semi-preserved intermediate moisture foods** relying on the principle of high-solutes preservation, where the combined effect of high sugar concentration (achieving a water activity $a_w < 0.85$) and low pH (3.0–3.5) inhibits microbial proliferation, particularly yeasts, moulds, and spore-forming bacteria. ## 1.2 Classification Under KTU 2024 Syllabus | Product | Base Material | Texture | Pectin Requirement | Final Soluble Solids (TSS) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Jam** | Whole/Pulp of fruit | Thick, with fruit bits | Moderate | $68°$ Brix $\pm 1°$ | | **Jelly** | Filtered juice | Clear, sparkling gel | High | $68°$ Brix $\pm 1°$ | | **Marmalade** | Pulp + peel shreds | Jam with peel | Moderate | $68°$ Brix $\pm 1°$ | | **Preserve** | Whole fruit pieces | Fruit in syrup | Variable | $68°$ Brix $\pm 1°$ | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **sandcastle on a beach**. A jelly is like a **delicate glass goblet** — the pectin forms invisible "scaffolding rods" (like steel rods in concrete), the sugar acts as the **concrete filler** that locks the structure, and the acid is the **foreman** that decides exactly when the setting reaction hardens everything. Too little acid → the rods never crosslink (soupy jelly). Too much acid → the gel shrinks and weeps water (syneresis, like an over-dried sandcastle crumbling). > > A **jam** is the same castle, but you deliberately leave a few **whole bricks (fruit pieces) embedded inside the concrete** for texture and flavor bursts. ## 1.4 Key Physicochemical Constants - **Brix (% TSS) of finished product:** $68\%$ minimum - **pH of finished product:** $3.0$ – $3.5$ - **Water activity ($a_w$):** $0.82$ – $0.86$ - **Pectin-sugar-acid ratio (classic):** $1 : 150 : 1$ (pectin : sugar : acid by mass) - **Pectin content required in fruit juice:** $0.5\%$ – $1.5\%$ for adequate gel formation - **Boiling temperature at finish point:** $104°$C – $105°$C (at sea level) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Tripartite Gel Theory — Pectin + Sugar + Acid > [!NOTE] > A fruit jelly sets because of a **delicate three-way handshake** between pectin, sugar, and acid. Disrupting any one of them breaks the gel. ### 2.1.1 Role of Pectin (The Polymer Backbone) Pectin is a **high-molecular-weight polysaccharide** (chiefly polygalacturonic acid with methyl ester groups). It is classified by its degree of esterification (DE): - **High-Methoxy (HM) Pectin:** DE > 50% → Requires sugar + acid to gel (used in jams/jellies) - **Low-Methoxy (LM) Pectin:** DE < 50% → Gels with calcium ions (used in low-sugar products) The HM pectin molecules in acidic medium lose their negative charges (COO⁻ → COOH), allowing them to approach each other and form junction zones via **hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions**. ### 2.1.2 Role of Sugar (The Dehydrating Agent) Sugar competes with pectin for water molecules. By **tying up free water**, it: 1. Reduces water activity ($a_w$) 2. Increases the effective concentration of pectin 3. Promotes pectin-pectin association through hydrophobic interactions between methyl ester groups Without sugar, pectin remains hydrated and dispersed → no gel forms. ### 2.1.3 Role of Acid (The pH Trigger) Acid performs two critical functions: 1. **Neutralizes the carboxyl groups** of pectin, reducing electrostatic repulsion between polymer chains 2. **Optimizes gelation at pH 3.0–3.5** (this is the **isoelectric zone** of pectin gelation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **SHEAR Test (Sheet/Rake Test):** A drop of boiling jelly poured on a cold plate should **sheet off the spoon as a continuous sheet** rather than running as droplets — this indicates proper setting point. ## 2.2 The Pectin-Sugar-Acid Triangle $$\boxed{\text{Gel Strength} \propto \frac{[\text{Pectin}] \times [\text{Sugar}]}{[\text{Acid} \text{ range}]}}$$ Subject to the boundary conditions: - pH must lie in $3.0 \leq \text{pH} \leq 3.5$ - Sugar concentration must exceed $60\%$ w/w - Pectin must be present at $\geq 0.5\%$ ## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Pectin Yield Calculation | $P_y = \dfrac{m_{\text{pectin}}}{V_{\text{juice}}} \times 100$ | % (w/v) | Determines if juice has enough pectin to set | | Brix Correction | $B_f = B_i + (0.5 \times \Delta T)$ | °Brix | Temperature correction for hot refractometer readings | | TSS Final Target | $B_f = 68 \pm 1$ | °Brix | Industry standard for jam/jelly | | Yield of Finished Product | $Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{fruit}}}{m_{\text{final product}}}$ | dimensionless | Mass balance calculation | | Pectin:Sugar Ratio | $R_{ps} = \dfrac{m_{\text{pectin}}}{m_{\text{sugar}}}$ | dimensionless | Should be $\approx 1:150$ to $1:200$ | | pH Adjustment | $\Delta\text{pH} = -\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[H^+]_f}{[H^+]_i}\right)$ | pH units | Acid addition calculations | | Water Activity (approx) | $a_w \approx 1 - 0.01 \times B$ | dimensionless | For high-sugar systems (Grover's approx) | | Acidity Requirement | $A = 0.5\% - 1.5\%$ | % citric acid | Minimum TA for gelation | | Setting Point (Temp) | $T_{set} = 104.5 - 105.5$ | °C | Endpoint temperature at sea level | | Endpoint (Sheet Test) | Length $\geq 5$ cm drop | cm | Visual gel strength indicator | ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering The jam/jelly process is a **classical unit operation in food process engineering** involving: - **Heat transfer** (conduction/convection in boiling) - **Mass transfer** (water evaporation concentrating TSS) - **Rheology** (gel formation kinetics) - **Microbial inactivation** (yeasts/moulds inhibition) Industrial production uses **vacuum evaporators** to concentrate at lower temperatures ($60°$C – $70°$C), preserving colour, flavour, and vitamin C. KTU examiners frequently test the **material and energy balance** of this operation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Complete Standardized Protocol (KTU Board Pattern) ### A. Preparation of JAM — Step-by-Step **Step 1: Fruit Selection and Sorting** Select sound, ripe fruit (e.g., mango, apple, mixed fruit). Discard damaged, overripe, or diseased pieces. Wash thoroughly under running potable water to reduce surface microbial load from $10^5$–$10^6$ CFU/g to $10^2$–$10^3$ CFU/g. **Step 2: Peeling and Pulping** Peel fruits. For soft fruits, blanch in steam for $2$–$4$ minutes at $100°$C to inactivate **pectinmethylesterase (PME)**, which would otherwise de-esterify pectin and ruin gel formation. Pass through a pulper to obtain uniform pulp. **Step 3: Formulation Calculation** For a batch of $1$ kg fruit pulp, the standard formulation is: $$m_{\text{sugar}} = m_{\text{pulp}} \times (0.8 \text{ to } 1.0)$$ $$m_{\text{pectin}} = 0.005 \times m_{\text{pulp}} = 5 \text{ g (if natural pectin is low)}$$ $$m_{\text{acid}} = 0.005 \times m_{\text{pulp}} = 5 \text{ g citric acid}$$ **Step 4: Cooking (Concentration Stage)** 1. Add pulp to a stainless steel steam-jacketed kettle (avoid copper/iron → catalyze vitamin C oxidation and colour degradation). 2. Add **half the sugar** initially → dissolves and penetrates fruit pieces → pre-osmotic equilibration prevents fruit shrinkage. 3. Heat to $70°$C with constant stirring (wooden ladle) to prevent scorching. 4. Add remaining sugar, pectin (pre-mixed with $3\times$ its weight of sugar to prevent clumping), and acid solution. 5. Boil briskly until TSS reaches $68°$Brix. **Step 5: Endpoint Determination** Apply **three simultaneous tests**: - **Brix test:** Refractometer reads $68°\pm 1$ - **Temperature test:** Thermometer reads $105°$C $\pm 1°$C - **Sheet test:** Drop sheeting $\geq 5$ cm from a cold spoon **Step 6: Filling and Cooling** Remove from heat. Skim off surface foam (it traps air → oxidative deterioration). Pour hot ($85°$C) into pre-sterilized dry glass jars. Cool at room temperature (24 h) — gel sets as it cools below $50°$C. ### B. Preparation of JELLY — Step-by-Step **Step 1: Juice Extraction** Wash, crush, and **heat the fruit to $80°$C** for $5$–$10$ minutes. This: - Inactivates enzymes (PPO, PME) - Aids in pectin solubilization from cell walls - Improves juice yield by $10\%$–$15\%$ Press through cheesecloth/jelly bag. The juice must be **brilliantly clear** — filter twice if needed. **Step 2: Pectin Test (Pre-Formulation Quality Control)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Alcohol Test (the most reliable field test):** > Mix $1$ tablespoon cooled juice with $3$ tablespoons $95\%$ ethanol (denatured spirit). If a **single gelatinous mass precipitates** that can be lifted with a spoon, pectin is adequate. If it breaks into fragments → pectin is low; if no precipitate → pectin is absent. **Step 3: Cooking the Jelly** 1. Add measured juice to the kettle. 2. Add sugar = $0.75 \times m_{\text{juice}}$ (typical). 3. Heat, stir to dissolve sugar. 4. Boil vigorously. Skim foam continuously. 5. Add citric acid solution when TSS reaches $60°$Brix (premature acid addition hydrolyses pectin). 6. Add pectin (if needed) at the same stage. 7. Cook to $68°$Brix / $105°$C. **Step 4: Hot-Pour and Set** Pour immediately into jars (no fruit pieces). Cover with **paraffin wax** (older method) or use **vacuum caps** (modern). Invert jars for $5$ min → sterilizes lid → upright to cool. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pectin Quantification and Quality Control ### Derivation: Acid Titratable Acidity (TA) Calculation Given a juice sample of $10$ mL titrated against $0.1$ N NaOH, using phenolphthalein indicator, the titre value is $V$ mL. $$\text{Acidity (\% citric acid)} = \frac{V \times N \times 0.064 \times 100}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$ Substituting the standard values: $$\text{Acidity (\% citric acid)} = \frac{V \times 0.1 \times 0.064 \times 100}{10}$$ Simplifying: $$\boxed{\text{Acidity (\% citric acid)} = V \times 0.064}$$ Where: - $0.064$ = equivalent weight of citric acid $\div 10$ - $V$ = titre volume in mL ### Derivation: Material Balance for a Jam Batch For an input of $m_f$ kg fruit pulp at TSS $= B_f$ % and $m_s$ kg sugar at $100\%$ TSS: $$m_{\text{final}} = m_f + m_s - m_{\text{evaporated water}}$$ TSS balance at endpoint ($B_{fp} = 68\%$): $$m_f \cdot B_f + m_s \cdot 100 = m_{\text{final}} \cdot 68$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{m_{\text{final}} = \frac{m_f \cdot B_f + 100 \cdot m_s}{68}}$$ **Worked Example:** $1$ kg fruit pulp ($15°$Brix) + $0.8$ kg sugar: $$m_{\text{final}} = \frac{1 \times 15 + 0.8 \times 100}{68} = \frac{15 + 80}{68} = \frac{95}{68} = 1.397 \text{ kg}$$ Water evaporated = $(1 + 0.8) - 1.397 = 0.403$ kg ## 3.3 The Hurdle Concept in Jam/Jelly Preservation $$\text{Shelf-life} = f(\text{TSS} \uparrow, \text{pH} \downarrow, \text{a}_w \downarrow, \text{Heat} \uparrow)$$ | Hurdle | Jam/Jelly | Mechanism | |---|---|---| | High TSS ($68\%$) | ✓ | Osmotic stress on microbes | | Low pH ($3.0$–$3.5$) | ✓ | Disrupts microbial metabolism | | Reduced $a_w$ ($<0.85$) | ✓ | Removes available water | | Heat treatment | ✓ | Kills vegetative cells | This multi-barrier approach is what KTU examiners call the **"Hurdle Technology"** — designed by Prof. Lothar Leistner. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Jam and Jelly Production ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Fruit Reception] --> B[Sorting and Washing] B --> C[Peeling and Pulping] C --> D{Pectin Adequate?} D -- No --> E[Add Commercial Pectin] D -- Yes --> F[Add Sugar 50%] E --> F F --> G[Heat to 70 deg C] G --> H[Add Remaining Sugar and Acid] H --> I[Boil to 68 deg Brix] I --> J{Endpoint Tests} J -- Pass --> K[Skim Foam] J -- Fail --> I K --> L[Hot Fill into Sterile Jars] L --> M[Seal and Cool] M --> N[Storage at Ambient] ``` ## 4.2 Jelly-Specific Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Whole Fruit] --> B1[Crushing and Heating 80 deg C] B1 --> C1[Pressing Through Jelly Bag] C1 --> D1[Filtration to Clarity] D1 --> E1[Alcohol Test for Pectin] E1 -- Adequate --> F1[Add Sugar to Juice] E1 -- Inadequate --> G1[Add Commercial Pectin] G1 --> F1 F1 --> H1[Boil to 60 deg Brix] H1 --> I1[Add Citric Acid] I1 --> J1[Continue to 68 deg Brix at 105 deg C] J1 --> K1[Sheet Test Pass] K1 --> L1[Pour into Sterile Jars] L1 --> M1[Seal and Label] ``` ## 4.3 The Pectin-Sugar-Acid Gel Triangle (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR PectinNode[Pectin
Polymer Network] -->|Hydrogen bonding| GelNode[Strong Gel] SugarNode[Sugar
Water Removal] -->|Dehydration| GelNode AcidNode[Acid
pH 3.0 to 3.5] -->|Charge Neutralization| GelNode PectinNode -.->|Without sugar, stays hydrated| FailNode[Liquid] SugarNode -.->|Without pectin, no network| FailNode AcidNode -.->|Wrong pH, no crosslink| FailNode ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram of the Concentration Unit Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Input_Stage[INPUT STAGE] I1[Fruit Pulp or Juice m1 kg] I2[Sugar m2 kg] I3[Acid and Pectin m3 kg] end subgraph Heat_Stage[HEAT TRANSFER STAGE] H1[Steam Jacketed Kettle Q in] H2[Boiling Temperature 104 to 105 deg C] end subgraph Mass_Stage[MASS TRANSFER STAGE] M1[Water Evaporation m evap] M2[TSS Rises to 68 percent] end subgraph Output_Stage[OUTPUT STAGE] O1[Final Product m final] O2[Condensed Water Loss] end Input_Stage --> Heat_Stage Heat_Stage --> Mass_Stage Mass_Stage --> Output_Stage ``` ## 4.5 Gel Failure Diagnosis Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Fail[Product Did Not Set] --> Q1{Pectin Present?} Q1 -- No --> AddP[Add Pectin and Re-cook] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{pH Between 3.0 and 3.5?} Q2 -- No --> AddAcid[Add Citric Acid Slowly] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Sugar More Than 60 percent?} Q3 -- No --> AddSugar[Boil Off Water or Add Sugar] Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Cooked Too Long?} Q4 -- Yes --> PectinHydrolysis[Pectin Hydrolyzed Discard Batch] Q4 -- No --> Other[Check for PME Activity or Contaminants] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **Define the term "High Methoxyl Pectin" and state its role in jam/jelly preparation.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** High Methoxyl (HM) pectin is a pectin fraction with degree of esterification (DE) greater than $50\%$, typically $60\%-75\%$. In jam/jelly preparation, HM pectin is responsible for forming the **three-dimensional gel network** when heated with sugar ($>60\%$ concentration) under acidic conditions (pH $3.0$–$3.5$). The methoxyl groups ($-COOCH_3$) participate in **hydrophobic interactions** that crosslink adjacent polymer chains, while the acid neutralizes free carboxyl groups to minimize electrostatic repulsion. **1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for DE specification, **1 mark** for mechanism in gel formation. ### Question 2 **List the standard endpoint tests for jam/jelly with their acceptance criteria.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three standard endpoint tests are: 1. **Brix (TSS) Test:** Refractometer reading of $68 \pm 1°$Brix (1 mark) 2. **Temperature Test:** Boiling mixture reaches $105°$C $\pm 1°$C at sea level (1 mark) 3. **Sheet/Rake Test:** A drop from a cold spoon forms a continuous sheet $\geq 5$ cm, not droplets (1 mark) ## 5.2 Part B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure for the preparation of **fruit jelly** in the laboratory, clearly stating the role of each ingredient used. **7 marks** **(b)** Explain the **theory of gel formation** in fruit jellies with a neat labeled diagram of the pectin-sugar-acid interaction. **7 marks** #### Model Solution for (a): 1. **Selection of fruit** (0.5 mark): Sound, ripe, fresh fruit with adequate pectin (e.g., guava, apple, sour grape). 2. **Washing and crushing** (0.5 mark): Wash thoroughly, crush into small pieces. 3. **Heat treatment** (1 mark): Heat crushed fruit to $80°$C for $5$–$10$ min to inactivate pectinmethylesterase (PME) and improve juice extraction. 4. **Juice extraction** (0.5 mark): Press through a cheesecloth/jelly bag. Filter to obtain a brilliantly clear juice. 5. **Pectin adequacy test** (1 mark): Alcohol test — $1$ tbsp juice + $3$ tbsp $95\%$ ethanol → if gelatinous mass forms, pectin is adequate; else add commercial pectin ($0.5\%$ of juice weight). 6. **Cooking** (1.5 marks): Add $0.75\times$ juice weight of sugar. Heat to dissolve. Boil. Add citric acid ($0.5\%$ of juice weight) at $60°$Brix. Continue to $68°$Brix/$105°$C. 7. **Endpoint tests** (1 mark): Brix, temperature, sheet test. 8. **Filling** (0.5 mark): Pour hot ($85°$C) into pre-sterilized dry glass jars. Seal. Cool. 9. **Role of ingredients** (0.5 mark): Sugar (dehydration, sweetness, preservation); Acid (pH adjustment, gel trigger); Pectin (gel network former). #### Model Solution for (b): 1. **Introduction to pectin chemistry** (2 marks): Pectin is a polymer of $\alpha$-(1→4)-D-galacturonic acid units, partially methyl-esterified. HM pectin has DE $> 50\%$. 2. **Three components of gelation** (1.5 marks): - Pectin: forms the polymer matrix - Sugar (sucrose): removes water, increases effective pectin concentration - Acid: reduces pH to $3.0$–$3.5$, neutralizes $-COO^-$ groups on pectin 3. **Mechanism of junction zone formation** (1.5 marks): At low pH, hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions between methoxyl groups create **junction zones** linking adjacent polymer chains, forming a three-dimensional network that traps water. 4. **Critical conditions** (1 mark): pH $3.0$–$3.5$; sugar $\geq 60\%$; pectin $\geq 0.5\%$. 5. **Labeled diagram** (1 mark): Show pectin chains, junction zones, trapped water, and sugar molecules. 6. **Conclusion** (0.5 mark): The gel is a **two-phase system** — a continuous pectin-sugar-acid network trapping a discontinuous water phase. ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Differentiate between **jam and jelly** based on (i) raw material, (ii) texture, (iii) clarity, (iv) processing steps, and (v) industrial application. **7 marks** **(b)** A laboratory batch uses $1.5$ kg of mango pulp at $14°$Brix and $1.2$ kg of sugar to prepare jam. Assuming no other solid ingredients contribute to TSS, calculate: (i) the final mass of jam produced, and (ii) the mass of water evaporated, when the endpoint TSS is $68°$Brix. **7 marks** #### Model Solution for (a): | Parameter | Jam | Jelly | |---|---|---| | **(i) Raw material** | Whole fruit pieces or pulp | Filtered, clarified fruit juice | | **(ii) Texture** | Thick, spreadable, with fruit pieces | Smooth, firm, quivering gel | | **(iii) Clarity** | Opaque/translucent | Brilliantly clear, sparkling | | **(iv) Processing** | Pulping → cooking with sugar & acid | Juice extraction → filtration → cooking | | **(v) Application** | Spreads, sandwich fillings, baking | Accompaniment, glazing, dessert toppings | **Award:** 1.4 marks per parameter (5 × 1.4 = 7 marks). #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** - $m_f = 1.5$ kg mango pulp - $B_f = 14°$Brix - $m_s = 1.2$ kg sugar - $B_{fp} = 68°$Brix - Sugar TSS = $100\%$ **Step 1: TSS balance equation** (2 marks): $$m_f \cdot B_f + m_s \cdot 100 = m_{\text{final}} \cdot B_{fp}$$ **Step 2: Substituting values** (2 marks): $$1.5 \times 14 + 1.2 \times 100 = m_{\text{final}} \times 68$$ $$21 + 120 = 68 \cdot m_{\text{final}}$$ $$141 = 68 \cdot m_{\text{final}}$$ **Step 3: Solving for $m_{\text{final}}$** (1.5 marks): $$\boxed{m_{\text{final}} = \frac{141}{68} = 2.074 \text{ kg}}$$ **Step 4: Mass of water evaporated** (1.5 marks): $$m_{\text{evap}} = m_f + m_s - m_{\text{final}}$$ $$m_{\text{evap}} = 1.5 + 1.2 - 2.074 = \boxed{0.626 \text{ kg}}$$ ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Order of ingredient addition** — Many students add acid and pectin at the start, but this is wrong. Acid is added only when TSS reaches $60°$Brix to prevent pectin hydrolysis. **(−2 marks)** > 2. **Confusing jam with jelly raw material** — Jam uses **pulp**, jelly uses **clarified juice**. Reversing this is a fatal error. **(−3 marks)** > 3. **Forgetting units in numericals** — Mass balance answers without units ($kg$, $°$Brix) lose $0.5$ mark per sub-part. > 4. **Skipping the pectin adequacy test** — Skipping the alcohol test in jelly preparation shows lack of process control knowledge. **(−1 mark)** > 5. **Endpoint test** — Stating only one test (Brix) is incomplete. Examiners expect all three tests for full credit. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** > - **Jam = Pulp + Sugar + Acid + Pectin** (if needed); sets at $68°$Brix, pH $3.0$–$3.5$ > - **Jelly = Clarified Juice + Sugar + Acid + Pectin** (if needed); sets at $68°$Brix, pH $3.0$–$3.5$ > - **Tripartite gel** requires pectin, sugar ($>60\%$), and acid (pH $3.0$–$3.5$) — **all three mandatory** > - **HM Pectin** = DE $> 50\%$; **LM Pectin** = DE $< 50\%$ (requires Ca²⁺) > - **Endpoint tests (3):** Brix ($68°\pm 1$), Temperature ($105°\pm 1°$C), Sheet Test ($\geq 5$ cm) > - **Alcohol Test:** $1$ tbsp juice + $3$ tbsp $95\%$ ethanol → gelatinous mass = pectin adequate > - **Acid addition rule:** Add citric acid only when TSS reaches $60°$Brix (prevents pectin hydrolysis) > - **Sugar addition rule:** Add half sugar initially → osmotic equilibration → prevents fruit shrinkage in jam > - **Pectin-Sugar-Acid ratio (target):** $1 : 150 : 1$ (mass basis) > - **Hurdle Technology:** TSS $\uparrow$ + pH $\downarrow$ + $a_w \downarrow$ + Heat = preservation > - **Standard ratio for fruit pulp: sugar = $1 : 0.8$ to $1 : 1$** > - **Standard ratio for fruit juice: sugar = $1 : 0.75$** > - **Common fruits for jelly:** Guava, apple, sour grape, plum (high pectin) > - **Common fruits for jam:** Mango, strawberry, pineapple, mixed fruit > - **TSS Mass balance equation:** $m_f \cdot B_f + m_s \cdot 100 = m_{\text{final}} \cdot B_{fp}$ > - **Water activity of finished product:** $a_w < 0.85$ (yeast/mould inhibition) > - **Equipment:** Steam-jacketed stainless steel kettle; avoid Cu, Fe (catalyze oxidation) > - **Fill temperature:** $85°$C minimum (vacuum seal); cool undisturbed for $24$ h

topics.title: Preparation of Paneer
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Paneer

# Preparation of Paneer — KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Notebook

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paneer (Heat-Acid Coagulated Milk Protein):**
> Paneer is defined as a **soft, unripened, non-melting cheese variety** obtained by the **thermal-acid coagulation** of standardized cow or buffalo milk (or a combination thereof), followed by draining of whey and pressing of the curd mass. As per the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (FSSAI, India)**, paneer shall contain not less than **50% milk fat** on a dry matter basis, and not more than **70% moisture** on the final product.

In food processing terminology, paneer belongs to the family of **acid-set, rennet-free fresh cheeses**, analogous to Indian *chhena*, Mexican *queso fresco*, and European *fromage frais*. The unique characteristic of paneer is that it is prepared by adding a permitted food-grade acid (citric acid, lactic acid, or sour whey) to hot milk (**70–80 °C**), causing simultaneous denaturation and aggregation of **casein micelles**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Curd in a Teacup" Analogy:**
> Imagine pouring a few drops of lemon juice into a cup of hot milk. Suddenly, white fluffy clumps form and sink, leaving a translucent greenish-yellow liquid behind. The white clumps are the **casein protein** that has clumped together (coagulated) because the acid disrupted its electrical charge, and the yellowish liquid is **whey** (water + lactose + minerals). Paneer is essentially the white clumps, washed, drained, and pressed into a block — that simple.

The chemistry: at neutral pH (~6.7), casein micelles carry a net **negative surface charge** which keeps them suspended like magnets repelling each other. When acid is added, the pH drops toward the **isoelectric point of casein (pH ≈ 4.6)**, where the charge becomes zero. The proteins lose their repulsion, clump together, and form a continuous gel network.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Moisture content** | **≤ 70%** | FSSAI Regulation 2.1.13 |
| **Milk fat (on dry basis)** | **≥ 50%** | FSSAI Regulation 2.1.13 |
| **Coagulation temperature** | **70–80 °C** | Industry Standard |
| **Isoelectric pH of casein** | **≈ 4.6** | Dairy Chemistry Reference |
| **Standard cow milk yield** | **≈ 15–17% (w/w)** | Dairy Handbook |
| **Standard buffalo milk yield** | **≈ 20–22% (w/w)** | Dairy Handbook |
| **Common acidulants** | Citric, Lactic, Sour Whey | FSSAI Permitted |

### 1.4 Course Outcome Mapping (CO Alignment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Aligned KTU 2024 Course Outcomes:**
> - **CO2:** Apply principles of food processing to prepare dairy-based traditional products.
> - **CO3:** Evaluate the effect of processing variables (temperature, acidity, pressing) on product quality.
> - **CO5:** Document laboratory experiments following standard scientific-report writing format.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Science of Heat–Acid Coagulation — Broken Down

The formation of paneer is governed by **three simultaneous physico-chemical phenomena**:

### A. Acidification (Protonation of Casein)
- Food-grade acid (commonly **citric acid at 1–2% w/v** solution) is added to hot milk.
- **H⁺ ions** donate protons to the **κ-casein** layer that stabilizes each casein micelle.
- As the pH approaches **4.6 (isoelectric point)**, the net charge of casein becomes zero.
- Electrostatic repulsion between micelles → **vanishes**.

### B. Thermal Denaturation
- Heating milk to **70–80 °C** causes **whey proteins** (β-lactoglobulin and α-lactalbumin) to denature.
- Denatured whey proteins **co-precipitate** with casein, increasing curd yield.
- Heat also drives off dissolved CO₂, lowering pH marginally and accelerating coagulation.

### C. Aggregation & Gel Network Formation
- At the isoelectric pH, casein micelles lose their colloidal stability.
- They **aggregate via calcium phosphate bridges** into a three-dimensional protein network.
- The network **entraps fat globules, water, and residual lactose**, forming the soft curd.

## 2.2 Critical Processing Variables (The Four Pillars)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner will almost always test these four variables. Memorize them.

| Variable | Optimal Range | Effect on Paneer Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **Milk type & standardization** | Cow (4.5% fat) / Buffalo (6% fat) | Buffalo → Higher yield, softer body |
| **Coagulation temperature** | **70–80 °C** | <70 °C → Soft, fragile curd; >85 °C → Hard, rubbery texture |
| **Acidulant concentration** | 1–2% citric acid solution | Excess acid → Sour, brittle, acidic flavor |
| **Pressing pressure & time** | **1–2 kg/cm² for 15–20 min** | Under-pressing → High moisture, short shelf life |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield derivations** for lab-report and viva-voce questions.

### (i) Paneer Yield (Standard Method)

$$
Y_{\text{paneer}} \; (\% \; w/w) \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{paneer}}}{M_{\text{milk}}} \times 100
$$

where $M_{\text{paneer}}$ = mass of pressed paneer (g), and $M_{\text{milk}}$ = mass of milk taken (g).

### (ii) Moisture Content of Paneer (Oven Method)

$$
\text{Moisture} \; (\%) \;=\; \frac{W_{\text{wet}} \;-\; W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{wet}}} \times 100
$$

Standard paneer must contain **moisture ≤ 70%**.

### (iii) Fat on Dry Basis (FDB)

$$
\text{FDB} \; (\%) \;=\; \frac{\% \text{Fat of paneer}}{100 \;-\; \% \text{Moisture}} \times 100
$$

Standard paneer must contain **FDB ≥ 50%**.

### (iv) Total Solids (TS) of Milk (for yield prediction)

$$
\text{TS}_{\text{milk}} \; (\%) \;=\; \frac{\text{Fat} \;+\; \text{SNF (Solid-Not-Fat)}}{100} \times 100
$$

### (v) Theoretical Paneer Yield (Pearce Equation)

$$
Y_{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; \frac{(\text{Fat \%} + \text{Casein \%}) \times 0.85}{100} \times M_{\text{milk}}
$$

The constant **0.85** accounts for the average **85% recovery** of fat and protein into the curd.

### (vi) Acid Concentration Required (Citric Acid Method)

$$
V_{\text{acid}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{milk}} \times \text{Desired Final Acidity}}{C_{\text{acid}} \times 10}
$$

A practical shortcut used in labs: **approximately 2.0–2.5 g of citric acid per litre of milk**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Understanding the principles of paneer preparation extends far beyond this single lab experiment:
> - **Industrial Dairy Plants:** Paneer production is a **₹15,000+ crore industry** in India, with manufacturers like Amul, Mother Dairy, and Britannia running automated coagulation lines.
> - **Process Scale-Up:** The same heat–acid coagulation principle is used to manufacture **chhena, ricotta, queso blanco, and tofu** (with MgCl₂ or CaSO₄ instead of acid).
> - **Waste Valorization:** Whey, the by-product, is a rich source of **lactose and whey proteins**, used in infant formula, sports nutrition, and bioethanol production.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of Paneer Yield from Standardized Milk

Let us derive the expected paneer yield from **1 litre of cow milk** standardized to **4.5% fat and 8.5% SNF** (total solids = 13.0%).

### Given Data
- Volume of milk $V_{\text{milk}}$ = **1.0 L** = **1000 mL**
- Density of milk $\rho_{\text{milk}}$ = **1.030 g/mL**
- Fat content = **4.5 % (w/w)**
- SNF content = **8.5 % (w/w)**
- Casein in SNF ≈ **80 %** of total protein, and protein ≈ **38 %** of SNF.

### Step 1 — Mass of Milk Taken

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{milk}} &= V_{\text{milk}} \times \rho_{\text{milk}} \\
&= 1000 \; \text{mL} \times 1.030 \; \text{g/mL} \\
&= 1030 \; \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Mass of Fat in the Milk

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{fat}} &= M_{\text{milk}} \times \frac{\% \text{Fat}}{100} \\
&= 1030 \times \frac{4.5}{100} \\
&= 46.35 \; \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Casein in the Milk

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{casein}} &= M_{\text{milk}} \times \text{SNF \%} \times \text{Protein in SNF \%} \times \text{Casein in Protein \%} \\
&= 1030 \times \frac{8.5}{100} \times \frac{38}{100} \times \frac{80}{100} \\
&= 1030 \times 0.085 \times 0.38 \times 0.80 \\
&= 26.62 \; \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Theoretical Paneer Yield (Pearce Equation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{(M_{\text{fat}} + M_{\text{casein}}) \times 0.85}{1} \\
&= (46.35 + 26.62) \times 0.85 \\
&= 72.97 \times 0.85 \\
&\approx 62.02 \; \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Approximately **62 g of paneer** is expected from **1 litre of cow milk** standardized to 4.5% fat and 8.5% SNF. Practical lab yields are typically **150–170 g** because paneer is pressed and contains ~55–60% moisture, while theoretical yield above represents the dry-matter basis.

### Step 5 — Practical Yield (Including Moisture)

If the final paneer contains **55% moisture**, then the dry matter is 45%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{practical}} &= \frac{Y_{\text{theoretical}}}{1 - \text{Moisture fraction}} \\
&= \frac{62.02 \; \text{g}}{1 - 0.55} \\
&= \frac{62.02}{0.45} \\
&\approx 137.8 \; \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **138 g** is consistent with the industry-reported practical yield of **130–160 g/L**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Variables on Paneer Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **favorite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE. Memorize the trends.

| Parameter Changed ↑ | Yield | Texture | Flavor | Shelf Life | Color |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Fat content of milk | **↑** Higher | Softer, smoother | Richer | **↑** Longer | Creamier white |
| ↑ Coagulation temperature (up to 80 °C) | **↑** Slightly higher | Firmer | Neutral | **↑** Longer | Slightly yellow |
| ↑ Coagulation temperature (>85 °C) | ↓ Lower | **Hard, rubbery** | Cooked | ↑ | Brownish |
| ↑ Acid concentration | ↓ Lower | Brittle, crumbly | **Sour** | ↓ Shorter | Whitish |
| ↑ Pressing pressure | ↓ Lower (less moisture) | **Hard, dry** | Concentrated | **↑** Longer | Dull white |
| ↓ Pressing time | ↑ Higher (more moisture) | Soft, spongy | Mild | ↓ Shorter | Bright white |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Paneer Preparation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Milk Reception and Quality Check] --> B[Standardization of Fat and SNF]
    B --> C[Filtration and Clarification]
    C --> D[Heating to 70 to 80 degrees Celsius]
    D --> E[Addition of 1 to 2 percent Citric Acid Solution]
    E --> F[Coagulation at Isoelectric pH 4.6]
    F --> G[Straining through Muslin Cloth]
    G --> H[Whey Separation and Collection]
    H --> I[Washing of Curd with Potable Cold Water]
    I --> J[Pressing at 1 to 2 kg per cm square for 15 to 20 minutes]
    J --> K[Block Formation and Chilling at 4 degrees Celsius]
    K --> L[Packaging in Food Grade Polythene or Vacuum Pack]
    L --> M[Storage at 4 degrees Celsius Shelf Life 5 to 7 Days]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Acid Coagulation Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STREAM]
        A1[Standardized Milk 4.5 percent Fat]
        A2[Citric Acid 1 to 2 percent Solution]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[COAGULATION UNIT]
        B1[Heater Plate Heat Exchanger]
        B2[Acid Mixing Tank with Stirrer]
        B3[Curd Vat with pH Sensor]
        B4[Mechanical Press with Timer]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STREAMS]
        C1[Pressed Paneer Block]
        C2[Whey Byproduct Rich in Lactose]
        C3[Wash Water Containing Soluble Salts]
    end

    subgraph QC[QUALITY CONTROL]
        D1[Moisture Analyzer]
        D2[Fat Estimation by Gerber Method]
        D3[pH Meter and Texture Profile Analyzer]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3
    A2 --> B2
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Casein Micelle Behaviour at Varying pH

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Casein Micelle at pH 6.7 with Net Negative Charge] -->|Stable Suspension| S2[Repulsion keeps micelles apart]
    S2 -->|Acid Addition H plus ions| S3[Protonation of Kappa Casein]
    S3 -->|Reaches pH 4.6| S4[Zero Net Charge Isoelectric Point]
    S4 -->|Loss of Repulsion| S5[Aggregation via Calcium Phosphate Bridges]
    S5 -->|Heat at 70 to 80 degrees Celsius| S6[Three Dimensional Protein Gel Paneer Curd]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define paneer. State the FSSAI compositional standards for paneer.**

**Model Answer:**
Paneer is a soft, unripened cheese obtained by heat–acid coagulation of standardized cow or buffalo milk, followed by draining of whey and pressing of the curd. As per FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2011), paneer shall contain **not more than 70% moisture** and **not less than 50% milk fat on a dry-matter basis**. The product is rennet-free and ready-to-eat after chilling. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the isoelectric point of casein? Why is it important in paneer preparation?**

**Model Answer:**
The isoelectric point (pI) of casein is **pH ≈ 4.6**, the pH at which casein carries **zero net electrical charge**. At this pH, electrostatic repulsion between casein micelles vanishes, leading to **aggregation and curd formation**. In paneer preparation, adding acid (citric/lactic) to hot milk reduces the pH to ~4.6, triggering coagulation and forming the curd mass that becomes paneer. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **lose 2 marks** by writing "paneer is a cheese" without specifying *acid-set, unripened, non-melting*.
> - Many students **forget to mention the isoelectric pH (4.6)** and only describe the procedure — always link chemistry to the process.
> - **Don't write the procedure as a paragraph** — use numbered steps. Examiners allocate step-marks.
> - For 7-mark sub-questions, always include **a labelled diagram or flow chart** to score the full 7.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **standardized procedure for the preparation of paneer** from cow milk in the laboratory. Mention the chemicals, equipment, and quality parameters evaluated.

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus & Chemicals Required:**
1. Stainless-steel vat (5 L capacity)
2. Muslin cloth (boiled and sterilized)
3. Citric acid solution (1–2 % w/v)
4. Thermometer (range 0–100 °C)
5. Stainless-steel ladle, curd cutter, and mechanical press
6. pH meter, Gerber butyrometer, moisture oven

**Procedure (Standard Cow Milk Paneer):**

1. **Milk Reception:** Take 1 litre of fresh, standardized cow milk (4.5% fat, 8.5% SNF). Record initial mass.
2. **Heating:** Heat milk to **45 °C**, then filter through muslin to remove visible impurities.
3. **Standardization:** Confirm fat content using the **Gerber method**.
4. **Reheating:** Reheat filtered milk to **70–80 °C** while stirring gently to avoid scorching.
5. **Acid Addition:** Prepare **1–2 % citric acid solution**. Add acid slowly in a thin stream while stirring until the whey becomes **clear greenish-yellow** and curd separates cleanly. Final pH should be **~4.6**.
6. **Curd Settling:** Stop heating. Allow curd to settle for **5 minutes**.
7. **Straining:** Pour the coagulum through a muslin cloth. Collect whey separately.
8. **Washing:** Wash the curd mass with **potable chilled water (4 °C)** to remove residual acid and lactose.
9. **Pressing:** Tie the muslin and place the curd under a mechanical press at **1–2 kg/cm² for 15–20 minutes** to remove excess moisture.
10. **Block Formation:** Unwrap the pressed paneer, cut into blocks, and chill at **4 °C** for 2 hours.
11. **Storage:** Wrap in food-grade polyethylene and store refrigerated.

**Quality Parameters Evaluated:**
- **Yield** = (Mass of paneer / Mass of milk) × 100
- **Moisture** by oven drying (≤ 70 %)
- **Fat** by Gerber method
- **FDB** (Fat on Dry Basis) ≥ 50 %
- **pH** (target 5.4–5.8)
- **Sensory evaluation** (colour, body, texture, flavour)

*(7 marks — well-distributed across apparatus, procedure steps, and quality parameters)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of paneer formation** with reference to the isoelectric point of casein. How does the temperature of coagulation affect the texture and yield of paneer?

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
Paneer is formed by **heat–acid coagulation** of milk. Milk contains **casein micelles** stabilized by κ-casein and surrounded by a net negative charge at the natural pH of milk (~6.7). When **food-grade acid** is added, the H⁺ ions protonate the casein, lowering the pH. At the **isoelectric point (pH ≈ 4.6)**, the net charge on casein becomes zero, eliminating electrostatic repulsion. This causes the casein micelles to **aggregate via calcium phosphate bridges**, entrapping fat globules and water into a three-dimensional protein network — the curd (paneer). Concurrent heating at 70–80 °C **denatures whey proteins**, which co-precipitate with casein and improve yield.

**Effect of Coagulation Temperature:**

| Temperature Range | Effect on Texture | Effect on Yield |
|---|---|---|
| < 70 °C | Soft, fragile, crumbly curd | Lower yield (poor protein denaturation) |
| **70–80 °C (optimum)** | **Smooth, firm, sliceable body** | **Maximum yield** |
| > 85 °C | Hard, rubbery, over-cooked texture | Decreased yield (curd shrinks, moisture loss) |

> **Explanation:** Higher temperatures cause excessive whey-protein denaturation, leading to a tighter protein network and moisture expulsion. Lower temperatures give incomplete denaturation, leaving the curd weak and moist.

*(7 marks — covers principle, isoelectric chemistry, and temperature-texture-yield relationship)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the **factors affecting the yield and quality of paneer**? Discuss any five factors in detail.

**Model Solution:**

**Five Major Factors:**

1. **Type and Quality of Milk:**
Cow milk yields **softer, sliceable paneer** (~15% w/w) while buffalo milk gives **higher-yield, creamier paneer** (~20% w/w). The fat and casein content directly determine yield.

2. **Fat Standardization:**
Paneer requires a minimum **50% FDB** (fat on dry basis). Standardizing milk to 4.5–5% fat improves richness, texture, and mouthfeel.

3. **Acidulant Type and Concentration:**
- **Citric acid** → Clean, sharp flavor; mild sourness.
- **Lactic acid** → Milder, cultured flavor.
- **Sour whey** → Traditional flavor; adds residual lactose.
Excess acid → brittle texture and sour off-flavor.

4. **Coagulation Temperature:**
Optimal at **70–80 °C**. Below 70 °C, curd is soft and yield is low. Above 85 °C, curd becomes hard, rubbery, and yield decreases due to moisture loss.

5. **Pressing Conditions:**
- Higher pressure and longer time → **lower moisture**, harder body, longer shelf life.
- Lower pressure → **soft, moist paneer** with shorter shelf life.

6. *(Bonus: Storage Temperature)*
Storage at **4 °C** extends shelf life to **5–7 days**; ambient storage leads to microbial spoilage within 24–48 hours.

*(7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **isoelectric point**. Calculate the **expected paneer yield** from **1.5 litres of cow milk** standardized to 4.5% fat and 8.5% SNF, assuming 55% moisture in the final product and an 85% recovery efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The **isoelectric point (pI)** is the pH at which a protein carries **zero net electrical charge**. For casein, pI ≈ **4.6**. At this pH, casein micelles lose electrostatic repulsion and aggregate to form curd.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{milk}} = 1.5 \; \text{L} = 1500 \; \text{mL}$
- Density $\rho = 1.030 \; \text{g/mL}$, so $M_{\text{milk}} = 1500 \times 1.030 = 1545 \; \text{g}$
- Fat = 4.5 %, SNF = 8.5 %
- Moisture in final paneer = 55 % → Dry matter = 45 %
- Recovery efficiency = 85 %

**Step 1 — Mass of Fat:**

$$
M_{\text{fat}} = 1545 \times \frac{4.5}{100} = 69.53 \; \text{g}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Casein (assume 38% of SNF is protein, 80% of protein is casein):**

$$
M_{\text{casein}} = 1545 \times \frac{8.5}{100} \times \frac{38}{100} \times \frac{80}{100} = 39.93 \; \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Dry Paneer (using 85% recovery):**

$$
Y_{\text{dry}} = (69.53 + 39.93) \times 0.85 = 109.46 \times 0.85 = 93.04 \; \text{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Yield (with 55% moisture):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{practical}} &= \frac{Y_{\text{dry}}}{1 - 0.55} \\
&= \frac{93.04}{0.45} \\
&\approx 206.8 \; \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Approximately **207 g of paneer** will be obtained from **1.5 litres of standardized cow milk** under optimum conditions.

*(7 marks — definition 1 mark + stepwise calculation 6 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize for KTU ESE**

- ✅ Paneer is a **soft, unripened, acid-set cheese** made by heat–acid coagulation.
- ✅ FSSAI Standards: **Moisture ≤ 70%** and **Fat on Dry Basis (FDB) ≥ 50%**.
- ✅ **Isoelectric point of casein = pH 4.6** — the chemistry cornerstone of paneer.
- ✅ Optimum **coagulation temperature = 70–80 °C**.
- ✅ Common acidulants: **Citric, Lactic, Sour whey** (all FSSAI-permitted).
- ✅ Standard yield: **~150 g/L cow milk**, **~210 g/L buffalo milk**.
- ✅ **Pressing:** 1–2 kg/cm² for 15–20 minutes → standard 55–60% moisture paneer.
- ✅ **Storage:** 4 °C refrigerated, shelf life **5–7 days**.
- ✅ **Pearce equation** $Y = (F + C) \times 0.85$ — high-yield viva question.
- ✅ **Difference from Chhena:** Paneer is **pressed and contains fat**; chhena is unpressed and low-fat.
- ✅ **Difference from Tofu:** Paneer uses **acid coagulation**; tofu uses **salt coagulation** (CaSO₄ / MgCl₂).
- ✅ **By-product:** Whey — rich in lactose, used for beverages, infant formula, and bioethanol.
- ✅ **Quality tests:** Gerber (fat), oven-drying (moisture), pH meter, sensory evaluation.
- ✅ **Examiner's trap:** Always mention **isoelectric pH + heat denaturation of whey proteins** in principle-type questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Tomato Puree/Ketchup
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - Food Technology Laboratory
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Tomato Puree/Ketchup

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tomato Puree** is defined under **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) Regulations, 2011** as the strained or extracted liquid derived exclusively from sound, ripe, fresh tomatoes (*Solanum lycopersicum*), concentrated by evaporation, containing not less than **9.0% (w/w) of Total Soluble Solids (TSS)** and not less than **0.5% (w/w) acidity as citric acid** before the addition of any permitted seasoning, spice, or flavouring. It must be free from seeds, skin, and coarse particles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tomato Ketchup (Sauce)** is a thick, viscous, spiced, sweetened, and acidified product manufactured from tomato **pulp/puree** with permitted additions of sugar, salt, vinegar, onion, garlic, spices, and condiments. As per FSSAI, it must contain not less than **25% Total Soluble Solids (TSS)** and not less than **1.0% (w/w) acetic acid** as acidity.

**Differentiating the Two Products — Table 1.1**

| Parameter | Tomato Puree | Tomato Ketchup |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| Definition (FSSAI) | Strained tomato juice, concentrated | Tomato pulp + sugar + vinegar + spices |
| Minimum TSS (°Brix) | **9.0%** | **25.0%** |
| Minimum Acidity | 0.5% (as citric acid) | 1.0% (as acetic acid) |
| Added Sugar | None (only natural) | Yes (5–8% typical) |
| Added Vinegar | None | Yes (1–2% typical) |
| Added Spices | None | Onion, Garlic, Clove, Cinnamon, etc. |
| Texture | Smooth, seedless, skinless | Smooth, glossy, viscous |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Squeeze-Strain-Cook Analogy**
> Imagine you are making fresh orange juice at home. You **squeeze** the orange (analogous to **crushing** tomatoes), **strain** the pulp to remove seeds and fibrous bits (analogous to **pulping/finishing** through a mesh), and then let the juice simmer on the stove until it thickens (analogous to **evaporative concentration**). That thickened juice = **Tomato Puree**.
> Now, if you add a spoon of honey (sweetness), a splash of lemon (acidity), and a pinch of ginger-cinnamon (spice blend), and cook it further until glossy and thick enough to stick to a spoon — **Bingo! That is Tomato Ketchup!**
>
> **The Big Idea:** Puree is just **"concentrated, strained tomatoes."** Ketchup is **"puree that has been re-engineered with sugar + acid + spices."**

### 1.3 Key Industry Standards (Bold Constants)

> **FSSAI Quality Benchmarks:**
> - **Tomato Puree TSS** = **9.0% minimum** (°Brix)
> - **Tomato Ketchup TSS** = **25.0% minimum** (°°Brix)
> - **Tomato Ketchup Acidity** = **1.0% minimum** (as acetic acid)
> - **pH of Ketchup** = **3.9 – 4.2** (for shelf stability)
> - **Brix : Acid Ratio** = **(25–28) : 1** (sensory balance)
> - **Concentration temperature** = **85–95°C**
> - **Recommended moisture content** = **66–70%** in ketchup

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Science of Tomato Processing

Tomato is composed of approximately **94% water**, **4–5% carbohydrates (sugars + pectin + cellulose)**, **0.5–1.0% organic acids (citric + malic)**, **0.5% proteins**, **0.3% pigments (lycopene, β-carotene)**, and minor minerals. The unit operations involved in converting fresh tomatoes into puree/ketchup target **three engineering objectives**:

1. **Water Removal** → Achieved by thermal evaporation → increases TSS, reduces volume, improves shelf life.
2. **Particle Refinement** → Achieved by mechanical pulping → removes seeds, skin, cores → smooth texture.
3. **Microbial Stabilization** → Achieved by **acidity + sugar + thermal pasteurization** → pH < 4.5 inhibits *Clostridium botulinum* and most spoilage organisms (Hurdle Technology principle).

### 2.2 Operational Principle — Step-Wise Logic Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Raw Material Selection:** Only **fully ripe, firm, deep red tomatoes** are chosen. *Why?* Lycopene (red pigment) peaks at full ripeness, pectin is at optimal level for body, and acidity is moderate.
- **Step 2 — Sorting & Washing:** Removes damaged, rotten, moldy fruits and field soil. *Why?* Eliminates the largest microbial load entry point.
- **Step 3 — Trimming & Coring:** Removal of stem scars, green portions, and blemishes. *Why?* Chlorophyll-rich green parts cause bitterness and off-flavours.
- **Step 4 — Crushing:** Mechanical breakdown to liberate juice. Two strategies exist:
    * **Hot Break (85–95°C before crushing):** Inactivates pectin methyl esterase (PME) enzyme → retains more pectin → thicker body.
    * **Cold Break (25–30°C crushing):** Preserves fresh tomato flavour but produces thinner puree.
- **Step 5 — Pulping / Finishing:** Crushed mash passed through a **paddle finisher** (cylindrical mesh, ~0.5 mm holes) to remove seeds, skin, and fibrous cores. *Why?* Refines texture dramatically.
- **Step 6 — Concentration (Evaporation):** Open pan / steam-jacketed kettle / vacuum evaporator used. Water evaporates until TSS reaches target level (9% for puree, 25% for ketchup base).
- **Step 7 — Ingredient Addition (Ketchup only):** Sugar dissolved, salt added, vinegar + spice extract blended. *Why?* Sugar masks acidity, salt enhances flavour, vinegar acts as a natural preservative.
- **Step 8 — Cooking & Bottling:** Hot-filling at **≥ 85°C** into pre-sterilized glass bottles. Vacuum sealing or crown-capping follows.
- **Step 9 — Pasteurization:** Bottles pasteurized at **88–95°C for 30–45 minutes** in boiling water bath. *Why?* Destroys spoilage microbes and yeasts.
- **Step 10 — Cooling, Labeling, Storage:** Bottles cooled in stages (to prevent breakage), labeled, and stored at **ambient (25–30°C)** in cool, dry place.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for PCFTL507 Exam Calculations**

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{Process Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{W_{\text{final product}}}{W_{\text{fresh tomato}}} \times 100$ | Overall mass recovery from raw material | **% (w/w)** |
| 2 | $\text{Concentration Factor (CF)} = \dfrac{\text{TSS}_{\text{final}}}{\text{TSS}_{\text{initial}}}$ | Ratio of final TSS to initial tomato juice TSS | **dimensionless** |
| 3 | $\text{Evaporation Loss (\%)} = \dfrac{W_{\text{initial juice}} - W_{\text{concentrated}}}{W_{\text{initial juice}}} \times 100$ | Water removed during boiling | **% (w/w)** |
| 4 | $\text{Acidity (\% as acetic acid)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.060 \times 100}{W_{\text{sample}}}$ | Titrimetric determination of total acidity | **% (w/w)** |
| 5 | $\text{TSS (°Brix)} = \text{Refractometer reading corrected to 20°C}$ | Direct optical measurement using Brix refractometer | **°Brix / %** |
| 6 | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | Hydrogen ion concentration (measured by pH meter) | **dimensionless (0–14)** |
| 7 | $\text{Brix-Acid Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{°Brix}}{\text{\% Acidity}}$ | Sensory balance indicator for ketchup | **dimensionless** |
| 8 | $W_{\text{sugar required}} = \dfrac{(\text{TSS}_{\text{target}} - \text{TSS}_{\text{pulp}}) \times W_{\text{pulp}}}{100 - \text{TSS}_{\text{target}}}$ | Sugar to add to reach target TSS | **kg or g** |
| 9 | $W_{\text{water to evaporate}} = W_{\text{initial}} \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{TSS}_{\text{initial}}}{\text{TSS}_{\text{final}}}\right)$ | Volume reduction during concentration | **kg or L** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Experiment Matters for Food Engineers:**
> 1. **Industrial Scale-Up:** The same unit operations (crushing → pulping → evaporation → hot-fill) form the backbone of every fruit processing plant — mango pulp, apple juice concentrate, pineapple concentrate.
> 2. **Hurdle Technology:** Demonstrates how **pH + TSS + thermal processing** combine to make food shelf-stable without refrigeration.
> 3. **Quality Control Training:** Trains students in **titrimetric acidity determination** and **refractometric TSS measurement** — two of the most common quality tests in any food industry QC lab.
> 4. **Process Calculation Skills:** Yield, concentration factor, and evaporation losses are calculated identically in **dairy, brewing, sugar, and beverage industries**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (Board Exam Style):**
> A batch of **20 kg of fresh ripe tomatoes** (initial TSS = **5.0°Brix**) is crushed, pulped, and concentrated to produce **Tomato Puree** with a final TSS of **10.0°Brix**. Calculate: **(a)** Process yield, **(b)** Concentration Factor, **(c)** Weight of water evaporated during concentration.

---

### Part (a) — Process Yield Calculation

**Given Data:**
- Fresh tomato weight: $W_{\text{fresh}} = 20 \text{ kg}$
- Final puree weight (assumed equivalent to pulp concentrated to 10°Brix from a pulp mass that retains all solids):

**Step 1 — Calculate weight of final puree using mass balance on solids:**

Since solids are conserved during evaporation (only water leaves):

$$
W_{\text{pulp solids}} = W_{\text{fresh}} \times \dfrac{\text{TSS}_{\text{initial}}}{100}
$$

$$
W_{\text{pulp solids}} = 20 \times \dfrac{5.0}{100} = 1.0 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine final puree weight from final TSS:**

$$
W_{\text{final puree}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{pulp solids}}}{\text{TSS}_{\text{final}} / 100} = \dfrac{1.0}{10.0/100}
$$

$$
W_{\text{final puree}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 100}{10.0} = 10.0 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute yield:**

$$
\text{Process Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{W_{\text{final puree}}}{W_{\text{fresh}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Process Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{10.0}{20.0} \times 100 = \mathbf{50.0\,\%}
$$

---

### Part (b) — Concentration Factor (CF)

**Step 1 — Apply CF definition:**

$$
\text{CF} = \dfrac{\text{TSS}_{\text{final}}}{\text{TSS}_{\text{initial}}}
$$

$$
\text{CF} = \dfrac{10.0}{5.0} = \mathbf{2.0}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The tomato juice is **concentrated 2 times** — every 2 L of initial juice yields 1 L of puree.

---

### Part (c) — Weight of Water Evaporated

**Step 1 — Apply the water-evaporation mass balance:**

$$
W_{\text{water evaporated}} = W_{\text{initial juice}} \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{TSS}_{\text{initial}}}{\text{TSS}_{\text{final}}}\right)
$$

$$
W_{\text{water evaporated}} = 20.0 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{5.0}{10.0}\right)
$$

$$
W_{\text{water evaporated}} = 20.0 \times \left(1 - 0.5\right)
$$

$$
W_{\text{water evaporated}} = 20.0 \times 0.5 = \mathbf{10.0 \text{ kg}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** Initial mass (20 kg) = Final puree (10 kg) + Water evaporated (10 kg). ✔ Balance verified.

---

### 3.2 Extended Worked Example — Tomato Ketchup Sugar Calculation

> **Problem:** A factory has **15 kg of tomato pulp** at **7.0°Brix**. They want to make ketchup at **25.0°Brix**. Calculate the weight of sugar to add.

**Step 1 — Apply the sugar-addition formula:**

$$
W_{\text{sugar}} = \dfrac{(\text{TSS}_{\text{target}} - \text{TSS}_{\text{pulp}}) \times W_{\text{pulp}}}{100 - \text{TSS}_{\text{target}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
W_{\text{sugar}} = \dfrac{(25.0 - 7.0) \times 15.0}{100 - 25.0}
$$

$$
W_{\text{sugar}} = \dfrac{18.0 \times 15.0}{75.0}
$$

$$
W_{\text{sugar}} = \dfrac{270.0}{75.0} = \mathbf{3.6 \text{ kg}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Add **3.6 kg sugar** to 15 kg pulp to obtain ketchup at 25°Brix (assuming sugar is pure soluble solid).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Critical Process Parameters

| Stage | Operation | Temperature (°C) | Time | TSS / Key Output |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|------|------------------|
| 1 | Sorting & Washing | 25 (ambient) | 10–15 min | Clean tomatoes |
| 2 | Trimming & Coring | 25 | 5–10 min | Blemish-free fruit |
| 3 | Crushing (Hot Break) | **85–95** | 5 min | Mash, PME inactivated |
| 4 | Pulping / Finishing | 70–80 | Continuous | Seedless pulp, ~5–7°Brix |
| 5 | Concentration | **85–95** | 30–60 min | Puree (9–11°Brix) |
| 6 | Ingredient mixing (ketchup) | **80–90** | 15–20 min | Sauce base, 25°Brix |
| 7 | Hot filling | **≥ 85** | Continuous | Sealed bottles |
| 8 | Pasteurization | **88–95** | **30–45 min** | Shelf-stable product |
| 9 | Cooling | 25 (gradual) | 30 min | Bottles ready for storage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Tomato Puree / Ketchup Manufacturing

```mermaid

    A[Raw Tomato Receipt & Sorting] --> B[Washing in Rotary Washer]
    B --> C[Trimming, Coring and Inspection]

    E --> F{QP Decision: Puree or Ketchup}
    F -->|Puree Path| G1[Evaporative Concentration in Steam Jacketed Kettle]
    F -->|Ketchup Path| G2[Evaporative Concentration to 12-15 Brix]
    G1 --> H1[TSS Check by Refractometer, Target 9-11 Brix]
    H1 --> I1[Hot Filling at 85C into Sterilized Bottles]
    G2 --> J[Addition of Sugar, Salt, Vinegar and Spice Extract]
    J --> K[Cooking to Target TSS 25 Brix]
    K --> L[Acidity and pH Verification, Target 1.0% Acetic]
    L --> M[Hot Filling at 85C into Sterilized Bottles]
    I1 --> N[Sealing and Crown Capping]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Pasteurization at 88-95C for 30-45 min]
    O --> P[Gradual Cooling to 25C]
    P --> Q[Labeling, Coding and Storage at Ambient]
```

    Q4 --> Q5[pH of Fresh Juice Target 4.2-4.5]
    Q5 --> Q6[In-Process During Concentration]
    Q6 --> Q7{TSS Reaches Target Puree 9 or Ketchup 25?}
    Q7 -->|No, continue boiling| Q6
    Q7 -->|Yes| Q8[Refractometer Reading Confirmed]
    Q8 --> Q9[Acidity Titration for Ketchup Target 1.0%]
    Q9 --> Q10{pH within 3.9 to 4.2?}
    Q10 -->|No, adjust with vinegar| Q11[Re-blend with Vinegar]

    Q10 -->|Yes| Q12[Hot Fill and Pasteurize]
