<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, PCFTL507]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Tomato Puree and Tomato Ketchup based on TSS and acidity as per FSSAI standards.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> As per **FSSAI Regulations, 2011**:
> - **Tomato Puree** must contain a **minimum of 9.0% Total Soluble Solids (TSS)** and **minimum 0.5% acidity (as citric acid)**.
> - **Tomato Ketchup** must contain a **minimum of 25.0% TSS** and **minimum 1.0% acidity (as acetic acid)**.
> - Additionally, ketchup contains added sugar, salt, vinegar, onion, garlic, and spices, while puree contains only concentrated, strained tomatoes. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. What is the principle behind "Hot Break" treatment of tomatoes during crushing?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Hot Break involves rapidly heating crushed tomatoes to **85–95°C** before pulping. The principle is **enzymatic inactivation of pectin methyl esterase (PME)**, which otherwise de-methylates pectin and reduces its gelling capacity. By inactivating PME, the **natural pectin is preserved**, resulting in a thicker, more viscous puree with higher consistency and yield. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Module 2 Question Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**Describe in detail the step-by-step manufacturing process of Tomato Ketchup, including the role of each ingredient and the quality control parameters tested.**

**Sub-part (a) — Process Flow with Ingredient Roles (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Raw Material Selection:**
Fully ripe, deep red, sound tomatoes of variety like **Roma, Pusa Ruby, or Hybrid** are selected. Green portions are trimmed.

**Step 2 – Washing:**
Tomatoes are washed in a rotary washer using chlorinated water (5–10 ppm chlorine) to reduce surface microbial load.

**Step 3 – Crushing (Hot Break):**
Tomatoes are crushed and immediately heated to **85–95°C** for 5 minutes. This inactivates **pectin methyl esterase** enzyme, preserving pectin and yielding thick body.

**Step 4 – Pulping:**
The hot mash is passed through a **paddle finisher** with 0.5 mm screen to remove seeds, skin, and cores. The pulp is collected (~5–7°Brix).

**Step 5 – Concentration:**
Pulp is concentrated in a **steam-jacketed kettle** at 85–95°C to ~12–15°Brix.

**Step 6 – Ingredient Addition and Cooking:**
The following ingredients are added per **100 kg pulp**:

| Ingredient | Quantity | Role |
|------------|----------|------|
| Sugar | 8–10 kg | Sweetness, body, preservative |
| Salt | 1.5–2 kg | Flavour enhancement |
| Vinegar (5% acetic) | 5–7 L | Acidification (pH < 4.2), preservative |
| Onion powder | 0.3 kg | Flavour |
| Garlic powder | 0.1 kg | Flavour |
| Spice mix (clove, cinnamon, pepper) | 0.2 kg | Aroma |
| **Total Cooking TSS target** | **25°Brix** | FSSAI compliance |

Cooking continues until TSS reaches **25°Brix** and acidity ≥ **1.0% acetic acid**.

**Step 7 – Hot Filling:**
Ketchup at ≥ 85°C is filled into pre-sterilized glass bottles and sealed with crown caps.

**Step 8 – Pasteurization:**
Sealed bottles pasteurized at **88–95°C for 30–45 minutes** in boiling water bath.

**Step 9 – Cooling, Labeling, Storage:**
Gradual cooling to 25°C, labeling, and storage at ambient. **(7 marks)**

---

**Sub-part (b) — Quality Control Parameters (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Total Soluble Solids (TSS / °Brix):**
Measured by **hand-held refractometer** at 20°C. Must be **≥ 25%** for ketchup.

**2. Acidity (% as acetic acid):**
Determined by **titration with 0.1 N NaOH** using phenolphthalein indicator.

$$
\text{Acidity (\%)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.060 \times 100}{W_{\text{sample}}}
$$

Must be **≥ 1.0%** for ketchup.

**3. pH:**
Measured by **digital pH meter**. Must be **3.9 – 4.2** for microbial safety.

**4. Brix : Acid Ratio:**
Optimal sensory balance at **25 : 1 to 28 : 1**.

**5. Bostwick Consistency:**
Time for ketchup to flow 30 cm on Bostwick consistometer at 25°C. Acceptable: **< 8 cm in 30 sec**.

**6. Colour:**
Measured using **Lovibond tintometer** or Hunter Lab colorimeter; should be deep red (lycopene-rich).

**7. Microbiological Tests:**
Total plate count < 50 CFU/g; yeast and mould < 10 CFU/g; no coliforms. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**A batch of 25 kg of fresh tomatoes at 5.5°Brix is processed to obtain tomato puree at 10°Brix. Calculate (i) yield, (ii) concentration factor, (iii) weight of water evaporated. If the puree is further used to make ketchup at 28°Brix, calculate (iv) weight of sugar to add per 100 kg of this puree.**

**Sub-part (a) — Puree Calculations (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $W_{\text{fresh}} = 25 \text{ kg}$, $\text{TSS}_{\text{initial}} = 5.5\%$, $\text{TSS}_{\text{final}} = 10.0\%$

**(i) Process Yield:**

Solids in fresh tomatoes:
$$
W_{\text{solids}} = 25 \times \dfrac{5.5}{100} = 1.375 \text{ kg}
$$

Final puree weight:
$$
W_{\text{puree}} = \dfrac{1.375 \times 100}{10.0} = 13.75 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\text{Yield} = \dfrac{13.75}{25.0} \times 100 = \mathbf{55.0\%}
$$

**(ii) Concentration Factor:**
$$
\text{CF} = \dfrac{10.0}{5.5} = \mathbf{1.818}
$$

**(iii) Water Evaporated:**
$$
W_{\text{water}} = 25.0 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{5.5}{10.0}\right) = 25.0 \times 0.45 = \mathbf{11.25 \text{ kg}}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

**Sub-part (b) — Sugar Calculation for Ketchup (7 marks)**

**Given:** 100 kg of puree at 10°Brix, target ketchup TSS = 28°Brix.

**Apply sugar-addition formula:**
$$
W_{\text{sugar}} = \dfrac{(\text{TSS}_{\text{target}} - \text{TSS}_{\text{puree}}) \times W_{\text{puree}}}{100 - \text{TSS}_{\text{target}}}
$$

$$
W_{\text{sugar}} = \dfrac{(28.0 - 10.0) \times 100}{100 - 28.0}
$$

$$
W_{\text{sugar}} = \dfrac{18.0 \times 100}{72.0} = \dfrac{1800}{72.0} = \mathbf{25.0 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Verification:** New TSS check:
$$
\text{TSS}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{(100 \times 10) + (25 \times 100)}{100 + 25} = \dfrac{1000 + 2500}{125} = \dfrac{3500}{125} = 28.0\% \;\checkmark
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PCFTL507 Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing Puree and Ketchup TSS limits** — Students often write 9% for ketchup or 25% for puree. **Memorize: Puree ≥ 9°Brix, Ketchup ≥ 25°Brix.**
> 2. **Wrong acidity unit** — Puree uses **citric acid** (0.060 factor), ketchup uses **acetic acid** (0.060 factor but different normality interpretation). Read FSSAI carefully.
> 3. **Sugar calculation formula error** — A very common mistake is using $(100 - \text{TSS}_{\text{initial}})$ in the denominator instead of $(100 - \text{TSS}_{\text{target}})$.
> 4. **Ignoring Hot Break principle** — Many students write "Hot Break kills microbes" — this is **wrong**. Hot Break inactivates **pectin methyl esterase**, not directly killing microbes.
> 5. **Yield calculation oversight** — Always perform a **mass balance check** to verify water evaporated + final product = initial weight.
> 6. **No mention of Brix-Acid ratio** — Examiners often award 1 extra mark for stating the optimal ratio of 25–28 : 1 in ketchup.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Preparation of Tomato Puree/Ketchup**

- ☐ **Tomato Puree** = strained, concentrated tomato juice only; **TSS ≥ 9%**, acidity ≥ **0.5% citric acid**.
- ☐ **Tomato Ketchup** = tomato puree + sugar + salt + vinegar + spices; **TSS ≥ 25%**, acidity ≥ **1.0% acetic acid**, **pH 3.9–4.2**.
- ☐ **FSSAI standard** is the regulatory benchmark; cite it in any definition-based answer.
- ☐ **Hot Break (85–95°C)** inactivates **pectin methyl esterase (PME)** → thicker body, better yield.
- ☐ **Paddle Finisher (0.5 mm screen)** removes seeds, skin, and cores for smooth texture.
- ☐ **Concentration** is done in a **steam-jacketed kettle** at 85–95°C with constant stirring to prevent scorching.
- ☐ **Hurdle Technology principle:** low pH + high sugar + thermal pasteurization → shelf-stable without refrigeration.
- ☐ **Three must-know calculations:**
    1. **Yield (%)** = $\dfrac{W_{\text{final}}}{W_{\text{fresh}}} \times 100$
    2. **Concentration Factor** = $\dfrac{\text{TSS}_{\text{final}}}{\text{TSS}_{\text{initial}}}$
    3. **Sugar to add** = $\dfrac{(\text{TSS}_{\text{target}} - \text{TSS}_{\text{pulp}}) \times W_{\text{pulp}}}{100 - \text{TSS}_{\text{target}}}$
- ☐ **Acidity titration formula:** $\dfrac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.060 \times 100}{W_{\text{sample}}}$ (factor 0.060 = equivalent weight of acetic acid × 10⁻³ × 100).
- ☐ **Bostwick Consistency** test quantifies ketchup thickness (flow in 30 sec on Bostwick trough).
- ☐ **Pasteurization** of bottled ketchup at **88–95°C for 30–45 min** ensures microbial safety.
- ☐ **Brix-Acid Ratio** = 25–28 : 1 → balanced sweet-tangy sensory profile.
- ☐ **Lycopene** (red pigment) is heat-stable — concentration actually *increases* its concentration per gram of product.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Sauerkraut
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Sauerkraut

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sauerkraut** (German: *Sauerkraut*, meaning "sour cabbage") is a traditional lactic acid-fermented white cabbage (*Brassica oleracea* var. *capitata*) product that originates from Central and Eastern Europe. According to the **Codex Alimentarius (Codex Standard 162-1995)** and **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) standards for fermented vegetable products**, sauerkraut is defined as:

> A fermented vegetable product obtained by the natural lactic acid fermentation of shredded white cabbage, where the concentration of salt typically ranges between **2.0% to 2.5% (w/w)**, the pH drops to below **3.6**, and the titratable acidity (expressed as lactic acid) reaches a minimum of **1.5% to 2.0% (w/w)** at the end of the maturation period.

The fermentation process relies entirely on the **epiphytic (naturally present) lactic acid bacteria (LAB)** on the cabbage surface, particularly species of the genus *Leuconostoc*, *Lactobacillus*, *Pediococcus*, and *Streptococcus*. No external starter culture is added in traditional preparation, making sauerkraut a classic example of **spontaneous mixed-culture fermentation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sauerkraut preparation is the **canonical model experiment** for studying the principles of **submerged lactic acid fermentation** of vegetables, the role of **salt in osmotic dehydration and microbial selection**, and the **homofermentative vs. heterofermentative** metabolic pathways in food biotechnology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are making a **pickle**, but instead of using vinegar (external acid), you let the cabbage **create its own acid naturally** using the sugar locked inside its cells. The process is analogous to how a **closed terrarium works** — once the cabbage is submerged in its own salty juice, an oxygen-free (anaerobic) environment is created. The friendly bacteria already living on the cabbage leaves (think of them as **invisible tenants on the cabbage surface**) wake up and begin converting the cabbage sugars into lactic acid.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of sauerkraut fermentation as a **"relay race" of microorganisms**:

1. First, the **sprinters** (*Leuconostoc mesenteroides*) start the race. They are fast but cannot tolerate very high acid.
2. As they produce lactic acid and CO₂, the environment becomes more acidic.
3. The **middle-distance runners** (*Lactobacillus brevis*, *Pediococcus cerevisiae*) take over.
4. Finally, the **marathon runners** (*Lactobacillus plantarum*) finish the race, producing the final, stable acidity that preserves the cabbage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is salt added?** Salt (NaCl) plays **three critical roles** in sauerkraut preparation:
> 1. **Osmotic extraction** of water and sugars from cabbage cells (drawing out juice to create brine).
> 2. **Selective inhibition** of spoilage organisms (especially Gram-negative bacteria like *Enterobacteriaceae* and the yeast *Endomyces*).
> 3. **Texture control** — prevents softening or mushiness of the final product.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Quality Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Salt concentration (initial brine) | **2.0 – 2.5% (w/w)** | Selects LAB over spoilage organisms |
| Fermentation temperature | **18 – 22°C (optimal)** | Affects microbial succession rate |
| Final pH | **3.4 – 3.6** | Ensures microbiological safety |
| Titratable acidity (as lactic acid) | **1.5 – 2.0% (w/w)** | Indicator of proper fermentation |
| Salt in final product | **1.0 – 1.5% (w/w)** | After equilibrium with brine |
| Texture (sensory) | Crisp, not slimy | Pectin integrity preserved |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three-Phase Fermentation Model

The biochemical and microbiological transformation during sauerkraut preparation follows a well-defined **three-phase kinetic model** (Pederson & Albury, 1969):

### **Phase 1: Initial Lag / Salt Extraction Phase (0 – 24 hours)**

1. Shredded cabbage is mixed with dry salt (**2.25% w/w** standard commercial level).
2. Salt exerts **osmotic pressure** across the semi-permeable cabbage cell membranes.
3. Water and soluble cellular constituents (sugars, amino acids, vitamins) diffuse out — this is termed **"self-brining"** or **autolysis phase**.
4. The aerobic mesophilic flora (Gram-negative bacteria, yeasts, molds) is suppressed by NaCl.
5. Anaerobic conditions develop as the cabbage is firmly packed and weighted down below the brine level.
6. The cabbage **loses approximately 20–25% of its original weight** as liquid exudate.

### **Phase 2: Mixed Acid Fermentation (Day 1 – Day 7)**

1. *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* (heterofermentative) initiates active fermentation.
2. Gas production (CO₂) is observed; the environment becomes increasingly anaerobic.
3. Acid accumulation begins; pH drops from **~6.0 → 4.5**.
4. *Lactobacillus brevis*, *Pediococcus cerevisiae*, and *Streptococcus faecalis* become active in succession.
5. The heterofermentative pathway produces **lactic acid, acetic acid, ethanol, and CO₂**.

### **Phase 3: Maturation / Homofermentative Phase (Day 7 – Day 21+)**

1. *Lactobacillus plantarum* (homofermentative) dominates as pH drops below 4.5.
2. The homofermentative pathway converts hexose sugars almost exclusively to **lactic acid**.
3. Final pH reaches **3.4 – 3.6**; total titratable acidity stabilizes at **1.5 – 2.0%**.
4. The "green" or harsh flavor of early fermentation mellows into a balanced, mildly acidic, aromatic profile.
5. The product is microbiologically stable and ready for cold storage (4°C) or further processing.

## 2.2 Core Biochemical Equations

### 2.2.1 Heterofermentative Pathway (*Leuconostoc mesenteroides*)

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2$$

(Glucose → Lactic acid + Ethanol + Carbon dioxide)

The theoretical yield is **1 mole lactic acid + 1 mole ethanol + 1 mole CO₂ per mole of glucose**. Only **~50%** of the carbon is recovered as lactic acid.

### 2.2.2 Homofermentative Pathway (*Lactobacillus plantarum*)

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}$$

(Glucose → 2 Lactic acid)

The theoretical yield is **2 moles lactic acid per mole of glucose**, with **100% carbon recovery** as lactic acid.

### 2.2.3 Pentose Heterofermentation (*L. brevis* on arabinose, xylose from cabbage cell walls)

$$3\,\text{C}_5\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 \longrightarrow 5\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}$$

(Pentose → Lactic acid via phosphoketolase pathway)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The shift from heterofermentative to homofermentative phase is what determines the **flavor quality and acid yield** of the final sauerkraut. The heterofermentative phase contributes aroma complexity (via diacetyl, acetaldehyde, ethanol), while the homofermentative phase ensures acid preservation and safety.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

| # | Concept | Key Value / Principle | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard salt level | **2.25% w/w** | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | Initial pH of cabbage | **~6.0 – 6.2** | ★★★★ |
| 3 | Final pH of sauerkraut | **3.4 – 3.6** | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | Final titratable acidity | **1.5 – 2.0% lactic acid** | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | Optimum temperature | **18 – 22°C** | ★★★★ |
| 6 | First dominant organism | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | Final dominant organism | *Lactobacillus plantarum* | ★★★★★ |
| 8 | Heterofermentative products | Lactic acid + ethanol + CO₂ | ★★★★ |
| 9 | Homofermentative product | Lactic acid only | ★★★★ |
| 10 | Defect — pink kraut | Yeast growth (high salt) | ★★★ |
| 11 | Defect — soft kraut | Pectinolytic enzyme action | ★★★ |
| 12 | Preservative function | Acid (pH < 3.6) + anaerobic | ★★★★ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles studied in sauerkraut preparation are directly applied in the **industrial production of fermented vegetable products** such as:
- **Kimchi** (Korean fermented cabbage) — uses similar LAB succession principles.
- **Pickles** (cucumber fermentation) — uses brine fermentation (5–8% salt).
- **Olives** (Spanish/green style) — uses lactobacillus-mediated debittering.
- **Silage** for animal feed — uses the same spontaneous LAB-driven lactic acid preservation.
- **Probiotic functional foods** — leveraging the live LAB strains isolated from spontaneous vegetable fermentations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Application:** Modern commercial sauerkraut production in India (e.g., by **Mother Dairy**, **Ching's Secret**) uses **thermophilic controlled fermentation in stainless steel tanks** with temperature-controlled brine circulation, achieving uniform fermentation in **10 – 14 days** instead of the traditional 3 – 4 weeks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Process Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Laboratory / Industrial Procedure

The complete preparation of sauerkraut under the **KTU PCFTL507 Module Experiment 2** protocol follows a sequence of 10 controlled steps. Each step has measurable parameters, and each transition can be evaluated quantitatively.

---

### **STEP 1 — Raw Material Selection and Preparation**

1. Select firm, sound, mature heads of white cabbage (*Brassica oleracea* var. *capitata*).
2. Discard outer wilted, damaged, or insect-infested leaves; retain 3 – 4 sound outer green leaves for covering.
3. Trim the core (the hard central stem) and cut the head into halves or quarters.
4. Wash thoroughly in clean, potable, chlorinated water (50 ppm active chlorine) to reduce surface microbial load.
5. Shred the cabbage uniformly to a thickness of **~1 – 2 mm** using a stainless steel shredder. Uniform shredding is critical because:
   - Thin shreds release water and ferment faster.
   - Thick shreds retain crispness but ferment slowly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Material balance:** A standard 10 kg cabbage head yields approximately **8.5 – 9.0 kg of usable shredded cabbage** after trimming and coring. The dry solids content of fresh cabbage is approximately **5 – 8% w/w**, with **sucrose, glucose, and fructose** being the principal fermentable sugars.

---

### **STEP 2 — Salt Addition and Initial Mixing**

1. Weigh the shredded cabbage accurately on a calibrated balance.
2. Add food-grade NaCl at a level of **2.0 – 2.5% (w/w)** of the cabbage weight. The most widely accepted commercial standard is **2.25% (w/w)**.
3. Mix thoroughly by hand or in a mechanical mixer for 5 – 10 minutes.
4. Observe the release of liquid from the cabbage tissues (this is the **osmotically extracted cell sap** forming the natural brine).

**Mass-balance example calculation (KTU typical exam problem):**

*Given:* Shredded cabbage mass = **10.0 kg**; salt level = **2.25% w/w**.

*Step 1:* Mass of salt to be added:
$$m_{\text{salt}} = \frac{2.25}{100} \times 10.0 = 0.225\,\text{kg} = 225\,\text{g}$$

*Step 2:* Mass of cabbage + salt:
$$m_{\text{total}} = 10.0 + 0.225 = 10.225\,\text{kg}$$

*Step 3:* Assume the cabbage releases **20% of its weight** as liquid exudate (typical value). Mass of liquid exudate:
$$m_{\text{exudate}} = 0.20 \times 10.0 = 2.0\,\text{kg}$$

*Step 4:* Total mass of brine + cabbage at the start of fermentation:
$$m_{\text{brine+solids}} = 2.0 + 10.225 = 12.225\,\text{kg}$$

*Step 5:* Concentration of salt in the liquid phase (assuming salt fully dissolves in exudate):
$$\%\text{NaCl}_{\text{brine}} = \frac{0.225}{2.0} \times 100 = 11.25\%\,\text{(w/w)}$$

*Step 6:* This initial high salt concentration in the brine is **NOT the operative salt level** for LAB selection. As fermentation proceeds and water moves in/out of cells, the brine salt concentration equilibrates to approximately **2.0 – 2.5% w/w** in the liquid phase. This is what the LAB "experience" during fermentation.

---

### **STEP 3 — Packing into Fermentation Vessel**

1. Transfer the salted, shredded cabbage into a **food-grade fermentation crock, glass jar, or stainless steel tank**.
2. Pack the cabbage **firmly and uniformly** to eliminate air pockets. Air pockets harbor aerobic spoilage organisms.
3. The vessel should have a **wide mouth** to facilitate packing and to keep the cabbage submerged under its own brine.
4. Leave a headspace of approximately **5 – 8 cm** at the top to accommodate expansion.
5. Place the **3 – 4 reserved whole outer leaves** on top to act as a physical barrier against surface mold growth.

---

### **STEP 4 — Weighing Down and Brine Submersion**

1. Place a clean, food-grade **weight (a glass plate, food-grade stone, or plastic follower with weights)** directly on top of the cabbage.
2. The weight should be sufficient to bring the brine **at least 2 – 3 cm above** the cabbage surface within 6 – 12 hours.
3. If the natural exudate is insufficient, prepare a **supplementary brine** of 2.0 – 2.5% NaCl in boiled and cooled water to top up the vessel.
4. The brine layer is **critical** — it creates an anaerobic seal and prevents surface mold (*Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, and oxidative yeasts) from colonizing the top layer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU practical mistake:** Students often use **iodized table salt** for laboratory sauerkraut. Iodide and anti-caking agents (sodium ferrocyanide) can **inhibit LAB growth** and cause dark discoloration. Always use **non-iodized, food-grade pickling salt** (pure NaCl).

---

### **STEP 5 — Incubation / Fermentation Initiation**

1. Maintain the fermentation vessel at **18 – 22°C** (room temperature acceptable; optimal at 22°C).
2. Allow initial fermentation to proceed for **3 – 7 days** at this temperature.
3. During the first 24 – 48 hours, observe:
   - Bubbling (CO₂ release) — visual indicator of active heterofermentative fermentation.
   - Cloudy brine (suspended bacterial cells).
   - A tangy, slightly sour aroma (early lactic acid + acetic acid formation).
4. After 48 – 72 hours, the pH should drop to approximately **4.0 – 4.5**.

**pH monitoring calculation (KTU exam level):**

The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, although strictly used for buffer systems, gives an estimate of the buffering behavior in fermenting cabbage:

$$\text{pH} = \text{pK}_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$$

Where:
- $\text{pK}_a$ of lactic acid = **3.86** at 25°C
- $[\text{A}^-]$ = concentration of lactate ion
- $[\text{HA}]$ = concentration of undissociated lactic acid

At **pH 3.86** (pKa), exactly half of the lactic acid is dissociated.

The actual pH in sauerkraut is determined more by the **total acid produced** and the **buffering capacity of cabbage** (proteins, phosphates) than by simple Henderson-Hasselbalch equilibrium. Hence empirical pH measurement is the standard KTU laboratory method.

---

### **STEP 6 — Microbial Succession and Monitoring**

Monitor the pH and titratable acidity every 24 hours using:
- **Digital pH meter** (calibrated with pH 4.0 and pH 7.0 buffer solutions).
- **Titration with 0.1 N NaOH** using phenolphthalein indicator for titratable acidity.

**Titratable acidity calculation (standard KTU question):**

*Procedure:* Take **10 mL** of sauerkraut brine; dilute to **100 mL**; take a **10 mL aliquot** (= 1 mL original brine); titrate against **0.1 N NaOH** using 2 – 3 drops phenolphthalein until faint pink endpoint persists for 15 seconds.

Suppose the titre value $V_{\text{NaOH}}$ is **1.7 mL**.

$$\%\text{Acidity (as lactic acid)} = \frac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times \text{Equivalent weight of lactic acid}}{V_{\text{sample}} \times 1000} \times 100$$

Substituting values (equivalent weight of lactic acid = **90.08 g/eq**):

$$\%\text{Acidity} = \frac{1.7 \times 0.1 \times 90.08}{1.0 \times 1000} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Acidity} = \frac{15.31}{10.0} = 1.531\%$$

Since the value falls within **1.5 – 2.0%**, the sauerkraut is **properly fermented** and microbiologically safe.

---

### **STEP 7 — Maturation Phase**

1. Continue incubation at **18 – 22°C** for an additional **7 – 14 days** (total 2 – 3 weeks).
2. During this phase, *Lactobacillus plantarum* dominates.
3. The flavor becomes **milder and more aromatic**.
4. The product develops its characteristic **golden-white to pale yellow** color (original green chlorophyll is partially degraded by acid).
5. pH stabilizes at **3.4 – 3.6**.

---

### **STEP 8 — Quality Evaluation Parameters**

Standard KTU evaluation criteria for the prepared sauerkraut:

| Parameter | Method | Acceptable Value |
|---|---|---|
| pH | Digital pH meter | **3.4 – 3.6** |
| Titratable acidity | 0.1 N NaOH titration | **1.5 – 2.0% lactic acid** |
| Salt content | Mohr's method (AgNO₃ titration) | **1.0 – 1.5% w/w** |
| Color | Visual / Hunter colorimeter | Uniform pale yellow |
| Texture | Penetrometer (sensory) | Crisp, firm |
| Microbiological safety | Total plate count | < 10⁴ CFU/g |
| Flavor | Sensory (panel of 5–10) | Sour, mildly salty, no off-flavors |

---

### **STEP 9 — Storage and Preservation**

1. Once matured, the sauerkraut can be:
   - **Cold-stored** at 4°C in the same brine (stable for 6 – 12 months).
   - **Pasteurized** at 70 – 75°C for 15 minutes and hot-packed in glass jars (commercial shelf-stable product).
   - **Canned** in retort pouches (commercial Indian market product).
2. Avoid prolonged exposure to air; always keep submerged in brine.

---

### **STEP 10 — Defects and Troubleshooting (KTU Frequently Asked Topic)**

| Defect | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| **Pink kraut** | Growth of pigmented yeasts (*Rhodotorula*) in high-salt, low-acid conditions | Use proper salt level; ensure pH drops below 4.0 quickly |
| **Soft / slimy kraut** | Pectinolytic enzyme activity from late-stage *Bacillus* or fungal contamination | Use correct salt; maintain anaerobic conditions |
| **Dark / discolored kraut** | Maillard browning (high temperature fermentation) | Ferment at 18 – 22°C, not above 25°C |
| **Rancid / cheesy odor** | Butyric acid bacteria (*Clostridium*) growth | Use proper salt; exclude oxygen |
| **Excessive gas / bursting jars** | Overactive heterofermentative CO₂ production | Allow initial gas release; do not seal airtight in early phase |

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Fermentation Vessel

This block diagram represents the **functional zones** within a typical sauerkraut fermentation crock, illustrating the role of each physical layer in maintaining anaerobic conditions and preventing surface contamination.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Z1[Zone 5 Air Gap and Weight] --> Z2[Zone 4 Reserved Outer Leaves Barrier]
    Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3 Brine Layer 2 to 3 cm Anaerobic Seal]
    Z3 --> Z4[Zone 2 Submerged Shredded Cabbage Active Fermentation]
    Z4 --> Z5[Zone 1 Sediment and Microbial Floc at Base]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation of zones:**
> - **Zone 1 (Base sediment):** Inert cellulose, dead bacterial cells, and precipitated salts.
> - **Zone 2 (Submerged cabbage):** Active fermentation zone, dominated by LAB.
> - **Zone 3 (Brine seal):** Physical barrier preventing oxygen ingress and mold colonization.
> - **Zone 4 (Leaf barrier):** Secondary physical barrier; may also contribute minor LAB inoculum.
> - **Zone 5 (Weight and air gap):** Maintains constant pressure and headspace for CO₂ release.
