# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define sauerkraut. List the principal groups of microorganisms involved in its fermentation and state the role of salt.**

#### Model Answer:

**Sauerkraut** is a fermented white cabbage product produced by spontaneous lactic acid fermentation in the presence of 2.0 – 2.5% NaCl, where the natural sugars of cabbage are converted to lactic acid by epiphytic lactic acid bacteria, reducing the pH to 3.4 – 3.6 and yielding a microbiologically stable, mildly acidic product.

**Principal microbial groups involved (in chronological succession):**
1. *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* — initiator of fermentation (heterofermentative).
2. *Lactobacillus brevis* — co-dominant heterofermentative.
3. *Pediococcus cerevisiae* — homofermentative, intermediate phase.
4. *Lactobacillus plantarum* — final dominant homofermentative.

**Role of salt (NaCl):**
1. Osmotic extraction of cellular juice (sugars, vitamins, water) from cabbage.
2. Selective inhibition of spoilage Gram-negative bacteria and oxidative yeasts/molds.
3. Maintenance of crisp texture by inhibiting pectinolytic enzyme activity.

*(Marks distribution: Definition — 1 mark; Microbial groups — 1 mark; Role of salt — 1 mark.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Write the biochemical equations for heterofermentative and homofermentative lactic acid fermentation. Why is heterofermentative fermentation essential at the start of sauerkraut preparation?**

#### Model Answer:

**Heterofermentative pathway:**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2$$

**Homofermentative pathway:**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}$$

**Why heterofermentative fermentation is essential at the start:**

1. **Rapid pH drop:** *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* grows faster than homofermenters and quickly acidifies the medium, suppressing competing spoilage organisms.
2. **CO₂ production:** The CO₂ generated displaces residual oxygen, creating strictly anaerobic conditions necessary for the subsequent growth of obligate anaerobes.
3. **Aroma and flavor development:** Heterofermentative by-products (ethanol, acetic acid, diacetyl, acetaldehyde) contribute to the complex sensory profile of mature sauerkraut.
4. **Ecological succession:** The heterofermentative phase primes the environment for *Lactobacillus plantarum* to take over and complete fermentation as a homofermenter.

*(Marks distribution: Equations — 1.5 marks; Justification — 1.5 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step laboratory procedure for the preparation of sauerkraut**, including raw material selection, salt addition, packing, fermentation conditions, and storage. Highlight the key biochemical changes occurring at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **microbial succession** during sauerkraut fermentation. Explain the role of each organism and the metabolic pathway it follows. Why is *Lactobacillus plantarum* the final dominant organism? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**1. Raw material selection:**
- Mature, sound, white cabbage (*Brassica oleracea* var. *capitata*) is selected.
- Damaged outer leaves and core are removed; the head is halved or quartered.
- Cabbage is washed in chlorinated water (50 ppm) to reduce surface microbial contamination.

**2. Shredding:**
- The cabbage is shredded to a uniform thickness of 1 – 2 mm using a stainless steel shredder.
- Uniform shredding ensures uniform fermentation and consistent texture.

**3. Salt addition:**
- Food-grade non-iodized NaCl is added at **2.25% w/w** of the cabbage mass.
- The mixture is tumbled for 5 – 10 minutes to allow uniform salt distribution.
- Salt induces osmotic release of cell sap, forming natural brine.

**4. Packing and weighting:**
- The salted cabbage is firmly packed into a food-grade crock or jar.
- A clean weight (glass plate) is placed on top to ensure the cabbage remains submerged below the brine surface.
- The reserved outer cabbage leaves are placed as a secondary barrier against surface contamination.

**5. Fermentation conditions:**
- The vessel is maintained at **18 – 22°C** for 3 – 4 weeks.
- pH and titratable acidity are monitored every 24 hours.

**6. Biochemical changes:**
- **Day 0 – 1:** Salt extraction; cellular sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose) leach out.
- **Day 1 – 7:** Heterofermentative fermentation by *Leuconostoc*; pH falls to ~4.0; CO₂ released; lactic acid, ethanol, acetic acid produced.
- **Day 7 – 21:** Homofermentative fermentation by *L. plantarum*; pH stabilizes at 3.4 – 3.6; lactic acid concentration reaches 1.5 – 2.0%.

**7. Storage:**
- Mature sauerkraut is stored at **4°C** in the same brine.
- For commercial products, pasteurization at 70 – 75°C for 15 min and hot-pack canning is used.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Microbial succession and metabolic pathways:**

| Phase | Dominant Organism | Type | Products | pH Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initiation (Day 1 – 3) | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* | Heterofermentative | Lactic acid, ethanol, CO₂ | 6.0 → 4.5 |
| Co-dominant (Day 3 – 5) | *Lactobacillus brevis* | Heterofermentative | Lactic acid, acetic acid, CO₂ | 4.5 → 4.0 |
| Transition (Day 5 – 7) | *Pediococcus cerevisiae* | Homofermentative | Lactic acid (only) | 4.0 → 3.8 |
| Final (Day 7 – 21) | *Lactobacillus plantarum* | Homofermentative | Lactic acid (only) | 3.8 → 3.4 |

**Why *Lactobacillus plantarum* is the final dominant organism:**

1. **Acid tolerance:** *L. plantarum* can grow at pH as low as 3.5, while *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* is inhibited below pH 4.5.
2. **Homofermentative efficiency:** It produces only lactic acid (no CO₂ or ethanol), giving maximum acid yield per mole of sugar.
3. **Substrate preference:** It ferments both hexose and pentose sugars efficiently.
4. **Ecological dominance:** As pH drops, the heterofermenters self-inhibit; *L. plantarum* outcompetes the remaining flora in the acidic environment.
5. **Final product stability:** The high lactic acid concentration produced by *L. plantarum* ensures long-term microbiological stability of sauerkraut.

*(Marks distribution: (a) — Detailed procedure with biochemical changes — 7 marks; (b) — Microbial succession with rationale — 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B — Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **role of salt in sauerkraut preparation**. Discuss the effects of using (i) too little salt and (ii) too much salt on the quality of the final product. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **quality evaluation parameters** of sauerkraut. Explain the determination of titratable acidity with a sample calculation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Role of salt (NaCl) in sauerkraut preparation:**

1. **Osmotic extraction of cellular juice:** Salt draws water and dissolved sugars from shredded cabbage cells, forming natural brine. This brine is the medium in which fermentation occurs.
2. **Selective microbial inhibition:** Salt suppresses Gram-negative spoilage bacteria (*Enterobacteriaceae*, *Pseudomonas*) and aerobic yeasts/molds, while allowing salt-tolerant LAB to flourish.
3. **Texture preservation:** Salt inhibits pectinolytic enzymes (polygalacturonases) that would otherwise soften the cabbage tissues.
4. **Flavor contribution:** Salt provides the characteristic mildly salty base note of the final product.
5. **Water activity reduction:** Salt lowers the water activity (a_w) of the brine, contributing to microbial stability.

**Effects of incorrect salt levels:**

**(i) Too little salt (< 1.5% w/w):**
- Insufficient inhibition of spoilage organisms.
- Soft, mushy texture due to active pectinolysis.
- Overgrowth of aerobic bacteria and molds; surface pellicle formation.
- Uncontrolled mixed fermentation producing off-flavors (butyric, putrefactive).
- Risk of *Clostridium*-type butyric acid fermentation (foul odor).

**(ii) Too much salt (> 3.0% w/w):**
- Severe inhibition of LAB, leading to **sluggish or stuck fermentation**.
- Excessive shrinkage and saltiness of the final product.
- Growth of salt-tolerant pigmented yeasts (*Rhodotorula*) causing **pink kraut** defect.
- Reduced vitamin C retention.
- Hard, leathery texture.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Quality evaluation parameters of sauerkraut:**

1. **pH** — measured using a calibrated digital pH meter; acceptable range **3.4 – 3.6**.
2. **Titratable acidity** — measured by titration with 0.1 N NaOH; acceptable range **1.5 – 2.0% (as lactic acid)**.
3. **Salt content** — measured by Mohr's method using 0.1 N AgNO₃; acceptable range **1.0 – 1.5% w/w**.
4. **Moisture content** — measured by oven drying at 105°C to constant weight.
5. **Color** — assessed visually or by Hunter Lab colorimeter; uniform pale yellow.
6. **Texture** — assessed by penetration test (penetrometer) and sensory panel.
7. **Microbiological quality** — total plate count < 10⁴ CFU/g; absence of coliforms.
8. **Sensory evaluation** — flavor, aroma, color, and overall acceptability.

**Sample calculation — Titratable acidity:**

*Procedure:* 10 mL of sauerkraut brine is diluted to 100 mL; 10 mL of this diluted solution (equivalent to 1 mL original brine) is titrated against 0.1 N NaOH using phenolphthalein indicator until a faint pink endpoint persists for 15 seconds. Suppose the titre is **1.8 mL**.

$$\%\text{Titratable Acidity} = \frac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times \text{Equivalent weight of lactic acid}}{V_{\text{sample}} \times 1000} \times 100$$

Substituting:
- $V_{\text{NaOH}} = 1.8\,\text{mL}$
- $N_{\text{NaOH}} = 0.1\,\text{N}$
- Equivalent weight of lactic acid $\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}$ = **90.08 g/eq**
- $V_{\text{sample}} = 1.0\,\text{mL}$

$$\%\text{Acidity} = \frac{1.8 \times 0.1 \times 90.08}{1.0 \times 1000} \times 100 = 1.62\%$$

Since 1.62% falls within the acceptable range of **1.5 – 2.0% lactic acid**, the sauerkraut sample is **properly fermented and acceptable**.

*(Marks distribution: (a) — Role of salt with effects of low/high levels — 7 marks; (b) — Quality parameters with detailed titration calculation — 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students marks in sauerkraut-related questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the % salt in brine vs. % salt in cabbage** — When a question states "2.25% salt," students must clarify whether this refers to **w/w of cabbage** or **w/w of total brine**. The KTU convention is **w/w of cabbage**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the equivalent weight of lactic acid (90.08) in titration problems** — A full calculation requires the **equivalent weight**, not the molecular weight. Marks are deducted for using the wrong value.
> 3. **Mixing up heterofermentative and homofermentative products** — *Leuconostoc* produces lactic acid + ethanol + CO₂; *L. plantarum* produces only lactic acid. Writing "L. plantarum produces ethanol" is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Omitting the role of CO₂** — Many students write that *Leuconostoc* produces "lactic acid" but forget that the **CO₂ released creates the anaerobic environment** essential for the entire fermentation. This is a **high-value conceptual point** worth at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the final pH range** — Always mention **pH 3.4 – 3.6** when describing the completion of fermentation. A vague statement like "pH becomes acidic" will receive partial credit at best.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid revision checklist for Sauerkraut Preparation:**

- ✅ Sauerkraut is a **lactic acid-fermented white cabbage** product with characteristic **pH 3.4 – 3.6** and **1.5 – 2.0% titratable acidity**.
- ✅ Standard salt level: **2.25% (w/w of cabbage)**, always using **non-iodized food-grade NaCl**.
- ✅ Fermentation temperature: **18 – 22°C** for **2 – 3 weeks**.
- ✅ Microbial succession: *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* → *Lactobacillus brevis* → *Pediococcus cerevisiae* → *Lactobacillus plantarum*.
- ✅ **Heterofermentative** (Phase 2) produces: lactic acid + ethanol + CO₂; **Homofermentative** (Phase 3) produces: lactic acid only.
- ✅ Heterofermentative equation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \to \text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2$.
- ✅ Homofermentative equation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \to 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}$.
- ✅ Three roles of salt: **osmotic extraction**, **selective microbial inhibition**, **texture preservation**.
- ✅ Brine seal (2 – 3 cm above cabbage) is **mandatory** for anaerobic conditions.
- ✅ Common defects: **pink kraut** (yeast), **soft kraut** (pectinolysis), **dark kraut** (Maillard browning).
- ✅ Titratable acidity formula: $\%\text{Acidity} = \frac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 90.08}{V_{\text{sample}} \times 1000} \times 100$.
- ✅ Storage: **4°C in brine** (refrigerated) or **pasteurized at 70 – 75°C / 15 min** (commercial).
- ✅ Sauerkraut is a model example of **spontaneous mixed-culture submerged lactic acid fermentation**.
- ✅ Industrial applications extend to **kimchi, pickles, olives, and silage production**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Squash
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Squash

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SQUASH (as per FSSAI / BIS Standards):** A concentrated fruit beverage product prepared by mixing fruit juice/pulp/puree with sugar syrup, water, and permitted preservatives, formulated such that the final product requires dilution with water (typically in the ratio **1:4 to 1:6**) before consumption. The finished squash must contain a minimum of **25% fruit juice/pulp** and **40–50% total soluble solids (TSS)**, with acidity (as citric acid) not less than **1.0%** and not more than **3.5%** by weight.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Food Technology laboratory curriculum (PCFTL507)**, the experiment *"Preparation of Squash"* is classified as a **preservation and value-addition experiment** under the broader module dealing with fruit and vegetable processing techniques. The student is expected to demonstrate competency in raw material selection, formulation calculation, thermal processing, and quality evaluation of a commercially scalable beverage concentrate.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of squash as a "concentrated fruit memory."**
> Imagine you have a glass of fresh orange juice. If you could remove the water, sweeten the remaining juice heavily, add a pinch of "time-stopping" magic (preservative), and bottle it — you would have a product that sits on a shelf for months, and when you pour a small amount into a glass and add water, you instantly get back the original juice. **That bottled "memory" is squash.** It is essentially fruit juice that has been "shrunk" so that it is easier to ship, store, and serve.

**Common Real-World Examples:**
- *Fanta, Real, Paper Boat* – commercial squashes
- *Homemade lemon squash, mango squash, pineapple squash* – traditional summer drinks
- *Rooh Afza* – a rose-flavoured squash variant from the Indian subcontinent

### 1.3 Standardized Quality Benchmarks (BIS / FSSAI)

| Parameter | Standard Specification | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Fruit Content (min.)** | **25 %** | Ensures authentic fruit character |
| **Total Soluble Solids (TSS)** | **40 – 50 °Brix** | Osmotic preservation + sweetness |
| **Acidity (as Citric Acid)** | **1.0 – 3.5 %** | Microbial inhibition + flavour |
| **Preservative (KMS / SO₂)** | **≤ 350 ppm (SO₂)** | Anti-microbial shelf life |
| **Dilution Ratio (Serving)** | **1:4 to 1:6 (Squash:Water)** | Serves 4–6 glasses per 200 mL |
| **Shelf Life** | **6 – 12 months (ambient)** | Commercial marketability |
| **pH** | **2.5 – 3.8** | Inhibits pathogen growth |

> [!TIP]
> The trio of **High Sugar + High Acid + Preservative** is the "trinity of preservation" that makes squash shelf-stable without refrigeration. This is the underlying principle students must internalize.

---

### 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for KTU 2024

| CO Code | Course Outcome Description | How This Experiment Maps |
|---------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| **CO2** | Demonstrate unit operations in fruit & vegetable processing | Pre-processing, mixing, heating |
| **CO3** | Apply preservation principles in food product development | Chemical + osmotic preservation |
| **CO4** | Evaluate quality of processed food products | TSS, acidity, sensory evaluation |
| **CO5** | Document lab work as per industrial standards | Record writing, standard protocols |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Science Behind Squash — Why It Works

Squash is a brilliant example of **hurdle technology**, where multiple preservation mechanisms ("hurdles") act simultaneously to inhibit microbial spoilage. Each hurdle targets a different weakness of microorganisms.

**The Five Hurdles in Squash:**

1. **High Sugar Concentration (Osmotic Pressure)**
   - Sugar reduces water activity ($a_w$) by binding free water molecules.
   - When $a_w < 0.86$, most bacteria cannot grow; at $a_w < 0.70$, most yeasts and moulds cease.
   - A **45 °Brix** squash has $a_w \approx 0.85$, sufficient to halt most spoilage microbes.

2. **Low pH (Acid Hurdle)**
   - Citric acid lowers pH to **2.5 – 3.5**.
   - At pH < 3.8, *Clostridium botulinum* (the most dangerous food pathogen) cannot produce toxin.
   - Most spoilage bacteria prefer pH > 4.5.

3. **Chemical Preservatives (KMS / Sodium Benzoate)**
   - **Potassium Metabisulphite (KMS)** releases **SO₂**, which:
     - Inhibits enzymes (polyphenol oxidase, catalase)
     - Reacts with thiamine to suppress microbial growth
     - Acts as an antioxidant (prevents browning)
   - **Sodium Benzoate** is effective below pH 4.5; inhibits moulds and yeasts effectively.

4. **Heat Treatment (Pasteurization)**
   - Brief heating to **80 – 85 °C for 10 – 15 minutes** destroys vegetative cells of yeast, mould, and acid-tolerant bacteria.
   - Does NOT sterilize; relies on combined hurdles for shelf stability.

5. **Packaging (Hermetic Seal in Glass / PET)**
   - Prevents recontamination.
   - Amber-coloured bottles protect from UV-induced vitamin degradation.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas are examination-critical.** KTU 2024 scheme questions are often "calculation-type" where students must use the **Pearson's Square, Brix-Acid blend rules, and dilution formulas.**

#### **A. Pearson's Square Method (For Sugar Syrup Calculation)**

Used to determine the quantity of sugar and water required to achieve a desired Brix.

$$
\text{Quantity of Sugar} = \frac{\text{Syrup Quantity} \times (\text{Desired Brix} - \text{Water Brix})}{\text{Sugar Brix} - \text{Water Brix}}
$$

$$
\text{Quantity of Water} = \text{Syrup Quantity} - \text{Quantity of Sugar}
$$

> Here, **Sugar Brix = 100**, **Water Brix = 0**.

#### **B. Final Squash Formulation (Mass Balance)**

Let total batch weight = $W_{total}$ kg. The mass balance for each component:

$$
W_{juice} + W_{syrup} + W_{acid} + W_{preservative} = W_{total}
$$

For **1 kg of finished squash (standard formulation):**

| Component | Quantity | % of Batch |
|-----------|----------|------------|
| Fruit juice/pulp | **250 g** | **25 %** |
| Sugar syrup (65 °Brix) | **700 g** | **70 %** |
| Citric acid | **15 g** | **1.5 %** |
| KMS (as powder) | **0.7 – 1.0 g** | **0.07 – 0.1 %** |
| Water | Balance to 1 kg | **≈ 3.3 %** |

#### **C. Acidity Adjustment Formula**

To increase acidity by 1% in $M$ kg of squash using citric acid:

$$
W_{citric} = \frac{M \times (\text{Required \% acidity} - \text{Existing \% acidity})}{100 - \text{Required \% acidity}} \;\; \text{(kg)}
$$

#### **D. Sugar Syrup Preparation (Boiling Calculation)**

$$
\text{Sugar (kg)} = \text{Water (L)} \times \left( \frac{\text{Desired Brix}}{100 - \text{Desired Brix}} \right)
$$

#### **E. TSS (°Brix) Reading Correction for Temperature**

$$
\text{Corrected Brix} = \text{Observed Brix} + \text{Temperature Correction Factor}
$$

(Correction table is provided with every refractometer; usually +0.5 °Brix per 5 °C above 20 °C.)

#### **F. Dilution Rule for Serving**

$$
V_{water} = V_{serving} \times \text{Dilution Factor} - V_{serving}
$$

For a **200 mL glass** at **1:4** dilution:

$$
V_{serving} = \frac{200}{1 + 4} = 40 \;\text{mL of squash} + 160 \;\text{mL of water}
$$

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Role of Squash Technology |
|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Beverage Industry (e.g., Dabur, Patanjali)** | Base technology for *jeera, rose, fruit* concentrates |
| **Food Service & HORECA** | Cost-effective bulk fruit flavouring |
| **Defence / Armed Forces Rations** | Long-shelf-life vitamin C source |
| **Ayurvedic Tonics (e.g., Chyawanprash liquid)** | Sweetened herbal concentrates use same principle |
| **Pharmaceutical Syrups** | Identical sugar-acid-preservative system |
| **Export Trade** | Reduced water content → cheaper logistics |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Companies like **Mapro, Kissan, and ITC** use industrialized versions of this very experiment, scaled to **10,000+ L batches** in stainless-steel mixing tanks with automated Brix sensors and inline pH probes.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Sugar Syrup Calculation (Pearson's Square)

> **Problem:** A student needs to prepare **1 kg of 65 °Brix sugar syrup** for squash. Calculate the quantity of sugar and water required.

**Given:**
- Desired syrup quantity: $W_{syrup} = 1.0 \;\text{kg}$
- Desired Brix of syrup: $B_{desired} = 65\;^{\circ}\text{Brix}$
- Sugar purity (Brix): $B_{sugar} = 100$
- Water Brix: $B_{water} = 0$

**Step 1 — Apply the Pearson's Square formula for sugar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{sugar} &= \frac{W_{syrup} \times (B_{desired} - B_{water})}{B_{sugar} - B_{water}} \\
&= \frac{1.0 \times (65 - 0)}{100 - 0} \\
&= \frac{65}{100} \\
&= 0.65 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

So, **650 g of sugar** is required.

**Step 2 — Calculate water required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{water} &= W_{syrup} - W_{sugar} \\
&= 1.0 - 0.65 \\
&= 0.35 \;\text{kg} \approx 350 \;\text{mL of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification by mass balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSS in syrup} &= W_{sugar} \times B_{sugar}/100 \\
&= 0.65 \times 100/100 = 0.65 \;\text{kg} \\
\text{Total Brix} &= \frac{0.65}{1.0} \times 100 = 65\;^{\circ}\text{Brix} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Mix **650 g sugar + 350 mL water**, boil to dissolve, and cool — yields 1 kg of 65 °Brix syrup.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Final Squash Formulation (Mass Balance)

> **Problem:** Prepare **5 kg of mango squash** with the following specifications:
> - Fruit content: **25 %**
> - TSS (final): **45 °Brix**
> - Acidity: **1.5 %** (as citric acid)
> - KMS: **0.08 %** (= 800 ppm)
> - Use 65 °Brix sugar syrup from previous example.

**Given:** $W_{total} = 5 \;\text{kg} = 5000 \;\text{g}$

**Step 1 — Calculate fruit juice/pulp needed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{juice} &= W_{total} \times 25\% \\
&= 5000 \times 0.25 \\
&= 1250 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate KMS (preservative) needed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{KMS} &= W_{total} \times 0.08\% \\
&= 5000 \times 0.0008 \\
&= 4.0 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate citric acid needed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{acid} &= W_{total} \times 1.5\% \\
&= 5000 \times 0.015 \\
&= 75 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate sugar syrup required (by mass balance):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{syrup} &= W_{total} - (W_{juice} + W_{KMS} + W_{acid}) \\
&= 5000 - (1250 + 4 + 75) \\
&= 5000 - 1329 \\
&= 3671 \;\text{g} \approx 3.67 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the final TSS using weighted Brix blend:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{final} &= \frac{\sum (W_i \times B_i)}{W_{total}} \\
&= \frac{(1250 \times 12) + (3671 \times 65) + (75 \times 100) + (4 \times 0)}{5000}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Assume mango pulp has ~12 °Brix; citric acid solid = 100 Brix; KMS solid = 0 Brix)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{15000 + 238615 + 7500 + 0}{5000} \\
&= \frac{261115}{5000} \\
&\approx 52.2\;^{\circ}\text{Brix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — If final Brix is too high, dilute with water:**

Required final Brix = **45 °Brix**; current Brix = **52.2 °Brix**.

Since $52.2 > 45$, we need to dilute.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{water\;to\;add} &= W_{total} \times \left(\frac{B_{current} - B_{required}}{B_{required}}\right) \\
&= 5000 \times \left(\frac{52.2 - 45}{45}\right) \\
&= 5000 \times \frac{7.2}{45} \\
&= 5000 \times 0.16 \\
&= 800 \;\text{g of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Recalculate final batch weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total,final} &= 5000 + 800 = 5800 \;\text{g} = 5.8 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now the percentages change slightly. Adjust by maintaining ratios if exact compliance is needed (round to **6 kg final batch** for practical purposes).

**Final Recipe (per 5.8 kg finished mango squash):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. Mango pulp:} && 1.250 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{2. Sugar syrup (65 °Brix):} && 3.671 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{3. Citric acid:} && 0.075 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{4. KMS:} && 0.004 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{5. Potable water (to adjust Brix):} && 0.800 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Acidity Correction

> **Problem:** After preparation, the squash shows acidity of **0.8 %** by titration, but the FSSAI minimum is **1.0 %**. How much citric acid must be added to **5 kg of squash**?

**Given:**
- $M = 5 \;\text{kg}$
- Existing acidity $A_{existing} = 0.8\%$
- Required acidity $A_{required} = 1.0\%$

**Apply the acidity correction formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{citric\;to\;add} &= \frac{M \times (A_{required} - A_{existing})}{100 - A_{required}} \\
&= \frac{5 \times (1.0 - 0.8)}{100 - 1.0} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 0.2}{99} \\
&= \frac{1.0}{99} \\
&\approx 0.0101 \;\text{kg} \\
&\approx 10.1 \;\text{g of citric acid}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Add **~10 g of citric acid**, stir thoroughly, and re-verify by titration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why denominator is (100 − A_required)?** Because the added citric acid itself contributes 100% "acid content" to the small additional mass, and we are solving for the *extra* mass needed, not the total.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Complete Squash Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receiving Mango Orange Pineapple Lemon] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Washing Clean potable water 50 ppm chlorine]
    C --> D[Peeling and Destoning]
    D --> E[Pulp or Juice Extraction Pulper or Juicer]
    E --> F[Pulp / Juice Standardization Sieve to 0.5 mm]
    F --> G[Sugar Syrup Preparation Sugar plus Water 65 Brix]
    G --> H[Boiling and Straining]
    H --> I[Mixing Tank Juice plus Syrup plus Acid]
    I --> J[Addition of Preservative KMS or Sodium Benzoate]
    J --> K[Quality Check Brix Acidity pH]
    K --> L{Brix 45 to 50?}
    L -- No --> M[Adjustment Add water or sugar]
    M --> K
    L -- Yes --> N[Heat Pasteurization 80 to 85 C for 15 min]
    N --> O[Hot Filling at 80 C into sterilized bottles]
    O --> P[Sealing Crown cap or screw cap]
    P --> Q[Cooling and Labeling]
    Q --> R[Storage Ambient 25 C Shelf life 6 to 12 months]
```

### 4.2 Hurdle Technology Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PresHurdles[Five Preservation Hurdles in Squash]
        H1[Hurdle 1 High Sugar 45 Brix Low aw]
        H2[Hurdle 2 Low pH 2.5 to 3.5]
        H3[Hurdle 3 KMS 700 ppm SO2]
        H4[Hurdle 4 Heat Pasteurization 85 C]
        H5[Hurdle 5 Hermetic Sealed Packaging]
    end
    subgraph Target[Target Microbes]
        T1[Yeasts and Moulds]
        T2[Acid Tolerant Bacteria]
        T3[Spore Formers]
    end
    H1 --> T1
    H2 --> T2
    H3 --> T1
    H3 --> T2
    H4 --> T1
    H4 --> T2
    H4 --> T3
    H5 --> T1
    H5 --> T2
    H5 --> T3
```

### 4.3 Quality Control Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Finished Squash Sample]) --> q1{TSS by Refractometer}
    q1 -->|Less than 40 Brix| a1[Reject Add Sugar Syrup]
    q1 -->|More than 50 Brix| a2[Reject Dilute with Water]
    q1 -->|40 to 50 Brix| q2{pH by Digital Meter}
    q2 -->|Less than 2.5| a3[Reject Too Acidic Add Water]
    q2 -->|More than 3.8| a4[Reject Add Citric Acid]
    q2 -->|2.5 to 3.8| q3{Sensory Evaluation}
    q3 -->|Off Flavour| a5[Reject Check Preservative]
    q3 -->|Acceptable| ok[Approved for Packaging and Storage]
    a1 --> q1
    a2 --> q1
    a3 --> q2
    a4 --> q2
    a5 --> q3
```

### 4.4 Equipment Layout (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Equip[Equipment Required for Laboratory Scale Preparation]
        E1[Stainless Steel Vessel]
        E2[Gas Burner or Hot Plate]
        E3[Hand Refractometer 0 to 80 Brix]
        E4[Digital pH Meter]
        E5[Burette and Titration Setup]
        E6[Glass Thermometer 0 to 200 C]
        E7[Pulper or Juicer]
        E8[Weighing Balance 0.1 g accuracy]
        E9[Amber Glass Bottles with Caps]
        E10[Stainless Steel Strainer 100 mesh]
    end
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define squash. Mention any two BIS quality parameters for squash.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Squash is a concentrated fruit beverage prepared by combining fruit juice or pulp (minimum 25%) with sugar syrup, permitted acidulant, and preservative, formulated for dilution with water before serving. **Two BIS/FSSAI quality parameters:**
- **TSS:** Not less than **40 °Brix** (typically 40–50)
- **Acidity (as citric acid):** Not less than **1.0%** and not more than **3.5%**

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for parameters)*

---

#### **Q2. List the functions of potassium metabisulphite (KMS) in squash.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

KMS functions as:
1. **Antimicrobial agent:** Releases SO₂ that inhibits yeasts, moulds, and bacteria.
2. **Antioxidant:** Prevents browning by inhibiting polyphenol oxidase (PPO) enzyme.
3. **Preservative:** Extends shelf life by combining with thiamine in microbial cells.

*(3 marks: 1 point = 1 mark each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step laboratory procedure for the preparation of mango squash**, including formulation, processing, and quality control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle of hurdle technology** as applied to squash preservation. How do sugar, acid, and preservatives work synergistically? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

1. **Raw Material Selection:** Ripe, sound mangoes (Totapuri/ Alphonso) of TSS ≥ 14°Brix. (0.5 mark)
2. **Washing:** Tap water + 50 ppm chlorinated water to reduce microbial load. (0.5 mark)
3. **Peeling & Pulping:** Manual peeling; pulping through 0.5 mm sieve. (0.5 mark)
4. **Sugar Syrup Preparation:** Dissolve **650 g sugar in 350 mL water**, boil for 5 min, cool, strain. (1 mark)
5. **Mixing (Standard Formulation for 1 kg squash):**
   - Mango pulp — 250 g
   - 65 °Brix sugar syrup — 700 g
   - Citric acid — 15 g
   - KMS — 0.7 g
   - Water — q.s. (1 mark)
6. **Addition of Preservative & Acid:** Dissolve KMS in small quantity of pulp; add citric acid last to preserve colour. (0.5 mark)
7. **Pasteurization:** Heat to **80–85 °C for 15 minutes**; do NOT boil vigorously. (1 mark)
8. **Hot Filling:** Pour at 80 °C into pre-sterilized amber glass bottles. (0.5 mark)
9. **Sealing & Cooling:** Crown-cap immediately; invert bottles for 2 min, then cool gradually. (0.5 mark)
10. **Quality Control:** Measure TSS (45°Brix), acidity (1.5%), pH (3.2), and conduct sensory evaluation. (1 mark)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Principle of Hurdle Technology:** Microbial spoilage is prevented by creating multiple simultaneous "hurdles" or stress conditions, each of which alone is insufficient but in combination provides synergistic preservation. (2 marks)

**Synergistic Action in Squash:**

- **Sugar (45 °Brix) → Low $a_w$ (~0.85):** Plasmolysis of microbial cells; halts metabolic activity. (1.5 marks)
- **Acid (pH 2.5–3.5) → Disrupts membrane transport:** Proton influx denatures internal enzymes of microbes. (1.5 marks)
- **KMS (SO₂) → Inhibits key enzymes:** Reacts with thiamine, NAD, and disrupts disulfide bonds. (1.5 marks)
- **Synergy Example:** A yeast cell under low $a_w$ becomes more susceptible to acid damage; SO₂ penetrates more easily through stressed membrane. Hence, *combined hurdles are far more potent than the sum of individual effects.* (0.5 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** list hurdles without explaining *how* each one damages microbes — many students write "Sugar, Acid, KMS, Heat" without mechanism. KTU deducts **2–3 marks** for missing mechanistic depth.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse *water activity* with *moisture content* — these are different concepts. $a_w$ is *available* water, not total water.
> 3. **Do NOT** skip the temperature/time in pasteurization — it is a frequently asked detail.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Calculate the quantities of **sugar, water, fruit pulp, citric acid, and KMS** required to prepare **10 kg of orange squash** with the following specifications: TSS = 45°Brix, Acidity = 1.5%, KMS = 0.08%, Fruit content = 25%, and Sugar syrup concentration = 65°Brix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the common **defects observed in squashes** and their preventive measures? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Fruit pulp required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{pulp} &= 10 \times 0.25 = 2.5 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{(1 mark)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Citric acid required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{citric} &= 10 \times 0.015 = 0.15 \;\text{kg} = 150 \;\text{g} \\
&\text{(1 mark)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — KMS required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{KMS} &= 10 \times 0.0008 = 0.008 \;\text{kg} = 8 \;\text{g} \\
&\text{(1 mark)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sugar syrup required (mass balance):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{syrup} &= 10 - (2.5 + 0.15 + 0.008) \\
&= 10 - 2.658 \\
&= 7.342 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{(1.5 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sugar and water in syrup (Pearson's Square for 65°Brix):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{sugar\;in\;syrup} &= 7.342 \times \frac{65}{100} = 4.77 \;\text{kg} \\
W_{water\;in\;syrup} &= 7.342 - 4.77 = 2.57 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{(2 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer for 10 kg Batch:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Orange pulp:} && 2.500 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{Sugar:} && 4.770 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{Water (for syrup):} && 2.570 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{Citric acid:} && 0.150 \;\text{kg} \\
&\text{KMS:} && 0.008 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

| Defect | Cause | Preventive Measure |
|--------|-------|---------------------|
| **Browning (Maillard)** | Excess heat, high pH, exposure to air | Use KMS, fill hot, seal quickly |
| **Fermentation / Blown bottles** | Inadequate KMS, low sugar, contaminated bottles | Sterilize bottles, use correct KMS dose |
| **Cloudiness / Sediment** | Excess pectin, improper straining | Filter through 100-mesh sieve, use clear juice |
| **Mould growth on top** | Insufficient sugar or KMS, headspace too large | Headspace < 2 cm, invert after sealing |
| **Crystallization of sugar** | Over-concentration of syrup | Maintain 65 °Brix max for syrup |
| **Off-flavour** | Use of overripe fruit, prolonged storage | Use fresh, ripe but firm fruit |
| **Vitamin C loss** | Heat, oxygen, light | Use amber bottles, hot-fill, store cool |

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for defect + cause + prevention = 1 per row × 7 = 7)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Numerical Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract** KMS, acid, and pulp weight before calculating sugar syrup → wrong answer, **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up Brix units** (°Brix vs. % sugar) — they are *not* always equal; in pure sucrose solution they match, but in real squash with fruit acids and other solids, there is a deviation of ±2 °Brix.
> 3. **Not specifying units** (g vs. kg) in the final answer — KTU deducts **0.5 mark** for unit ambiguity.
> 4. **Treating 65 °Brix syrup as 65% of the total batch** — it is 65% of the syrup weight alone.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Preparation of Squash**

- **Definition:** Concentrated fruit beverage requiring dilution before consumption.
- **Standard Composition:** 25% juice + 40–50 °Brix TSS + 1.0–3.5% acidity + KMS ≤ 350 ppm.
- **Pearson's Square Formula:** Use for sugar-water calculations in syrup preparation.
- **Sugar Syrup Standard:** **65 °Brix** is the industry default for squash.
- **Hurdles of Preservation:** Sugar (low $a_w$) + Acid (low pH) + KMS (SO₂) + Heat (pasteurization) + Hermetic Seal.
- **Pasteurization Conditions:** **80–85 °C for 10–15 minutes** (do NOT boil).
- **Critical Acidity:** Final pH must be **< 3.8** to inhibit *C. botulinum*.
- **KMS Function:** Antimicrobial + Antioxidant + Enzyme inhibitor.
- **Hot-Fill Principle:** Filling at 80 °C creates vacuum upon cooling — provides hermetic seal.
- **Mass Balance Rule:** $W_{juice} + W_{syrup} + W_{acid} + W_{KMS} + W_{water} = W_{total}$.
- **Dilution Ratio for Serving:** Typically **1:4 to 1:6** (squash:water).
- **Equipment Refractometer:** Always correct Brix for temperature (calibrated at 20 °C).
- **Bottles:** Use **amber glass** to prevent UV degradation of vitamin C and anthocyanins.
- **Common Defects:** Browning, fermentation, mould, crystallization, cloudiness.
- **Sensory Test Parameters:** Colour, aroma, taste, mouthfeel, aftertaste — usually 9-point hedonic scale.
- **FSSAI Reference Number:** As per FSSAI (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, **2011**, Sub-regulation **2.3.6** for fruit beverages.
- **Common Fruit Squashes Tested in KTU Labs:** Mango, Orange, Pineapple, Lemon, Grape, Pomegranate.
- **Last-Minute Tip:** Always mention *hurdle technology* in any theory question on preservation — it is a **high-yield KTU keyword** worth 1–2 bonus marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Dahi/Yoghurt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL507 - PCFTL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Dahi/Yoghurt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dahi (Curd)**: *Dahi* is a **fermented milk product** obtained by the **lactic acid fermentation of milk** through the action of specific bacterial cultures, primarily *Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus* and *Streptococcus thermophilus* (or traditional undefined mesophilic cultures). The fermentation converts **lactose into lactic acid**, causing **coagulation of casein proteins** and producing a semi-solid, acidic, mildly tart product with characteristic flavor and texture. It is governed in India under the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (FSSAI)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Yoghurt**: A more standardized, globally-commercialized cousin of Dahi, defined by **CODEX ALIMENTARIUS (CXS 243-2003)** as a **coagulated milk product** obtained by the **fermentation of milk** using a defined symbiotic culture of *Streptococcus thermophilus* and *Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus*. The minimum titratable acidity required is **0.9% lactic acid**, and milk-fat and milk-protein composition must meet minimum standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine milk as a **busy city** where tiny protein molecules (**casein micelles**) float around in a stable, neutral environment (pH ~6.7) — they repel each other gently (negative charge) and stay dissolved. Now, introduce a **friendly bacterial workforce** that eats the sugar (lactose) in the city and excretes **lactic acid** as waste. As the acid builds up, the **pH drops** below the **isoelectric point of casein (pH 4.6)**. At this critical pH, the negative charges on casein are neutralized, the micelles **clump together (coagulate)**, and the liquid milk transforms into a **soft gel** — that gel is your **Dahi/Yoghurt**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Summary:** Bacteria + Warm Milk + Time = Thick, Tangy, Nutritious Curd. The bacteria are "fermentation factories" that turn sugar into acid, and the acid "sets" the milk like a culinary Jell-O.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Fermentation Temperature (Mesophilic Dahi)** | **30 °C – 35 °C** |
| **Fermentation Temperature (Thermophilic Yoghurt)** | **40 °C – 45 °C** |
| **Isoelectric point of Casein (pI)** | **pH 4.6** |
| **Required Titratable Acidity (Yoghurt)** | **≥ 0.9 % lactic acid** |
| **Required Titratable Acidity (Dahi, FSSAI)** | **0.5 – 1.0 % lactic acid** |
| **Standard Inoculum Size** | **1 – 2 % of milk volume** |
| **Incubation Time** | **6 – 12 hours** |
| **Storage Temperature** | **4 – 5 °C** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microbial & Biochemical Basis

The preparation of Dahi/Yoghurt is a textbook case of **controlled lactic acid fermentation**. The process involves three converging biochemical events:

### Step 1 — Lactose Hydrolysis (Galactosidase Activity)
The bacterial enzyme **β-galactosidase** cleaves the disaccharide **lactose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁)** into its two monosaccharide units: **glucose** and **galactose**. This reaction is essential because the bacteria cannot directly metabolize the disaccharide form.

### Step 2 — Glycolysis to Pyruvate
The released **glucose** enters the **Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) glycolytic pathway**, producing **2 molecules of pyruvate** per glucose, with a net yield of **2 ATP**.

### Step 3 — Lactic Acid Production (Homolactic Fermentation)
Pyruvate is reduced to **lactic acid (C₃H₆O₃)** by the enzyme **lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)**, regenerating NAD⁺. Because **>90 %** of the sugar is converted solely to lactic acid (no CO₂ release), this is termed **homofermentation** — the defining metabolic signature of safe, non-bloating Dahi.

### Step 4 — Casein Coagulation (Colloidal Chemistry)
As lactic acid accumulates:
- The **pH falls progressively from ~6.7 toward 4.6**.
- At the **isoelectric point (pI = 4.6)**, casein micelles **lose their net negative surface charge** (Zeta potential approaches zero).
- The now-electrostatically-neutral micelles **aggregate into a continuous three-dimensional protein network**, trapping water, fat globules, and bacterial cells → this network is the **curd gel**.

## 2.2 Symbiotic Relationship (Protocooperation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two bacteria?** In commercial yoghurt, *S. thermophilus* and *L. bulgaricus* engage in a documented **protocooperation**:
> - *S. thermophilus* grows **faster**, produces **formic acid** and **CO₂**, and lowers redox potential — creating conditions favorable for *L. bulgaricus*.
> - *L. bulgaricus* **proteolyzes casein**, releasing **peptides and free amino acids (especially glycine, histidine)** that stimulate *S. thermophilus* growth.
> - **Result:** Faster acid production (shorter incubation), and characteristic flavor compounds (acetaldehyde, diacetyl) are generated.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Relationship | Meaning / Variable | Unit |
|------|------------------------|--------------------|------|
| 1 | $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-gal}} 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$ | Lactose hydrolysis to glucose + galactose | mass (g) |
| 2 | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}$ | Homofermentative conversion to lactic acid (per glucose) | mol |
| 3 | $\%\,\text{Acidity (as Lactic Acid)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.09}{W_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | Titratable acidity by titration | % w/w |
| 4 | $\text{Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{M_{\text{curd}}}{M_{\text{milk}}} \times 100$ | Process yield efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\text{TS Recovery} = \dfrac{\text{TS}_{\text{curd}}}{\text{TS}_{\text{milk}}} \times 100$ | Total solids recovery (no whey loss) | % |
| 6 | $\text{pH}_{\text{final}} \approx 4.4 - 4.6$ | Set point near casein pI | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Incubation} \propto \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{inoculation}}}$ | Time–temperature inverse relationship | hours |
| 8 | $\text{AFE} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{pH}}{\Delta t}$ | Average Fermentation/Acidification Rate | pU/hr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where 0.09** in formula (3) is the **equivalent weight of lactic acid (CH₃CHOHCOOH)**, i.e., **M/1 = 90/1 = 90 g/eq → 0.009 g/meq**. When 1 mL of **0.1 N NaOH** is used per 9 g of sample, it represents **0.1 % lactic acid** — the FSSAI titratable acidity benchmark.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Scale:** Understanding rheology (gel firmness) helps food engineers design **fermentation tanks, scraped-surface heat exchangers, and aseptic filling lines**.
- **Quality Control:** Titratable acidity (formula 3) is the **most-used QC parameter** in dairy plants; every batch must be checked before packaging.
- **Probiotic R&D:** Yoghurt is a **delivery vehicle for live probiotics** (L. acidophilus, Bifidobacterium) that survive gastric transit and confer gut-health benefits.
- **Waste Valorization:** The **whey** drained from set-type Dahi is rich in lactose and whey proteins — recovered for **whey powder, beverages, and infant formula**.
- **Shelf-Life Engineering:** Post-fermentation cold storage at **4 °C** slows residual bacterial metabolism, arresting over-acidification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Stoichiometric Derivation: Lactose → Lactic Acid

We will derive the **theoretical maximum lactic acid production** from a known mass of lactose, which is the cornerstone for predicting fermentation efficiency.

### Given Data (Assumed Standard Problem)
- Initial lactose in milk: $L_0 = 48$ g per litre (typical cow milk)
- Molar mass of lactose: $M_{\text{Lac}} = 342$ g·mol⁻¹
- Molar mass of lactic acid: $M_{\text{LA}} = 90$ g·mol⁻¹
- Molar mass of glucose: $M_{\text{Glc}} = 180$ g·mol⁻¹

### Step 1 — Hydrolyze Lactose into Glucose and Galactose

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} &\longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;(\text{glucose}) + \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;(\text{galactose}) \\[4pt]
\text{1 mol lactose} &\longrightarrow \text{1 mol glucose} + \text{1 mol galactose}
\end{aligned}
$$

Moles of lactose initially:
$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{Lac}} &= \frac{L_0}{M_{\text{Lac}}} = \frac{48}{342} \\[4pt]
n_{\text{Lac}} &= 0.1404 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is also the moles of **glucose + galactose** formed (1:1 stoich).

### Step 2 — Convert Glucose to Lactic Acid via Glycolysis + LDH Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 &\longrightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} \\[4pt]
\text{1 mol glucose} &\longrightarrow \text{2 mol lactic acid}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore moles of lactic acid produced:
$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{LA}} &= 2 \times n_{\text{Glc}} = 2 \times 0.1404 \\[4pt]
n_{\text{LA}} &= 0.2807 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert Moles of Lactic Acid to Grams

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{LA}} &= n_{\text{LA}} \times M_{\text{LA}} = 0.2807 \times 90 \\[4pt]
m_{\text{LA}} &= 25.27 \text{ g per litre of milk}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Theoretical Titratable Acidity

Since titratable acidity is reported as % w/v lactic acid in the final product (assuming ~1 L of curd from 1 L of milk):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Acidity}_{\text{theo}} &= \frac{25.27}{1000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\%\,\text{Acidity}_{\text{theo}} &= 2.53\,\%\,\text{lactic acid}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, **only ~30–40 %** of lactose is converted before the curd "sets" (because pH 4.6 stops further metabolism). Hence the observed acidity in a set Dahi is typically **0.5 – 1.0 %**, not the theoretical 2.53 %.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Titratable Acidity from a Lab Titration

**Problem:** 9 g of well-mixed Dahi sample is titrated against **0.1 N NaOH** using phenolphthalein indicator. The burette reading is **8.5 mL**. Calculate the titratable acidity as % lactic acid.

### Step 1 — Recall the Master Formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Acidity} = \frac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.09}{W_{\text{sample}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Acidity} = \frac{8.5 \times 0.1 \times 0.09}{9} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the Numerator
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 8.5 \times 0.1 \times 0.09 \\[4pt]
&= 0.85 \times 0.09 \\[4pt]
&= 0.0765
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Division and Multiplication
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Acidity} &= \frac{0.0765}{9} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.0085 \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.85\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The sample has **0.85 % lactic acid**, which lies comfortably within the FSSAI specification for Dahi (0.5 – 1.0 %). The product is **acceptable for consumption**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Dahi vs. Yoghurt

| Parameter | Dahi (Indian) | Yoghurt (International) |
|---|---|---|
| **Culture Type** | Mixed mesophilic, often undefined ("back-slop") | Defined: *S. thermophilus* + *L. bulgaricus* |
| **Optimum Temperature** | 30 – 35 °C | 40 – 45 °C |
| **Incubation Time** | 12 – 18 h | 4 – 8 h |
| **Final Acidity (% LA)** | 0.5 – 1.0 | ≥ 0.9 (Codex) |
| **Final pH** | 4.0 – 4.6 | 4.0 – 4.5 |
| **Starter Form** | Liquid "mother culture" or freeze-dried | Commercial freeze-dried DVS (Direct Vat Set) |
| **Body & Texture** | Loose, often wheying-off | Firm gel (set) or viscous (stirred) |
| **Governing Standard** | FSSAI (India) | Codex CXS 243-2003 |
| **Flavor Compound** | Lactic acid dominant | Acetaldehyde, diacetyl, lactic acid |
| **Shelf Life (refrigerated)** | 3 – 5 days | 14 – 21 days |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Dahi/Yoghurt Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Milk Reception & QC] --> B[Standardization to 3.5% Fat and 8.5% SNF]
    B --> C[Pre-heating at 60 C for 30 min]
    C --> D[Homogenization at 200 bar]
    D --> E[Heat Treatment Pasteurization at 90 C for 15 min]
    E --> F[Cooling to Inoculation Temp 42 C for Yoghurt OR 32 C for Dahi]
    F --> G[Inoculation with 1 to 2% Starter Culture]
    G --> H[Incubation in Fermentation Tank 4 to 12 hr]
    H --> I{Set Point Reached? pH 4.4 to 4.6}
    I -->|No| H
    I -->|Yes| J[Cooling to 4 to 5 C in Cold Room]
    J --> K[Optional Fruit Flavour and Color Addition]
    K --> L[Packaging in Cups or Pouches]
    L --> M[Cold Storage at 4 C for Distribution]
    M --> N[Retail and Consumption]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style N fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Biochemical Pathway Schematic: Lactose → Lactic Acid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Lactose C12H22O11] -->|beta-galactosidase enzyme| P2[Glucose C6H12O6]
    P2 -->|EMP Glycolysis Pathway| P3[Pyruvate CH3COCOO-]
    P3 -->|Lactate Dehydrogenase LDH| P4[Lactic Acid CH3CHOHCOOH]
    P4 -->|pH drops| P5[Casein pI 4.6 Reached]
    P5 -->|Charge Neutralization| P6[Casein Coagulation and Gel Formation]

    style P1 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style P6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Process Hierarchy: Three-Stage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stage1["STAGE 1: PREPARATION"]
        s1a[Raw Milk] --> s1b[Filtration] --> s1c[Standardization] --> s1d[Homogenization]
    end

    subgraph Stage2["STAGE 2: HEAT TREATMENT"]
        s2a[Pre-heating 60 C] --> s2b[Boiling 90 C 15 min] --> s2c[Cooling to 42 C or 32 C]
    end

    subgraph Stage3["STAGE 3: FERMENTATION & FINISHING"]
        s3a[Inoculation 1-2% Starter] --> s3b[Incubation 4-12 hr] --> s3c[Set Point Check] --> s3d[Cold Storage 4 C] --> s3e[Packaging]
    end

    Stage1 --> Stage2 --> Stage3

    style Stage1 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Stage2 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Stage3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]**
**Define Dahi. Name the microorganisms primarily responsible for its fermentation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Dahi** is a fermented milk product obtained by the **lactic acid fermentation of milk**, characterized by its semi-solid consistency and acidic flavor. The primary microorganisms involved are **mesophilic lactic acid bacteria**, specifically *Lactococcus lactis subsp. lactis*, *Lactococcus lactis subsp. cremoris*, and *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* in traditional preparations, or thermophilic cultures *Streptococcus thermophilus* and *Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus* in standardized versions. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question]**
**What is the isoelectric point of casein, and why is it significant in the preparation of Dahi?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **isoelectric point of casein is pH 4.6**, the pH at which the protein carries **zero net electrical charge**. It is significant because during Dahi preparation, as lactic acid bacteria ferment lactose into lactic acid, the **milk pH drops to this critical value**, causing the casein micelles to **lose electrostatic repulsion**, **aggregate**, and **coagulate** to form the characteristic semi-solid curd gel. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **biochemical mechanism of lactic acid fermentation** during Dahi preparation. Include the enzymes, substrates, and end products. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **standard laboratory protocol** for the preparation of Dahi, including milk standardization, pasteurization, inoculation, incubation, and quality evaluation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Lactose Hydrolysis (2 marks):**
The bacterial enzyme **β-galactosidase** hydrolyses the disaccharide **lactose** into its two monosaccharide constituents, **glucose** and **galactose**. This is the first committed step in the fermentation pathway.

**2. Glycolysis (3 marks):**
Glucose is metabolized through the **Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) glycolytic pathway**, a ten-step enzymatic process occurring in the bacterial cytoplasm. The end product is **pyruvate (CH₃COCOO⁻)**, with a net yield of **2 ATP** and **2 NADH** per glucose molecule.

**3. Lactic Acid Formation (2 marks):**
Pyruvate is reduced to **lactic acid (CH₃CHOHCOOH)** by the enzyme **lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)**, which reoxidizes NADH to NAD⁺, maintaining the redox balance essential for continued glycolysis. Since lactic acid is the **sole end product**, this is termed **homofermentation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state both **net ATP yield = 2** and that the process is **homofermentative** — examiners specifically look for these keywords.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. Milk Standardization (2 marks):**
Cow/buffalo milk is standardized to **3.5 % fat** and **8.5 % solids-not-fat (SNF)** to ensure uniform body, texture, and taste. The milk is filtered through muslin to remove gross impurities.

**2. Pasteurization (2 marks):**
The standardized milk is heated to **90 °C for 15 minutes** (or 72 °C for 15 seconds in HTST systems). This destroys **pathogenic bacteria, vegetative spoilage organisms, and natural milk enzymes** (e.g., lipases, proteases) that could otherwise interfere with fermentation. It also **denatures whey proteins**, improving gel firmness.

**3. Cooling and Inoculation (1 mark):**
The milk is cooled to the **inoculation temperature** — **42 °C for thermophilic yoghurt cultures** (S. thermophilus + L. bulgaricus) or **30 – 35 °C for mesophilic dahi cultures**. A starter culture is added at **1 – 2 % (v/v)** and mixed thoroughly.

**4. Incubation (1 mark):**
The inoculated milk is held at the inoculation temperature for **6 – 12 hours** undisturbed, allowing fermentation and curd setting. The endpoint is reached when the pH drops to **4.4 – 4.6** and the curd shows a clean whey break on tilting.

**5. Quality Evaluation (1 mark):**
The set Dahi is evaluated for **(i) titratable acidity** (0.5 – 1.0 % lactic acid by NaOH titration), **(ii) pH** (4.0 – 4.6), **(iii) organoleptic quality** (smooth body, clean acidic flavor, no gas or off-odors), and **(iv) microbial count** (total viable count should conform to FSSAI limits). It is then **cold-stored at 4 – 5 °C** to arrest further acidification.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Dahi and Yoghurt** with respect to cultures used, temperature of fermentation, acidity, and texture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **9 g sample of Dahi** is titrated against **0.1 N NaOH**, and the **volume consumed is 8.5 mL**. Calculate the titratable acidity as % lactic acid and comment on whether the product meets FSSAI specifications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | Dahi (7 marks breakdown) | Yoghurt |
|---|---|---|
| **Microbial Culture** (2 marks) | Mixed mesophilic undefined cultures (e.g., Lactococcus lactis, Leuconostoc), often propagated by back-slopping at household level | Defined thermophilic symbiotic culture: *S. thermophilus* + *L. bulgaricus*, supplied as standardized DVS (Direct Vat Set) |
| **Fermentation Temperature** (2 marks) | 30 – 35 °C (mesophilic) | 40 – 45 °C (thermophilic) |
| **Final Acidity** (1 mark) | 0.5 – 1.0 % lactic acid (FSSAI) | ≥ 0.9 % lactic acid (Codex CXS 243-2003) |
| **Body & Texture** (2 marks) | Loosely set, may show whey syneresis on standing | Firm, smooth gel (set-type) or viscous, defect-free (stirred-type) |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — State the Master Formula (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Acidity} = \frac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.09}{W_{\text{sample}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Acidity} = \frac{8.5 \times 0.1 \times 0.09}{9} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator Calculation (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 8.5 \times 0.1 \times 0.09 = 0.0765
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Calculation (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Acidity} = \frac{0.0765 \times 100}{9} = 0.85\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The titratable acidity of **0.85 %** lies **within the FSSAI acceptable range of 0.5 – 1.0 % lactic acid**. Therefore, the product **conforms to FSSAI specifications** and is fit for sale and consumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.09 factor** — It is the milli-equivalent weight of lactic acid, NOT 90. Marks will be docked heavily.
> 2. **Mixing up equivalent weight and molecular weight** — Lactic acid is **monoprotic** (1 carboxyl group), so Eq. Wt. = Mol. Wt. / 1 = 90/1 = 90 g/eq → 0.009 g/meq → 0.09 in numerator.
> 3. **Unit confusion** — Sample weight must be in **grams** and NaOH normality correctly identified.
> 4. **Skipping the "Comment" part** — FSSAI compliance commentary carries **1 mark**; do not ignore it.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points:**

- ✅ **Dahi** = Lactic acid fermented milk; **Yoghurt** = Standardized international version.
- ✅ Two principal organisms in **yoghurt**: ***Streptococcus thermophilus*** (rod-shaped *Streptococcus*, faster grower) + ***Lactobacillus bulgaricus*** (rod-shaped, slower, proteolytic).
- ✅ Traditional **Dahi cultures** are **mesophilic** (30 – 35 °C), undefined, and propagated by **back-slopping**.
- ✅ **Isoelectric point of casein = pH 4.6** — the magic number that triggers coagulation.
- ✅ Master **titratable acidity formula** must be memorized: $\%\,\text{Acidity} = (V \times N \times 0.09 \times 100)/W$.
- ✅ **FSSAI acidity specification for Dahi:** 0.5 – 1.0 % lactic acid; **Codex Yoghurt:** ≥ 0.9 % lactic acid.
- ✅ **Pasteurization (90 °C / 15 min)** is mandatory before inoculation to (a) kill pathogens, (b) denature whey proteins for firmer gel, (c) eliminate competing native milk flora.
- ✅ **Inoculum size:** 1 – 2 % (v/v); **Incubation time:** 6 – 12 hours.
- ✅ **Homofermentation** → only lactic acid, no CO₂, no bloating.
- ✅ **Protocooperation (symbiosis)** between the two yoghurt strains reduces fermentation time and develops characteristic flavor (acetaldehyde, diacetyl).
- ✅ **Cold storage at 4 – 5 °C** post-fermentation is essential to arrest over-acidification and extend shelf life.
- ✅ **Set-type yoghurt** ferments and packs in the same container; **stirred-type yoghurt** ferments in bulk, then is broken, fruit-flavored, and packed.
- ✅ The **0.09 constant** in titration = milli-equivalent weight of lactic acid (90/1000).
- ✅ Theoretical maximum lactic acid from 48 g/L lactose ≈ **2.53 %**; practical set-Dahi acidity is **0.5 – 1.0 %** because fermentation self-limits at low pH.
- ✅ The **sour taste** in Dahi is due to the **H⁺ ions (H₃O⁺)** released by lactic acid, not the undissociated molecule itself.
